feynman.0: When you go fast enough — really close to the speed of light — the old familiar rules for time, length and energy quietly stop telling the truth. Here’s how it goes: we’ll state Einstein’s two simple postulates, see what they force on us through the Lorentz factor, and end up at the most surprising bargain in all of physics, where mass and energy turn out to be the very same thing: E0 = mc².
feynman.1: Classical mechanics quietly takes a few things for granted. It assumes time ticks at the same rate for everybody, that a metre stick is a metre stick for everybody, that velocities just add up the way you’d add apples, and that the kinetic energy ½mv² can climb as high as you please. At the speeds you and I live at, these are perfectly good assumptions — experiment backs them up. But as you push toward c = 3.00 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹, all four of them break down. Look at what happens in an accelerator: you can keep pouring energy into an electron forever, and here’s the funny thing — its speed never quite gets to c.
feynman.2: Light travels at c, as we saw back in topic 6 — but classical physics couldn’t answer a simple-sounding question: at c relative to what? If you just add velocities the ordinary way, then someone rushing toward a lamp ought to clock the light coming in faster than c. So the nineteenth century invented the ether, an invisible something filling all of space, through which light was supposed to travel at c. And if that were so, then the Earth plowing through it on its orbit should feel an “ether wind”, and the measured speed of light ought to come out different depending on which way you looked.
feynman.3: So in 1887 Michelson and Morley did a beautiful thing. They took one beam of light, split it down two arms of an interferometer set at right angles, bounced the pieces back, and let them meet again to make an interference pattern. Now, if there really were an ether wind, light running along the wind would take a different time than light running across it — so when you turned the whole apparatus around, the fringes ought to shift by an amount they could predict and measure. And what did they find? Nothing. The shift was zero — every direction, every hour of the day, every season of the year. That’s the famous null result.
feynman.4: What that nothing tells you is something: the speed of light comes out the same in every direction, no matter how the Earth is moving, and there isn’t a shred of evidence for the ether. Nature simply doesn’t play the game the old way. And it points straight at the heart of special relativity — that c is the same for every observer — which is exactly the thing Einstein took as a starting point, a postulate, in 1905.
feynman.5: Key idea Michelson and Morley figured an ether wind would shift the fringes when they spun their interferometer around. They looked, and there was no shift — not under any conditions they could try. And that great big nothing supports the postulate that the speed of light is the same for everybody, no matter how the source moves or how you move.
feynman.6: Look — a frame of reference is just the set of coordinates you measure positions and times and motions from. It’s where you’re standing to watch the show. Now an inertial frame of reference is one that doesn’t accelerate; it coasts along at constant velocity. And here’s a thing people trip over: constant speed isn’t enough. A car rounding a bend at a steady speed is still changing its direction, and that’s an acceleration, so its frame is non-inertial. Special relativity only talks about inertial frames — that’s the game we’re playing here.
feynman.7: The whole theory stands on just two postulates — two simple statements:
feynman.8: Postulate 1. The laws of physics are the same in every inertial frame of reference. That means no experiment you do, sealed up inside your own coasting frame, can ever tell you your absolute motion. Nature simply refuses to answer that question.
feynman.9: Postulate 2. The speed of light in a vacuum, c, comes out to the very same value for every observer in an inertial frame — and it doesn’t matter one bit how the light source is moving, or how you yourself are moving.
feynman.10: Now here’s the funny thing — the second postulate is where we part ways with classical physics. You’d think it had to be wrong. If a train rolling along at 0.50c switches on its headlights, common sense says somebody by the track should clock that light at 1.50c. But nature doesn’t care what you think: the postulate says the driver and the fellow by the track each measure exactly c, no more, no less. And the only way two people moving relative to each other can agree on the speed of one and the same light beam is if they disagree about distances and about time intervals. So out goes absolute time, and out goes absolute length. That’s the price, and it’s a wonderful price to pay.
feynman.11: Trap “Inertial” means constant velocity, not just constant speed — don’t mix those up. Take an electron whizzing around the storage ring of the Australian Synchrotron at a perfectly constant speed: it’s being yanked toward the centre the whole time, always accelerating, so its frame is non-inertial. The familiar time-dilation and length-contraction formulas are only good in inertial frames.
feynman.12: Look — an event is just something that happens, at one little spot in space and at one moment in time. A muon gets made; a clock goes tick. That’s an event. Now here’s the funny thing: two observers drifting past each other will not agree on how much time sat between two events, and they will not agree on how long an object is. They’re both honest, both careful — and they disagree. To make sense of the disagreement, each comparison gets pinned down by one special, privileged measurement we call the proper quantity.
feynman.13: Proper time t0 is the time between two events as measured by the one observer for whom both events happen at the very same place. Think of it this way: both beats of a traveller’s heart happen right there at the traveller’s chest — nowhere else — so the traveller is the one who reads off proper time. Everybody else sees that interval stretched out, dilated, longer. And proper length L0 is the length of a thing measured by whoever is sitting still right alongside it. Set yourself moving past the object and you measure it shorter — contracted — and only squashed along the direction you’re moving, not sideways.
feynman.14: So every relativity problem starts with two questions, and you ask them before you reach for a single formula. First: in whose frame do both events happen at the same spot? That fellow reads t0. Second: in whose frame is the object — or the stretch of distance being crossed — sitting still? That fellow reads L0. Answer those two, and you know who owns the proper quantities. How much the time stretches and the length shrinks is fixed by one number, the Lorentz factor:
feynman.15: Now since v < c for anything with mass — you simply can’t get a hunk of matter up to c — γ is never less than 1. At rest, γ = 1. Even at 0.10c it’s a measly 1.005, which is why nobody noticed any of this for centuries; the classical rules are plenty good at everyday speeds. But watch it climb: at 0.50c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.25) = 1.15; at 0.90c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.81) = 2.29; at 0.99c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.9801) = 7.09. Push v → c and that denominator dives toward zero, so γ shoots up without any bound at all, the way Figure 11.1 shows you.
feynman.16: Trap Don’t go calling these things illusions. Both observers are dead right, each in his own frame; a contracted length and a dilated time are honest-to-goodness measurements, not tricks of the eye. And here’s the part people trip over: proper time does not automatically belong to whoever is standing still, and proper time and proper length usually belong to two different observers in the very same problem. Take a muon: it’s born and it dies right there at the muon, so t0 is measured in the muon’s frame of reference. But the atmosphere it’s falling through is sitting still relative to Earth, so L0 is measured in the Earth frame. First you find the events and you find the object — only then do you hand out γ.
feynman.17: Key idea You name the proper quantity first, before any formula touches the page: t0 goes to the frame where both events land at the same spot, and L0 goes to the frame where the object sits still. Then t = γt0 stretches the time and L = L0/γ shrinks the length — both by the very same factor γ ≥ 1.
feynman.18: Now here’s the wonderful part. We don’t have to take any of this on faith. Three real systems put these predictions to the test, right out in the world, and we’ll work through each one with the same two proper-quantity questions we just asked.
feynman.19: Look — up high, where the cosmic rays slam into the top of the atmosphere, they make little particles called muons, about 5.0 km above a detector sitting at sea level. The muons come barrelling straight down at 0.995c. Now a muon sitting still doesn’t last long: on average it lives just 2.2 μs before it falls apart. So picture each one flying in a straight line at steady speed until, all of a sudden, it decays.
feynman.20: You do the bookkeeping the old classical way and you get a puzzle. In one lifetime the muon goes 0.995 × 3.00 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹ × 2.2 × 10⁻⁶ s = 6.6 × 10² m — only about an eighth of the way down. So almost none of them ought to make it. And yet the detectors at sea level are practically swimming in muons! Something is wrong with the simple picture. Now at 0.995c we have γ = 1/√(1 − 0.995²) = 10.0, and here’s the funny thing: each frame tells the story its own way, and both come out right.
feynman.21: In the Earth frame: the muon is born and the muon dies, both happening right at the muon, which is rushing past us. So the 2.2 μs lifetime is the proper time — that’s the muon’s own clock. From where we stand, that clock runs slow, and the stretched-out lifetime is t = γt0 = 10.0 × 2.2 μs = 22 μs. In 22 μs the muon covers 2.985 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹ × 2.2 × 10⁻⁵ s = 6.6 km, which is more than the 5.0 km of air there is to fall through. So down it comes. The muon arrives.
feynman.22: In the muon’s frame: now the muon says, “I’m not moving at all — it’s the whole sky rushing up past me at 0.995c.” Its 2.2 μs lifetime is the proper time. The atmosphere sits still with respect to Earth, so that 5.0 km is a proper length in the Earth frame; but to the muon it’s squashed down to L = L0/γ = 5.0 × 10³ ÷ 10.0 = 5.0 × 10² m, which it crosses in 1.7 μs — less than a single lifetime. So the muon arrives.
feynman.23: The two frames argue about the times and they argue about the distances — but you might think they’d argue about the muon too, and nature doesn’t care what you think. They agree on the one thing you can actually see: the muon arrives. What one frame calls slow time, the other calls short distance — they’re two ways of telling the same true story, as shown in Figure 11.2.
feynman.24: Take a linear accelerator, 3.2 km of it, sitting still in the laboratory — so the lab measures its proper length. Now the electrons go shooting down it at 0.998c (γ = 15.8), and to them the whole beamline is scrunched up to L = 3.2 × 10³ ÷ 15.8 = 2.0 × 10² m. And here’s the practical kicker: the people who build the accelerating structures have to design them for particles that genuinely live in this shorter length.
feynman.25: Now consider the GPS in your pocket. It figures out where you are from how long the timing signals take to arrive from the satellites — so those satellite clocks have got to agree with the clocks on the ground to a fantastic precision. Each satellite swings around at roughly 3.9 × 10³ m s⁻¹, which gives γ = 1 + 8.5 × 10⁻¹¹. A tiny number! But let it run for one whole day (86 400 s) and the orbiting clock — which is keeping proper time up there on the satellite — slips behind the ground clocks by about 7.3 μs. And in 7.3 μs light travels 2.2 km, so if you didn’t fix it, your position would wander off by kilometres every single day. That’s why they correct the GPS clocks for their orbital speed.
feynman.26: Connects These satellites we’re fixing are the very same circular orbits from topic 2. Look how it all hangs together: the orbital radius fixes the speed v, and the speed v fixes γ — so the relativistic correction is handed to us by the same orbital mechanics that put the satellite up there in the first place.
feynman.27: And now the punchline of the whole business. It's about energy. Look — an object just sitting there, doing nothing, is not empty. Its mass is energy, a bottled-up store of the stuff, and we call it the rest energy:
feynman.28: Set the thing moving, and now you've got two kinds of energy to add up — the rest energy it had all along, plus the kinetic energy of the motion:
feynman.29: Now here's the nice thing. Crawl along slowly and (γ − 1)mc² turns out to be just ½mv² — which is exactly why the old classical formula works so beautifully in everyday life. But push up close to c and the two part ways. Take an electron at 0.99c (γ = 7.09). The classical recipe says ½mv² = ½ × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × (2.97 × 10⁸)² = 4.0 × 10⁻¹⁴ J, while the real answer is Ek = (7.09 − 1) × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 9.00 × 10¹⁶ = 5.0 × 10⁻¹³ J — more than twelve times bigger. And as v → c, γ → ∞: the energy you'd have to pour in runs off to infinity. So nature simply won't let anything with mass ever quite reach the speed of light, no matter how hard you shove.
feynman.30: Take the Sun. It pours out 3.8 × 10²⁶ W from the fusion fire down in its core. Hydrogen nuclei jam together into helium, and — here's the funny thing — the helium that comes out weighs a little less than the stuff that went in. That missing mass doesn't vanish; it leaves as light. So let's figure out how much mass goes out the door each second.
feynman.31: The rule: every second the Sun throws away E = 3.8 × 10²⁶ J of energy, so we just flip E0 = mc² around to read m = E0/c².
feynman.32: Now put in the numbers: m = 3.8 × 10²⁶ ÷ (3.00 × 10⁸)² = 3.8 × 10²⁶ ÷ 9.00 × 10¹⁶
feynman.33: And out comes m = 4.2 × 10⁹ kg every single second — somewhere around four million tonnes of the Sun turned into sunshine, second after second. Sounds ruinous, doesn't it? But the Sun is so enormous that over its whole life this burns up well under 0.1% of its mass. It barely notices.
feynman.34: Sometimes the conversion goes the whole way — every last scrap of mass into energy — and that's positron–electron annihilation. The positron is the electron's antiparticle, same mass, opposite everything else. Let the two drift together at a dawdle and they wipe each other out completely, the whole rest energy flying off as two gamma-ray photons: 2mec² = 2 × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 9.00 × 10¹⁶ = 1.64 × 10⁻¹³ J all told, which works out to 8.2 × 10⁻¹⁴ J ≈ 0.51 MeV apiece. The two photons shoot off in exactly opposite directions, because there was hardly any momentum to begin with and it has to balance. And we put this to work: PET scanners in hospitals hunt down tumours by catching these back-to-back pairs.
feynman.35: Particle accelerators play the same game backwards: smash things together at enormous γ and the kinetic energy congeals into the rest energy of brand-new matter — particles heavier than the ones you started with. It's the same bookkeeping running both ways, with E0 = mc² keeping the accounts honest in either direction.
feynman.36: Trap Don't get fooled: γmc² is the total energy, not the kinetic energy. The kinetic part is only what sits above the rest energy: Ek = (γ − 1)mc². And don't reach for the old ½mv² anywhere near c — at 0.99c it lowballs the kinetic energy by a factor of about twelve.
feynman.37: Key idea Mass and energy are two faces of the same coin, swapped at the fixed exchange rate E0 = mc². The Sun turns mass into radiation, annihilation turns mass clean into a pair of photons, and accelerators turn kinetic energy into the mass of fresh particles.
feynman.38: Connects Remember the radius formula r = mv/qB we used for the synchrotron in topic 5? It came with the small print: v << c. Well, the electrons of the Australian Synchrotron flatly ignore that condition — on purpose — since their energy runs thousands of times their rest energy. So the magnets of that machine have to be built for the relativistic energies on this page, not for the cosy classical formula.

sagan.0: As we push outward toward the speed of light, the comfortable rules that governed time, length and energy for all of human history begin to fail us, returning answers that simply are not true. Here we listen to Einstein’s two postulates, follow their consequences through the Lorentz factor, and arrive at one of the most profound discoveries of our species — that mass and energy are one and the same: E0 = mc².
sagan.1: Classical mechanics carried within it a set of quiet certainties: that time flows at one rate for every observer in the cosmos, that a length is a length for all who measure it, that velocities simply add, and that the kinetic energy ½mv² can climb forever without limit. At the gentle speeds of ordinary life these assumptions match the world beautifully. But as motion approaches c = 3.00 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹, every one of them quietly collapses: an accelerator can pour energy into an electron without end, and still the electron’s speed never quite reaches c.
sagan.2: Light travels at c, as we found in topic 6 — but relative to what? Classical physics could not say. The old rule for adding velocities insisted that an observer rushing toward a source of light ought to measure something greater than c. So the nineteenth century imagined the ether, a vast invisible medium filling all of space, through which light was supposed to glide at c; and as Earth swept along its orbit through this cosmic sea, there should arise an “ether wind”, making the measured speed of light depend upon the direction in which one looked.
sagan.3: In 1887 Michelson and Morley took a single beam of light, split it down two perpendicular arms of an interferometer, and let the reflections fold back together into a delicate pattern of interference. If an ether wind truly blew, then light running with the wind would take a different time from light crossing it, and slowly turning the apparatus should make the fringes shift by a definite, measurable amount. They watched, and they turned it, through every orientation, every hour of the day, every season of the year — and the shift was zero. Nothing. The famous null result.
sagan.4: That silence speaks: the measured speed of light is the same in every direction, indifferent to Earth’s headlong motion through space, and the ether for which they searched left no trace at all. In its place stands the central and astonishing claim of special relativity — that c is the same for every observer in the universe — which Einstein, with the courage to abandon a comforting idea when the evidence demanded it, took up in 1905 as a postulate.
sagan.5: Key idea Michelson and Morley predicted that an ether wind would shift the fringes as their interferometer turned; they looked, under every condition they could contrive, and saw no shift whatever; that null result upholds the postulate that the speed of light is the same for all observers, indifferent alike to the motion of the source and of the one who watches.
sagan.6: Before we can speak of light and time, we need a place to stand. A frame of reference is that place: the set of coordinates from which we measure where things are, when they happen and how they move. An inertial frame of reference is one that does not accelerate; it drifts, serenely, at constant velocity. Constant speed alone will not do, for a car rounding a bend at a steady pace is forever turning, forever changing direction, and so its frame is non-inertial. It is to these untroubled, unaccelerating frames, and these alone, that special relativity speaks.
sagan.7: Upon two simple statements, two postulates, the whole edifice is raised:
sagan.8: Postulate 1. The laws of physics are the same in all inertial frames of reference. There is no privileged vantage point, no cosmic throne; no experiment performed within an inertial frame can reveal that frame's absolute motion, for there is none to find.
sagan.9: Postulate 2. The speed of light in a vacuum, c, has the same value for every observer in every inertial frame, no matter how the source of the light may be racing, no matter how the observer may be moving to meet it or to flee.
sagan.10: Here, in the second postulate, is where we are asked to abandon a comforting and ancient intuition. By the old reckoning, if a train rushing along at 0.50c should switch on its headlights, an observer beside the track would surely measure that light at 1.50c; common sense demands it. But nature, it turns out, does not consult our common sense: the driver and the watcher by the track each find exactly c, no more and no less. Two observers in relative motion can agree on the speed of one and the same beam of light only at a price, and the price is this, that they must disagree about distances and about intervals of time. And so absolute time and absolute length, which had seemed the very bedrock of the world, are quietly let go.
sagan.11: Trap “Inertial” means constant velocity, not merely constant speed, and the difference is everything. An electron circling the storage ring of the Australian Synchrotron may hold its speed perfectly steady, yet it is forever falling inward, accelerating toward the centre with every instant, so its frame is non-inertial. The familiar formulas of time dilation and length contraction belong to the inertial frames alone.
sagan.12: An event is something that happens at one point in space at one moment in time — the birth of a muon in the high cold air, a single tick of a clock somewhere in the universe. Two inertial observers drifting past one another will not agree on the time that elapses between two such events, nor on the length of a thing held between them. And yet each comparison has its anchor, a single privileged measurement that nature singles out, called the proper quantity.
sagan.13: Proper time t0 is the interval between two events as measured in the one frame of reference where both events happen at the same place. Both beats of a traveller’s heart fall at the traveller’s own chest, and so it is the traveller who reads the proper time; every other observer in the cosmos, watching from elsewhere, reads a longer, a dilated, time. In the same way proper length L0 is the length of an object measured in the frame in which that object rests quietly at home. Anyone moving past it measures something shorter, a contracted length — and the shrinking happens only along the direction of the motion, nowhere else.
sagan.14: And so every problem in relativity opens with two simple questions, asked humbly before a single formula is written down: in which frame do the two events occur at the same point in space — for that observer holds t0 — and in which frame does the object, or the stretch of distance to be crossed, lie at rest, so that that observer holds L0? How much time is stretched, how much length is gathered in, is decided by a single number, the Lorentz factor:
sagan.15: Because v < c for anything that carries mass, γ is never smaller than 1. At rest γ = 1, and even at 0.10c it has crept up only to 1.005 — which is why, in the slow gentle world of our daily lives, the old mechanics serves us faithfully. But press onward. At 0.50c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.25) = 1.15; at 0.90c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.81) = 2.29; at 0.99c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.9801) = 7.09. As v → c the denominator falls away toward zero and γ climbs without bound, soaring beyond every limit, as Figure 11.1 reveals.
sagan.16: Trap Each observer’s measurements are true within their own frame; a contracted length, a dilated time — these are honest readings of the world, not tricks of the eye. The proper time does not belong, by some comfortable habit of thought, to whoever happens to be standing still; and proper time and proper length most often belong to different observers in the very same problem. A muon is born and a muon dies, both at the muon itself, so t0 is read in the muon’s frame of reference; yet the atmosphere through which it plunges rests with the Earth, so L0 is read in the Earth frame. First find where the events happen and where the object lives; only then, with that settled, is γ assigned.
sagan.17: Key idea The proper quantity is named before any formula is set down: t0 belongs to the frame in which both events fall at the same point in space, and L0 belongs to the frame in which the object lies at rest. Then time is drawn out by t = γt0 and length is gathered in by L = L0/γ, both by the one same factor γ ≥ 1.
sagan.18: Three real systems, scattered across the sky and the laboratory and the orbits high above us, carry these predictions out of the realm of thought and into the world of measurement; each one yields to the same two questions about the proper quantities we asked above.
sagan.19: Far overhead, cosmic rays arriving from the depths of space strike the upper atmosphere and, in that collision, conjure muons into being some 5.0 km above a sea-level detector, plunging downward at 0.995c. Left to itself, at rest, a muon endures on average only 2.2 μs before it decays. We picture each one falling in a straight line, at constant speed, living out its brief allotted span until it vanishes.
sagan.20: Reckon it the classical way, and the muon travels only 0.995 × 3.00 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹ × 2.2 × 10⁻⁶ s = 6.6 × 10² m in a single lifetime, barely an eighth of the journey, so by all the old rules almost none should ever complete the descent to greet us. And yet the detectors at sea level record them in great abundance, raining down upon us by the multitude. The resolution lies in the number itself: at 0.995c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.995²) = 10.0, and each frame, in its own honest language, tells the same story.
sagan.21: In the Earth frame: the muon is born and the muon dies, both events at the muon itself, which races relative to the Earth, so the 2.2 μs lifetime is the proper time. Stretched by motion, that lifetime becomes t = γt0 = 10.0 × 2.2 μs = 22 μs. And in those 22 μs the muon covers 2.985 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹ × 2.2 × 10⁻⁵ s = 6.6 km, more than the 5.0 km of atmosphere standing between it and us. The muon arrives.
sagan.22: In the muon’s frame: the muon rests, serene and unhurried, its 2.2 μs lifetime is the proper time, and it is the whole atmosphere that comes rushing upward past it at 0.995c. That atmosphere is at rest relative to the Earth, so 5.0 km is a proper length in the Earth frame; but seen from the muon it is squeezed to L = L0/γ = 5.0 × 10³ ÷ 10.0 = 5.0 × 10² m, a span crossed in a mere 1.7 μs, less than a single lifetime. The muon arrives.
sagan.23: The two frames quarrel over times and over distances, yet they bow together before the one fact the universe will let us observe: the muon arrives. What one frame calls the dilation of time, the other calls the contraction of length; they are two faces of a single truth, as shown in Figure 11.2.
sagan.24: A linear accelerator 3.2 km long rests in the laboratory, so it is the laboratory that measures its proper length. But to the electrons streaming along it at 0.998c (γ = 15.8), that beamline shrinks before them to L = 3.2 × 10³ ÷ 15.8 = 2.0 × 10² m, and so the accelerating structures, fashioned by human hands, must be engineered to serve particles that genuinely experience this shorter world.
sagan.25: A GPS receiver finds its place on the Earth by reading the travel times of signals broadcast from satellites overhead, which means those satellite clocks must keep faith with the clocks on the ground to a precision almost beyond imagining. Each satellite sweeps along its orbit at roughly 3.9 × 10³ m s⁻¹, giving γ = 1 + 8.5 × 10⁻¹¹. A vanishingly small number, and yet over the course of a single day (86 400 s) the orbiting clock, faithfully recording its own proper time aloft, falls behind the ground clocks by about 7.3 μs. In 7.3 μs light journeys 2.2 km, so a system left uncorrected would wander off by kilometres each and every day; and so we correct the GPS clocks for their orbital speed, humbled into agreement with Einstein.
sagan.26: Connects The very satellites we correct here are the same circular orbits of topic 2: the orbital radius decrees the speed v, and v in turn decrees γ, so the relativistic correction is dictated by the same orbital mechanics that lifted the satellite into the heavens in the first place.
sagan.27: And so we arrive at the last and perhaps the most astonishing consequence of all, which concerns energy itself. A motionless object is not, as we once imagined, an empty and inert thing waiting to be set in motion: locked within its very mass is a vast reservoir of energy, the rest energy, present even when nothing at all appears to be happening:
sagan.28: Set the object in motion, and its total energy becomes the sum of two parts: that rest energy it carried all along, and the kinetic energy of its motion:
sagan.29: At the gentle speeds of ordinary life, (γ − 1)mc² agrees faithfully with the familiar ½mv², and this is precisely why the old formula served us so well for so long. But approach the speed of light c, and the two part company dramatically. Consider an electron racing at 0.99c (γ = 7.09): the classical formula offers ½mv² = ½ × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × (2.97 × 10⁸)² = 4.0 × 10⁻¹⁴ J, while the true relativistic value is Ek = (7.09 − 1) × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 9.00 × 10¹⁶ = 5.0 × 10⁻¹³ J, more than twelve times greater. And as v → c, γ → ∞: the energy demanded climbs without any limit whatsoever, and so the universe forbids any object with mass from ever reaching the speed of light. It is a barrier woven into the fabric of nature itself.
sagan.30: Look up at our nearest star. The Sun pours 3.8 × 10²⁶ W into the darkness, drawn from the nuclear fusion burning in its core. There, hydrogen nuclei fuse into helium, and the helium that emerges weighs slightly less than the hydrogen that went in. That missing mass has not vanished; it has become the sunlight that warms our world. Modelling the radiated energy as converted mass, we can calculate just how much of the Sun disappears into light each second.
sagan.31: Formula: in a single second the Sun radiates E = 3.8 × 10²⁶ J, and so E0 = mc² rearranges to tell us the mass behind that light, m = E0/c².
sagan.33: Answer: m = 4.2 × 10⁹ kg vanishes every second, some four million tonnes of the Sun's substance dissolved into the light by which we see and live. And yet, for all its prodigious extravagance, this consumes well under 0.1% of the Sun's mass across the whole long span of its lifetime.
sagan.34: There are encounters in which the conversion of mass into energy is not partial but absolute, total, complete: this is positron–electron annihilation. The positron is the antiparticle of the electron, its mirror twin, sharing the very same mass. When the two drift together at negligible speed, both cease to exist, and the whole of their rest energy departs as two gamma-ray photons: 2mec² = 2 × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 9.00 × 10¹⁶ = 1.64 × 10⁻¹³ J altogether, which is 8.2 × 10⁻¹⁴ J ≈ 0.51 MeV carried by each photon. They flee in exactly opposite directions, for the total momentum before the encounter was very nearly zero. It is upon this principle that PET medical scanners find tumours hidden within the body, listening for these back-to-back pairs of light.
sagan.35: Our particle accelerators run this same exchange in the opposite direction: in collisions at enormous γ, the raw kinetic energy of motion is woven into the rest energy of new matter, conjuring particles heavier than any that collided. These are nuclear transformations of the deepest kind, governed in both directions by the single equation E0 = mc².
sagan.36: Trap Take care: γmc² is the total energy, not the kinetic energy alone. The kinetic energy is only that portion rising above the rest energy: Ek = (γ − 1)mc². And the comfortable old ½mv² is no honest approximation as one nears c; at 0.99c it falls short of the true kinetic energy by a factor of about twelve. When the evidence demands it, the classical formula must be set aside.
sagan.37: Key idea Mass and energy are two faces of one thing, freely interchangeable at the unchanging rate E0 = mc². The Sun turns mass into radiance, annihilation turns mass wholly into pairs of photons, and accelerators turn the energy of motion into the mass of particles that never were before.
sagan.38: Connects The radius formula r = mv/qB that served the synchrotron in topic 5 carries with it the quiet condition v << c. The electrons of the Australian Synchrotron, by deliberate design, honour no such condition, for their energy runs to thousands of times their rest energy. And so the magnetic fields of that great machine must be engineered for the relativistic energies of this very page, not for the gentle classical formula that breaks down long before such speeds.

tyson.0: Push toward the speed of light and watch what happens — the classical rules for time, length and energy fall apart, handing you the wrong answers. Here’s the plan: we’ll state Einstein’s two postulates, pin down their consequences with the Lorentz factor, and land on the showstopper — the deep equivalence of mass and energy: E0 = mc².
tyson.1: Classical mechanics makes some cozy assumptions: that time ticks at the same rate for everybody, that a ruler is a ruler for everybody, that velocities just add up, and that the kinetic energy ½mv² can climb forever. At everyday speeds? Spot on — experiment agrees. But here’s the thing — crank the speed up toward c = 3.00 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹ and all four assumptions break. An accelerator can pour energy into an electron forever, and that electron still never quite reaches c. Ever.
tyson.2: Light travels at c — we nailed that down in topic 6 — but classical physics couldn’t answer the obvious follow-up: at c relative to what? Run the classical math on velocity addition and an observer racing toward a light source should clock the light at something faster than c. So nineteenth-century physics invented a fix: the ether, a hypothetical medium that light supposedly rides through at c. And if that’s true, Earth plowing through it on its orbit should kick up an “ether wind” — and the measured speed of light should depend on which way you look.
tyson.3: So in 1887, Michelson and Morley put it to the test. They split one beam of light down two perpendicular arms of an interferometer, bounced it back, and recombined it into an interference pattern. The logic was airtight: an ether wind would make light going along the wind take a different time than light going across it — so spin the apparatus and the fringes should shift by a predicted, measurable amount. Now watch — they looked at every orientation, every time of day, every season. The shift? Zero. Nothing. The null result.
tyson.4: And that zero says everything. The measured speed of light is the same in every direction, no matter how Earth is moving — and there’s not a shred of evidence for the ether. What it does support is the bombshell at the heart of special relativity: c is the same for every observer, full stop. Einstein took that and ran with it in 1905, planting it as a postulate.
tyson.5: Key idea Michelson and Morley predicted an ether wind would smear the fringes when their interferometer rotated; they spun it and saw no shift — under any conditions. That null result backs the postulate that the speed of light is the same for all observers, no matter how the source or the observer is moving.
tyson.6: Here's where we start. A frame of reference is just the set of coordinates you measure positions, times and motions from — your point of view, made rigorous. An inertial frame of reference is the special kind that doesn’t accelerate; it cruises along at constant velocity. And watch — constant speed alone won’t cut it: a car rounding a bend at a steady speed is still changing direction, so its frame is non-inertial. Special relativity? It plays only on inertial frames.
tyson.7: The whole theory stands on two postulates — just two:
tyson.8: Postulate 1. The laws of physics are the same in every inertial frame of reference. Think about that — no experiment you run inside an inertial frame can tell you the frame’s absolute motion. None. There’s no cosmic speedometer.
tyson.9: Postulate 2. The speed of light in a vacuum, c, comes out to the same value for every observer in an inertial frame — and it does not care how fast the light source is moving, or how fast you are moving.
tyson.10: Now here’s the postulate that breaks classical physics wide open. Classically, if a train barreling along at 0.50c flicks on its headlights, a trackside observer should clock that light at 1.50c — obvious, right? Wrong. The postulate insists the driver and the trackside observer each measure exactly c. Same beam. Same speed. And the only way two observers in relative motion can agree on that is if they disagree about distances and time intervals. So absolute time and absolute length — gone. Abandoned.
tyson.11: Trap “Inertial” means constant velocity — not constant speed. Don’t fall for it. An electron whipping around the Australian Synchrotron storage ring at constant speed is accelerating continuously toward the centre the whole time, so its frame is non-inertial. The standard time-dilation and length-contraction formulas live in inertial frames only.
tyson.12: Here's the thing about an event — it's just something that happens, at one point in space, at one moment in time. A muon springs into existence. A clock ticks once. Simple. Now watch what happens when two inertial observers go zipping past each other: they can't agree on the time between two events, and they can't even agree on how long an object is. So how do you ever pin anything down? You anchor it. Each comparison rests on one privileged measurement — the proper quantity.
tyson.13: Proper time t0 is the time interval between two events, measured in the one frame where both events happen at the same point in space. Picture a traveller — two beats of their heart both happen right there at their chest. So the traveller measures proper time. Everybody else? They clock a longer interval. Time, dilated. And proper length L0 is the length of an object measured in the frame where the object sits at rest. Go flying past it and you'll measure something shorter, contracted — and only along the direction you're moving. Sideways, nothing changes.
tyson.14: Every relativity problem opens with two questions — ask them before you reach for a single formula. One: in which frame do the two events happen at the same point in space? That observer measures t0. Two: in which frame is the object, or the distance being crossed, at rest? That observer measures L0. Get those right and the rest follows. Just how much does time stretch and length shrink? That's dialled in by one number — the Lorentz factor:
tyson.15: Here's the kicker — since v < c for anything with mass, γ never dips below 1. At rest, γ = 1. Even at a blistering 0.10c it's a measly 1.005 — which is exactly why classical mechanics gets away with it at everyday speeds. But now watch it wake up. At 0.50c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.25) = 1.15; at 0.90c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.81) = 2.29; at 0.99c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.9801) = 7.09. As v → c that denominator races toward zero and γ blows up without bound — straight off the chart, as shown in Figure 11.1.
tyson.16: Trap Don't fall for it — both observers are right, each in their own frame. A contracted length and a dilated time aren't tricks of the eye; they're real measurements. And here's where people stumble: proper time does not automatically belong to the observer standing still, and proper time and proper length usually belong to two different observers in the very same problem. Take the muon — its creation and its decay both happen right at the muon, so t0 lives in the muon's frame of reference. But the atmosphere it plunges through sits at rest relative to Earth, so L0 lives in the Earth frame. Locate the events and the object first. Only then do you hand out γ.
tyson.17: Key idea Nail down the proper quantity before you touch a single formula: t0 belongs to the frame where both events happen at the same point in space, and L0 belongs to the frame where the object is at rest. Then — and only then — t = γt0 stretches time and L = L0/γ shrinks length, both by the very same factor γ ≥ 1.
tyson.18: Enough theory — let’s watch it happen. Three real systems put these predictions to the test, and each one cracks open with the same two proper-quantity questions we just laid out.
tyson.19: Here’s the setup. Cosmic rays slam into the upper atmosphere and manufacture muons roughly 5.0 km above a sea-level detector, screaming downward at 0.995c. Sitting still, a muon lives an average of 2.2 μs — that’s it. Model each one as a straight-line sprinter at constant speed until it pops out of existence.
tyson.20: Run the classical numbers and watch the trouble start. In one lifetime the muon covers 0.995 × 3.00 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹ × 2.2 × 10⁻⁶ s = 6.6 × 10² m — about an eighth of the way down. So almost none of them should ever reach the detector. And yet — the detectors at sea level catch muons by the bucketload. So what gives? At 0.995c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.995²) = 10.0, and each frame settles the mystery its own way.
tyson.21: In the Earth frame: the muon is born and dies right at the muon, which is the thing moving relative to Earth — so that 2.2 μs lifetime is the proper time. Stretch it: t = γt0 = 10.0 × 2.2 μs = 22 μs. Now in 22 μs the muon covers 2.985 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹ × 2.2 × 10⁻⁵ s = 6.6 km — more than the 5.0 km of atmosphere standing in its way. The muon makes it.
tyson.22: In the muon’s frame: now the muon sits still, its 2.2 μs lifetime is the proper time, and the whole atmosphere comes rushing up past it at 0.995c. That atmosphere is at rest relative to Earth, so the 5.0 km is a proper length in the Earth frame — and in the muon’s frame it gets squeezed to L = L0/γ = 5.0 × 10³ ÷ 10.0 = 5.0 × 10² m, crossed in just 1.7 μs — less than a single lifetime. The muon makes it.
tyson.23: Two frames, two completely different stories about time and distance — and yet they shake hands on the one thing you can actually see: the muon arrives. One frame’s time dilation is the other frame’s length contraction, the very same fact wearing two costumes, as shown in Figure 11.2.
tyson.24: Now take a 3.2 km linear accelerator bolted to the lab floor — the lab is the one measuring its proper length. But the electrons ripping down it at 0.998c (γ = 15.8) don’t see 3.2 km at all; to them the beamline is crushed down to L = 3.2 × 10³ ÷ 15.8 = 2.0 × 10² m. And here’s the kicker — the engineers have to design the accelerating structures for particles that experience that shorter length.
tyson.25: Now the one that’s in your pocket. A GPS receiver pins down where you are from the travel times of timing signals beamed from satellites — which means those satellite clocks have to agree with ground clocks to ridiculous precision. Each satellite orbits at about 3.9 × 10³ m s⁻¹, giving γ = 1 + 8.5 × 10⁻¹¹. Tiny — almost nothing. But run it over one day (86 400 s) and the orbiting clock, ticking proper time up there aboard the satellite, slips behind the ground clocks by about 7.3 μs. In 7.3 μs light covers 2.2 km — so leave it uncorrected and your position drifts by kilometres every single day. That’s why GPS clocks are corrected for their orbital speed.
tyson.26: Connects Those satellites we just corrected? They’re the very same circular orbits from topic 2. The orbital radius locks in the speed v, and v locks in γ — so the relativistic correction is dictated by the exact same orbital mechanics that flung the satellite into orbit in the first place.
tyson.27: Here’s where relativity drops its biggest surprise — energy. A stationary object sitting there doing nothing? Not empty. Not even close. Its mass is itself a store of energy, the rest energy:
tyson.28: Now set that object in motion, and its total energy becomes the rest energy plus the kinetic energy — the two of them, added together:
tyson.29: At everyday speeds, (γ − 1)mc² lines up neatly with ½mv² — which is exactly why the classical formula works so beautifully when you’re strolling down the street. But push up near c, and the two part ways. Hard. Take an electron at 0.99c (γ = 7.09): the classical formula hands you ½mv² = ½ × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × (2.97 × 10⁸)² = 4.0 × 10⁻¹⁴ J, while the real answer is Ek = (7.09 − 1) × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 9.00 × 10¹⁶ = 5.0 × 10⁻¹³ J — more than twelve times bigger. And watch what happens as v → c: γ → ∞. The kinetic energy you’d need blows up without limit. Which means no object with mass — none, ever — can reach the speed of light.
tyson.30: The Sun pours out 3.8 × 10²⁶ W from nuclear fusion deep in its core. Hydrogen nuclei fuse into helium, and here’s the catch — the products weigh slightly less than what went in. That missing mass? It left as radiation. So let’s figure out exactly how much mass the Sun trades away each second.
tyson.31: Formula: every second the Sun radiates E = 3.8 × 10²⁶ J, so we take E0 = mc² and flip it around to m = E0/c².
tyson.33: Answer: m = 4.2 × 10⁹ kg — every single second. That’s about four million tonnes of mass turned into sunlight, gone, each second. And the kicker? Over its entire lifetime, the Sun burns through well under 0.1% of its own mass doing it.
tyson.34: Now for the full reveal — mass turned completely into energy, in positron–electron annihilation. The positron is the electron’s antiparticle, same mass, opposite everything else. Bring the two together at a crawl and they vanish — the entire rest energy comes screaming out as two gamma-ray photons: 2mec² = 2 × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 9.00 × 10¹⁶ = 1.64 × 10⁻¹³ J total, which works out to 8.2 × 10⁻¹⁴ J ≈ 0.51 MeV per photon. They fly off in exactly opposite directions, because the total momentum before annihilation was essentially zero. And we use this — PET medical scanners hunt down tumours by catching these back-to-back pairs.
tyson.35: Particle accelerators run the whole thing in reverse: smash particles together at enormous γ and the kinetic energy becomes the rest energy of brand-new matter — particles heavier than the ones that collided in the first place. Make mass out of motion. It’s all nuclear transformation, governed by E0 = mc² running in both directions.
tyson.36: Trap γmc² is the total energy — not the kinetic energy. The kinetic energy is only the part sitting above the rest energy: Ek = (γ − 1)mc². And don’t reach for the classical ½mv² anywhere near c — it just won’t do; at 0.99c it lowballs the kinetic energy by a factor of about twelve.
tyson.37: Key idea Mass and energy are two sides of the same coin, swappable at one fixed exchange rate: E0 = mc². The Sun turns mass into radiation, annihilation turns mass entirely into photon pairs, and accelerators turn kinetic energy into the mass of new particles.
tyson.38: Connects That tidy radius formula r = mv/qB you used for the synchrotron in topic 5 comes with fine print: v << c. And the electrons of the Australian Synchrotron break that rule on purpose — their energy runs thousands of times their rest energy. So the machine’s magnetic fields have to be engineered for the relativistic energies on this page, not the classical formula.

curie.0: As speeds approach the speed of light, I find the classical rules for time, length and energy give incorrect results. Here I set out Einstein’s two postulates, measure their consequences through the Lorentz factor, and arrive at the equivalence of mass and energy: E0 = mc².
curie.1: Classical mechanics assumes that time passes at the same rate for every observer, that lengths are identical for all observers, that velocities add directly, and that the kinetic energy ½mv² may grow without limit. At everyday speeds I find these assumptions consistent with what is measured. As speeds approach c = 3.00 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹, all four fail: an accelerator can supply energy to an electron without end, yet the electron’s speed never reaches c.
curie.2: Light travels at c, as established in topic 6, but classical physics could not say relative to what. Classical velocity addition implies that an observer moving toward a light source should measure a value greater than c. Nineteenth-century physics proposed the ether, a hypothetical medium through which light propagates at c; Earth’s orbital motion through this medium should produce an “ether wind”, causing the measured speed of light to differ with direction. It was a comfortable assumption, and the only honest test was to measure.
curie.3: In 1887 Michelson and Morley split one light beam along two perpendicular interferometer arms and recombined the reflections into an interference pattern. An ether wind would cause light travelling along the wind to take a different time from light travelling across it, so rotation of the apparatus should shift the fringes by a predicted, measurable amount. The observed shift was zero at every orientation, time of day and season: the null result. Patience and care had been spent, and the instrument returned nothing where it had been promised something.
curie.4: The null result establishes that the measured speed of light is the same in all directions regardless of Earth’s motion, and offers no evidence whatever for the ether. One must surrender the medium because the measurement requires it. The result supports the central claim of special relativity, that c is the same for all observers, which Einstein adopted in 1905 as a postulate.
curie.5: Key idea Michelson and Morley predicted that an ether wind would produce a fringe shift when their interferometer rotated; they observed no shift under any conditions; the null result supports the postulate that the speed of light is the same for all observers, independent of the motion of the source or the observer. In the laboratory one learns to trust only what is measured.
curie.6: A frame of reference is the set of coordinates from which one measures positions, times and motions. An inertial frame of reference is one that does not accelerate; it moves at constant velocity. Constant speed alone will not suffice: a car rounding a bend at steady speed changes its direction, and so its frame is non-inertial. I must be exact on this point, for special relativity applies to inertial frames and to no others.
curie.7: The whole theory rests upon two postulates, which I set down plainly:
curie.8: Postulate 1. The laws of physics are the same in all inertial frames of reference. No experiment one performs within an inertial frame can reveal that frame's absolute motion.
curie.9: Postulate 2. The speed of light in a vacuum, c, has the same value for all observers in inertial frames, whatever the motion of the light source or of the observer.
curie.10: It is the second postulate that breaks with classical physics. By the old reasoning, if a train moving at 0.50c turns on its headlights, an observer at the trackside ought to measure the light at 1.50c; but the postulate insists that the driver and the trackside observer each measure exactly c. Two observers in relative motion can agree on the speed of one and the same beam of light only if they disagree about distances and about intervals of time. The measurement compels it; absolute time and absolute length must therefore be surrendered.
curie.11: Trap “Inertial” means constant velocity, not constant speed. An electron circulating in the Australian Synchrotron storage ring at constant speed is accelerating continuously toward the centre, and so its frame is non-inertial. The standard formulas for time dilation and length contraction hold only in inertial frames.
curie.12: An event is something that happens at one definite point in space and at one definite moment in time, such as the birth of a muon or a single tick of a clock. I have learned that inertial observers in relative motion will not agree on the time interval between two events, nor on the length of an object; the disagreement is not error but a fact of nature. Each comparison must be anchored to one privileged measurement, which I call the proper quantity.
curie.13: Proper time t0 is the time interval between two events measured in the frame of reference in which both events occur at the same point in space. Both beats of a traveller’s heart occur at the traveller’s chest, so it is the traveller who measures proper time; every other observer measures a longer, dilated time. Proper length L0 is the length of an object measured in the frame of reference in which the object is at rest. An observer moving relative to the object measures a shorter, contracted length, and the contraction occurs only along the direction of relative motion, never across it.
curie.14: Before I reach for any formula, I ask two questions of every problem. In which frame do the two events occur at the same point in space (that observer measures t0), and in which frame is the object, or the distance being traversed, at rest (that observer measures L0)? The amount of dilation and of contraction is fixed by a single quantity, the Lorentz factor:
curie.15: Since v < c for any object that carries mass, γ is never less than 1: γ = 1 at rest, and at 0.10c it is still only 1.005, which is why classical mechanics serves us well at everyday speeds. At 0.50c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.25) = 1.15; at 0.90c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.81) = 2.29; at 0.99c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.9801) = 7.09. As v → c the denominator approaches zero and γ grows without bound, as shown in Figure 11.1.
curie.16: Trap Each observer’s measurement is correct in his own frame; a contracted length and a dilated time are measured facts, not illusions to be explained away. Proper time does not belong to the stationary observer by default, and in one problem proper time and proper length usually belong to different observers: a muon’s creation and decay both occur at the muon, so t0 is measured in the muon’s frame of reference, while the atmosphere through which it falls is at rest relative to Earth, so L0 is measured in the Earth frame. I locate the events and the object first; only then do I assign γ.
curie.17: Key idea I identify the proper quantity before any formula is written down: t0 belongs to the frame in which both events occur at the same point in space, and L0 belongs to the frame in which the object is at rest. Then t = γt0 stretches time and L = L0/γ shrinks length, by the one same factor γ ≥ 1.
curie.18: I find that three real systems display these predictions in operation; I analyse each by asking the two proper-quantity questions set out above.
curie.19: Cosmic rays striking the upper atmosphere create muons approximately 5.0 km above a sea-level detector, moving downward at 0.995c. A muon at rest has an average lifetime of 2.2 μs. I model each muon as travelling in a straight line at constant speed until it decays.
curie.20: By the classical reckoning, the muon travels 0.995 × 3.00 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹ × 2.2 × 10⁻⁶ s = 6.6 × 10² m in one lifetime, approximately an eighth of the distance, so I should expect almost none to reach the detector. Yet the detectors at sea level record muons in abundance, and one must trust what is counted. At 0.995c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.995²) = 10.0, and each frame resolves the result in its own way.
curie.21: In the Earth frame: the muon’s creation and decay both occur at the muon, which moves relative to Earth, so the 2.2 μs lifetime is the proper time. The dilated lifetime is t = γt0 = 10.0 × 2.2 μs = 22 μs. In 22 μs the muon travels 2.985 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹ × 2.2 × 10⁻⁵ s = 6.6 km, which exceeds the 5.0 km of atmosphere. The muon arrives.
curie.22: In the muon’s frame: the muon is at rest, its 2.2 μs lifetime is the proper time, and the atmosphere moves upward past it at 0.995c. The atmosphere is at rest relative to Earth, so 5.0 km is a proper length in the Earth frame; in the muon frame it is contracted to L = L0/γ = 5.0 × 10³ ÷ 10.0 = 5.0 × 10² m, crossed in 1.7 μs, which is less than one lifetime. The muon arrives.
curie.23: The frames disagree about times and distances, yet they agree on the one thing I can measure: the muon arrives. The time dilation of one frame is the length contraction of the other, as shown in Figure 11.2. I surrender the comfortable picture of a single time and a single length only because the count at the detector requires it.
curie.24: A linear accelerator 3.2 km long is at rest in the laboratory, so the laboratory measures its proper length. Electrons travelling along it at 0.998c (γ = 15.8) measure the beamline contracted to L = 3.2 × 10³ ÷ 15.8 = 2.0 × 10² m, and I must design the accelerating structures to operate for particles that experience this shorter length.
curie.25: A GPS receiver fixes its position from the travel times of timing signals broadcast by satellites, so the satellite clocks must agree with ground clocks to a precision I would once have thought needless. Each satellite orbits at approximately 3.9 × 10³ m s⁻¹, giving γ = 1 + 8.5 × 10⁻¹¹. The figure is small, yet over one day (86 400 s) the orbiting clock, which records proper time aboard the satellite, falls behind ground clocks by approximately 7.3 μs. Light travels 2.2 km in 7.3 μs, so an uncorrected system would drift by kilometres each day; for this reason the GPS clocks are corrected for their orbital speed. A small number, patiently accumulated, is not a small thing.
curie.26: Connects The satellites corrected here are the circular orbits of topic 2: the orbital radius fixes the speed v, and v fixes γ, so the relativistic correction is set by the very same orbital mechanics that placed the satellite in orbit.
curie.27: The final result of relativity concerns energy, and it is the one I find most quietly astonishing. A body at rest is not energetically empty: its mass is itself a store of energy, the rest energy, as my work with radioactive matter would in time confirm:
curie.28: Once the body is set in motion, its total energy is the sum of this rest energy and the kinetic energy:
curie.29: At low speeds (γ − 1)mc² agrees with ½mv², and this is precisely why the classical formula serves so well at everyday speeds. Near c the two expressions part company, and here one must trust the measurement and not the habit. For an electron at 0.99c (γ = 7.09), the classical formula gives ½mv² = ½ × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × (2.97 × 10⁸)² = 4.0 × 10⁻¹⁴ J, while Ek = (7.09 − 1) × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 9.00 × 10¹⁶ = 5.0 × 10⁻¹³ J, more than twelve times larger. As v → c, γ → ∞: the kinetic energy demanded grows without limit, so no massive body can ever attain the speed of light.
curie.30: The Sun radiates 3.8 × 10²⁶ W from the nuclear fusion in its core. Hydrogen nuclei fuse into helium, and the products carry slightly less mass than the reactants. I treat that radiated energy as converted mass, and the mass spent each second I compute as follows, taking nothing on faith that the arithmetic does not give me.
curie.31: Formula: each second the Sun radiates E = 3.8 × 10²⁶ J, so E0 = mc² rearranges to m = E0/c².
curie.33: Answer: m = 4.2 × 10⁹ kg each second, some four million tonnes of mass given up as sunlight in every passing second. Yet patience reassures us: this consumes well under 0.1% of the Sun’s mass over its lifetime.
curie.34: The conversion of mass into energy is total in positron–electron annihilation. The positron is the antiparticle of the electron and carries the same mass. When the two meet at negligible speed, both are annihilated and their whole rest energy departs as two gamma-ray photons: 2mec² = 2 × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 9.00 × 10¹⁶ = 1.64 × 10⁻¹³ J in total, which is 8.2 × 10⁻¹⁴ J ≈ 0.51 MeV per photon. The photons depart in opposite directions because the total momentum before annihilation is approximately zero. PET medical scanners, the daughters of the radiations I once measured, locate tumours by detecting these back-to-back pairs.
curie.35: Particle accelerators run the conversion the other way: collisions at large γ turn kinetic energy into the rest energy of new matter, bringing into being particles more massive than the ones that struck. These are nuclear transformations, governed by E0 = mc² in both directions alike.
curie.36: Trap γmc² is the total energy, not the kinetic energy. The kinetic energy is only the part standing above the rest energy: Ek = (γ − 1)mc². The classical ½mv² is no honest approximation near c; at 0.99c it understates the kinetic energy by a factor of about twelve, and the measurement will not forgive the substitution.
curie.37: Key idea Mass and energy are interchangeable at the one fixed rate E0 = mc². The Sun converts mass to radiation, annihilation converts mass wholly to photon pairs, and accelerators convert kinetic energy into the mass of new particles.
curie.38: Connects The radius formula r = mv/qB used for the synchrotron in topic 5 carries the condition v << c. The electrons of the Australian Synchrotron do not satisfy this condition by design, since their energy is thousands of times their rest energy; the magnetic fields of the machine must therefore be engineered for the relativistic energies on this page, not the classical formula. The evidence sets the design, never the other way about.

cox.0: As we move faster and faster, edging towards the speed of light, the comfortable old rules for time, length and energy quietly stop telling the truth. And what I find wonderful is that we worked this out anyway. This topic sets down Einstein’s two postulates, follows their consequences through the Lorentz factor, and arrives at one of the most beautiful statements in all of physics: that mass and energy are the same thing, E0 = mc².
cox.1: Classical mechanics quietly assumes that time flows at the same rate for everyone, that lengths are the same for all of us, that velocities simply add together, and that the kinetic energy ½mv² can climb forever. And at the speeds of everyday life, that picture works beautifully. But as we approach c = 3.00 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹, all four of those assumptions gently fall apart: an accelerator can pour energy into an electron without end, and yet the electron never quite reaches c. And I think that’s just extraordinary.
cox.2: Light travels at c, as we saw in topic 6, but classical physics could never say relative to what. And there’s the puzzle: classical velocity addition says that if you move towards a source of light, you ought to measure something faster than c. So nineteenth-century physics imagined the ether, a hidden medium through which light glides at c; and as the Earth swept along its orbit through this medium, there should be an “ether wind”, making the measured speed of light depend on which way you looked.
cox.3: In 1887 Michelson and Morley took a single beam of light, split it along two perpendicular arms of an interferometer, and brought the reflections back together into a delicate pattern of interference. If an ether wind were real, light running along the wind would take a different time from light running across it, and turning the apparatus should nudge those fringes by a precise, measurable amount. And the shift they saw, at every orientation, every hour of the day, every season of the year, was nothing at all: the null result.
cox.4: That quiet nothing tells us something profound: the measured speed of light is the same in every direction, no matter how the Earth is moving, and there is simply no sign of the ether. It points straight to the heart of special relativity, that c is the same for all observers, which Einstein took up in 1905 as a postulate. And I find it rather marvellous that an absence, a measurement of zero, can open a door this wide.
cox.5: Key idea Michelson and Morley predicted that an ether wind would shift the fringes as their interferometer turned; they watched, and under every condition there was no shift at all; and that gentle null result supports the postulate that the speed of light is the same for all observers, quite independent of the motion of the source or of the observer.
cox.6: Think about what we mean by a frame of reference: it’s simply the set of coordinates from which we measure positions, times and motions. And an inertial frame of reference is one that doesn’t accelerate; it drifts along at constant velocity. Now, constant speed alone isn’t enough, and I think that’s a subtle and rather lovely point: a car taking a bend at a steady speed is still changing direction, so its frame is non-inertial. Special relativity speaks only to inertial frames.
cox.7: And the whole of it rests on just two postulates:
cox.8: Postulate 1. The laws of physics are the same in every inertial frame of reference. There is no experiment you could perform, sealed inside your frame, that would reveal its absolute motion through the universe.
cox.9: Postulate 2. The speed of light in a vacuum, c, has the same value for every observer in an inertial frame, no matter how the source of the light is moving, and no matter how you yourself are moving.
cox.10: And here, in this second postulate, is the moment we part company with classical physics. Classically, if a train moving at 0.50c switches on its headlights, you’d expect someone standing at the trackside to measure that light at 1.50c; but the postulate insists that the driver and the trackside observer each measure exactly c. The only way two observers in relative motion can agree on the speed of one beam of light is if they quietly disagree about distances and about intervals of time. And so absolute time and absolute length simply have to be let go — and I think that’s just extraordinary.
cox.11: Trap “Inertial” means constant velocity, not constant speed. An electron circling the Australian Synchrotron storage ring at constant speed is forever accelerating inward, toward the centre, so its frame is non-inertial. The familiar time-dilation and length-contraction formulas belong to inertial frames.
cox.12: An event is simply something that happens, at one point in space and at one moment in time — the birth of a muon, say, or a single tick of a clock. And here is the remarkable thing: inertial observers moving relative to one another will not agree about the time that passes between two events, nor about the length of an object. Each of those disagreements, though, is anchored to one privileged measurement, which we call the proper quantity.
cox.13: Proper time t0 is the time interval between two events as measured in the frame of reference in which both events happen at the very same point in space. Both beats of a traveller’s heart happen there at the traveller’s chest, so it is the traveller who measures proper time; and every other observer, watching from outside, measures a longer, dilated time. In the same gentle way, proper length L0 is the length of an object measured in the frame in which the object is at rest. Anyone moving relative to it measures a shorter, contracted length — and only along the direction of the relative motion, which I think is rather beautiful.
cox.14: And so every relativity problem begins with two quiet questions, asked before a single formula is written down: in which frame do the two events occur at the same point in space — that observer measures t0 — and in which frame is the object, or the distance being crossed, at rest, so that that observer measures L0? How much the time stretches and the length shrinks is set by one number, the Lorentz factor:
cox.15: Now because v < c for anything with mass, γ is never less than 1: it is γ = 1 when the object is at rest, and at 0.10c it is still only 1.005, which is why classical mechanics serves us so well at the speeds of everyday life. At 0.50c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.25) = 1.15; at 0.90c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.81) = 2.29; at 0.99c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.9801) = 7.09. And as v → c the denominator slips toward zero and γ climbs without any limit at all, as shown in Figure 11.1.
cox.16: Trap Both observers’ measurements are correct, each in their own frame; a contracted length and a dilated time are real measurements, not illusions, and I think that’s just extraordinary. Proper time does not belong to the stationary observer by default, and proper time and proper length usually belong to different observers in the very same problem: a muon’s creation and decay both occur at the muon, so t0 is measured in the muon’s frame of reference, while the atmosphere through which it falls is at rest relative to Earth, so L0 is measured in the Earth frame. Locate the events and the object first; only then is γ assigned.
cox.17: Key idea The proper quantity is identified before any formula is applied: t0 belongs to the frame in which both events occur at the same point in space, and L0 belongs to the frame in which the object is at rest. And then, quite simply, t = γt0 stretches time and L = L0/γ shrinks length, by the same factor γ ≥ 1.
cox.18: Now here is where it becomes wonderful, because these are not just ideas on a page. There are three real systems out there in the world that show these predictions actually happening, and we can read each one using the same two quiet questions about proper quantities we asked above.
cox.19: High above us, cosmic rays come streaming in and strike the upper atmosphere, and out of that collision muons are born, about 5.0 km above a detector sitting at sea level, falling downward at 0.995c. A muon left to itself, at rest, lives on average for just 2.2 μs. And we picture each one travelling in a straight line, at a steady speed, until the moment it decays.
cox.20: Think about it classically and the muon goes 0.995 × 3.00 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹ × 2.2 × 10⁻⁶ s = 6.6 × 10² m in a single lifetime, roughly an eighth of the way down, so almost none of them ought to reach us at all. And yet the detectors at sea level catch muons in abundance. They arrive in their thousands. At 0.995c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.995²) = 10.0, and each frame, in its own gentle way, tells us how.
cox.21: In the Earth frame: the muon is born and the muon dies, both events happening right at the muon as it rushes past us, so that 2.2 μs lifetime is the proper time. The dilated lifetime is t = γt0 = 10.0 × 2.2 μs = 22 μs. And in those 22 μs the muon covers 2.985 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹ × 2.2 × 10⁻⁵ s = 6.6 km, which is more than the 5.0 km of atmosphere above it. The muon arrives.
cox.22: In the muon’s frame: now the muon is at rest, calmly living out its 2.2 μs proper time, while the whole atmosphere comes sweeping up past it at 0.995c. That atmosphere is at rest relative to Earth, so 5.0 km is a proper length in the Earth frame; but in the muon’s frame it is contracted to L = L0/γ = 5.0 × 10³ ÷ 10.0 = 5.0 × 10² m, crossed in 1.7 μs, less than a single lifetime. The muon arrives.
cox.23: The two frames disagree about the times and disagree about the distances, and yet they agree completely on the one thing you can actually see: the muon arrives. What is time dilation to one observer is length contraction to the other, the same truth told two ways, as shown in Figure 11.2. And I think that’s just extraordinary.
cox.24: Then there is a linear accelerator, 3.2 km long, sitting at rest in the laboratory, so the laboratory measures its proper length. But to the electrons flying along it at 0.998c (γ = 15.8), the whole beamline is contracted to L = 3.2 × 10³ ÷ 15.8 = 2.0 × 10² m, and so the accelerating structures have to be built for particles that genuinely experience this shorter length.
cox.25: And then there is the GPS in your pocket, which works out where it is from how long the timing signals take to arrive from the satellites, which means those satellite clocks have to agree with clocks on the ground to an almost unbelievable precision. Each satellite orbits at about 3.9 × 10³ m s⁻¹, giving γ = 1 + 8.5 × 10⁻¹¹. A tiny number, yes, but over a single day (86 400 s) the orbiting clock, which keeps proper time up there aboard the satellite, falls behind the ground clocks by about 7.3 μs. Light goes 2.2 km in 7.3 μs, so left uncorrected the whole system would wander off by kilometres every day; and so we correct the GPS clocks for their orbital speed. We hold the universe’s own rules in our hands, just to find our way home.
cox.26: Connects These satellites we’re correcting are the very same circular orbits from topic 2: the orbital radius sets the speed v, and v sets γ, so the relativistic correction is fixed by the same quiet orbital mechanics that lifted the satellite into the sky in the first place.
cox.27: And so we come to the last and, I think, the most beautiful result of all, which is about energy. A thing sitting perfectly still is not, as you might imagine, empty of energy. Its mass is itself a store of energy, what we call the rest energy:
cox.28: And when the object moves, its total energy is simply the rest energy and the kinetic energy, added together:
cox.29: At gentle, everyday speeds (γ − 1)mc² matches ½mv² almost exactly, and that is why the old classical formula serves us so well in ordinary life. But as you draw close to c the two part company. Take an electron moving at 0.99c (γ = 7.09): the classical formula gives ½mv² = ½ × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × (2.97 × 10⁸)² = 4.0 × 10⁻¹⁴ J, whereas the true value is Ek = (7.09 − 1) × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 9.00 × 10¹⁶ = 5.0 × 10⁻¹³ J, more than twelve times larger. And as v → c, γ → ∞: the energy you would need climbs without any limit at all, and that is why no object with mass can ever quite reach the speed of light.
cox.30: Look up at the Sun. It pours out 3.8 × 10²⁶ W from the nuclear fusion in its core. Down there, hydrogen nuclei fuse into helium, and the helium that comes out weighs a little less than the hydrogen that went in. We picture that radiated energy as the missing mass, made into light, and we can work out just how much mass vanishes each second, like this.
cox.31: Formula: every second the Sun radiates E = 3.8 × 10²⁶ J, so we simply rearrange E0 = mc² to give m = E0/c².
cox.33: Answer: m = 4.2 × 10⁹ kg each second, which is something like four million tonnes of mass turned into sunlight, every single second. And yet across its whole long lifetime the Sun spends well under 0.1% of itself doing this. I find that quietly astonishing.
cox.34: The turning of mass into energy is complete, total, in positron–electron annihilation. The positron is the antiparticle of the electron, with exactly the same mass. When the two of them meet, barely moving, both simply cease to be, and their entire rest energy departs as two gamma-ray photons: 2mec² = 2 × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 9.00 × 10¹⁶ = 1.64 × 10⁻¹³ J altogether, which comes to 8.2 × 10⁻¹⁴ J ≈ 0.51 MeV for each photon. They fly off in opposite directions, because the total momentum before they met was very nearly zero. And it is precisely these back-to-back pairs that PET medical scanners listen for, to find a tumour deep inside a living body.
cox.35: Particle accelerators run all of this the other way around: in collisions at enormous γ they take kinetic energy and turn it into the rest energy of brand-new matter, conjuring particles heavier than the ones that crashed together. These are nuclear transformations, and the same E0 = mc² governs them in both directions.
cox.36: Trap γmc² is the total energy, mind, not the kinetic energy. The kinetic energy is only the part that sits above the rest energy: Ek = (γ − 1)mc². And the old ½mv² simply will not do as an approximation near c; at 0.99c it falls short of the true kinetic energy by a factor of about twelve.
cox.37: Key idea Mass and energy are, in the end, two faces of the same thing, exchangeable at one fixed rate, E0 = mc². The Sun turns mass into radiation, annihilation turns mass wholly into pairs of photons, and accelerators turn kinetic energy into the mass of new particles. The same rule, all the way through.
cox.38: Connects The radius formula r = mv/qB we used for the synchrotron in topic 5 quietly carries the condition v << c. The electrons of the Australian Synchrotron are built not to obey it, their energy being thousands of times their rest energy, so the magnetic fields of the machine have to be engineered for the relativistic energies on this very page, and not for the classical formula at all.

greene.0: Picture yourself nudging an object faster and faster, ever closer to the speed of light. At some point the comfortable, classical rules for time, length and energy begin to give you the wrong answers. In what follows we will state Einstein’s two postulates, watch their consequences crystallise into a single number called the Lorentz factor, and arrive—step by careful step—at one of the most elegant statements in all of physics: that mass and energy are two faces of the same thing, E0 = mc².
greene.1: Classical mechanics rests on a few quiet assumptions we rarely question: that time flows at the same rate for everyone, that a ruler is a ruler no matter who holds it, that velocities simply add, and that the kinetic energy ½mv² can climb without ceiling. At the speeds of everyday life these assumptions match every experiment we can perform. But now imagine pushing toward c = 3.00 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹, and watch what happens: every one of those four assumptions quietly breaks. An accelerator can pour energy into an electron without end, and yet the electron’s speed creeps toward c and never quite touches it.
greene.2: Light travels at c, as we established in topic 6—but relative to what? Classical physics had no answer. If velocities simply add, then an observer rushing toward a light source ought to measure something greater than c. To plug this gap, nineteenth-century physicists imagined the ether, a hypothetical medium filling all of space, through which light glides at c. And here was the elegant prediction: as Earth sweeps along its orbit through this invisible sea, we ought to feel an “ether wind”, and the measured speed of light should depend on which way we look.
greene.3: So in 1887 Michelson and Morley set out to catch that wind. Picture a single beam of light split in two and sent down a pair of perpendicular arms, then recombined so the two reflections interfere. If an ether wind were blowing, light travelling along it would take a slightly different time from light travelling across it—and rotating the whole apparatus should make the interference fringes drift by a precise, calculable amount. They watched, and they waited, turning the instrument through every orientation, every hour of the day, every season of the year. The fringes never moved. This is the famous null result.
greene.4: Sit with what that silence tells us. The speed of light comes out the same in every direction, utterly indifferent to Earth’s motion, and there is no trace of the ether to be found. What at first looked like a failed experiment turns out to be a profound clue: it points straight at the central claim of special relativity, that c is the same for every observer—the very thing Einstein chose, in 1905, to elevate to a postulate.
greene.5: Key idea Michelson and Morley predicted that an ether wind would shift the fringes as their interferometer turned; instead they found no shift under any conditions whatsoever. That null result supports the postulate that the speed of light is the same for all observers, independent of the motion of the source or of the observer.
greene.6: Begin with the idea of a frame of reference — nothing more than the grid of coordinates from which you read off where things are, when they happen, and how they move.
