Now picture yourself in an inertial frame of reference: one that never accelerates, gliding along at constant velocity. Notice that constant speed alone will not do. Imagine sitting in a car as it sweeps around a bend at a perfectly steady speed; your direction is forever changing, and so your frame is non-inertial. It is to inertial frames, and only these, that special relativity speaks.
greene.7: The entire edifice rests on just two postulates:
greene.8: Postulate 1. The laws of physics are the same in every inertial frame of reference. No experiment you could ever perform, sealed within your own inertial frame, can reveal that frame's absolute motion — for there is no such thing to reveal.
greene.9: Postulate 2. The speed of light in a vacuum, c, takes the same value for every observer in an inertial frame, no matter how the source of the light may be moving, and no matter how the observer may be moving.
greene.10: It is this second postulate that quietly severs us from the classical world. Picture a train rolling along at 0.50c; it switches on its headlights. Common sense, and classical physics with it, insists that someone standing by the track must clock that light at 1.50c — the train's speed simply added to the light's. The postulate refuses. The driver and the trackside observer alike measure exactly c, not a sliver more. And here is the subtle, inescapable consequence: two observers in relative motion can agree on the speed of one and the same light beam only if they quietly disagree about distances and about intervals of time. Absolute time and absolute length, those bedrock certainties, must therefore be let go.
greene.11: Trap “Inertial” means constant velocity, never merely constant speed. Picture an electron circulating in the Australian Synchrotron storage ring: its speed holds steady, yet at every instant it accelerates inward, toward the centre, so its frame is non-inertial. The familiar formulas for time dilation and length contraction belong to inertial frames.
greene.12: Begin with the simplest building block. An event is simply something that happens at one point in space and one moment in time, the creation of a muon, say, or a single tick of a clock. Here is the subtlety: two inertial observers gliding past one another will not agree on the time elapsed between a pair of events, nor on the length of an object. Each disagreement, though, is anchored to one privileged vantage point, a measurement we call the proper quantity.
greene.13: Consider proper time t0, the interval between two events as recorded in the one frame where both events happen at the very same place. Picture a traveller's heart: two successive beats both occur right there at the traveller's chest, so it is the traveller who reads off proper time, while every other observer, watching that chest sweep past, measures a longer, dilated interval. Now turn to proper length L0, the length of an object measured in the frame where it sits at rest. Anyone moving relative to that object finds it shortened, contracted, and contracted only along the direction of the relative motion, never across it.
greene.14: So before you reach for a single formula, pause and ask yourself two questions. First: in which frame do the two events happen at the same point in space? That observer is the one holding t0. Second: in which frame is the object, or the stretch of distance being crossed, at rest? That observer is the one holding L0. Answer those, and only those, before anything else. How much the time stretches and the length shrinks is then dictated by a single number, the Lorentz factor:
greene.15: Notice that since v < c for anything carrying mass, γ can never dip below 1. At rest γ = 1, and even at 0.10c it has crept up to a mere 1.005, which is precisely why classical mechanics serves us so faithfully at everyday speeds. Push harder, though, and watch it climb: at 0.50c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.25) = 1.15; at 0.90c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.81) = 2.29; at 0.99c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.9801) = 7.09. As v → c the denominator slides toward zero and γ races off without bound, as shown in Figure 11.1.
greene.16: Trap Resist the temptation to call one observer right and the other deluded. Both measurements are entirely correct, each within its own frame; a contracted length and a dilated time are genuine readings of nature, not optical tricks. Proper time does not automatically belong to whoever happens to be standing still, and proper time and proper length usually live with different observers in the same problem. Follow a muon: its creation and its decay both happen right at the muon, so t0 is measured in the muon's frame of reference, while the atmosphere it plummets through sits at rest relative to Earth, so L0 is measured in the Earth frame. Locate the events and the object first; only then do you hand out γ.
greene.17: Key idea Identify the proper quantity before you touch a single formula: t0 belongs to the frame in which both events occur at the same point in space, and L0 belongs to the frame in which the object is at rest. With those settled, t = γt0 stretches time and L = L0/γ shrinks length, both governed by the very same factor γ ≥ 1.
greene.18: Picture three real systems in which these predictions step out of the equations and into the world. Each one yields to the same two questions we have been asking all along: where do the events share a single point in space, and in which frame does the object sit at rest?
greene.19: Begin high overhead. Cosmic rays strike the upper atmosphere and, in the collision, conjure muons roughly 5.0 km above a detector waiting at sea level, plunging downward at 0.995c. Now hold one of these muons still in your imagination: at rest, it lives, on average, just 2.2 μs before it decays. Picture each one falling in a straight line at constant speed until that moment arrives.
greene.20: Reason classically for a moment, as the nineteenth century would have. In a single lifetime the muon covers 0.995 × 3.00 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹ × 2.2 × 10⁻⁶ s = 6.6 × 10² m, only about an eighth of the descent, so scarcely any muon should survive to be counted. And yet the detectors at sea level register them in abundance. Something in the classical picture is missing. At 0.995c the Lorentz factor is γ = 1/√(1 − 0.995²) = 10.0, and watch how each frame, telling its own story, arrives at the very same ending.
greene.21: In the Earth frame: the muon is born and the muon dies, and both events happen right where the muon is, as it streaks past the Earth. So that 2.2 μs lifetime is the proper time, and for us, watching it rush by, time stretches: t = γt0 = 10.0 × 2.2 μs = 22 μs. In those 22 μs the muon travels 2.985 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹ × 2.2 × 10⁻⁵ s = 6.6 km, more than the 5.0 km of atmosphere standing in its way. The muon reaches us.
greene.22: In the muon’s frame: now ride along with the muon. Here it is the muon that holds still, its 2.2 μs lifetime once again the proper time, while the entire atmosphere comes rushing upward to meet it at 0.995c. That atmosphere is at rest relative to Earth, so its 5.0 km thickness is a proper length in the Earth frame; but to the muon, hurtling through it, that thickness collapses to L = L0/γ = 5.0 × 10³ ÷ 10.0 = 5.0 × 10² m, a span crossed in just 1.7 μs, comfortably within a single lifetime. The muon reaches us.
greene.23: The two frames quarrel over times and over distances, yet they fall into perfect agreement on the one thing you can actually observe: the muon arrives. What one frame calls a stretching of time, the other calls a shrinking of space, two faces of a single underlying truth, as Figure 11.2 lays bare.
greene.24: Now bring the experiment down to Earth. A linear accelerator stretches 3.2 km across the laboratory floor, sitting still in the lab, so the lab reads off its proper length. But ride with an electron streaming down it at 0.998c (γ = 15.8), and the whole beamline contracts to L = 3.2 × 10³ ÷ 15.8 = 2.0 × 10² m. The accelerating structures, then, must be engineered for particles that genuinely experience this foreshortened world.
greene.25: Finally, glance at the device in your pocket. A GPS receiver pins down where you stand by clocking how long timing signals take to arrive from satellites overhead, which means those satellite clocks must keep step with ground clocks to a staggering precision. Each satellite circles at roughly 3.9 × 10³ m s⁻¹, giving γ = 1 + 8.5 × 10⁻¹¹, a whisper of a number. Yet whispers accumulate: across a single day of 86 400 s, the orbiting clock, faithfully recording proper time aboard the satellite, slips behind the ground clocks by about 7.3 μs. In 7.3 μs light covers 2.2 km, so left uncorrected the system would wander off by kilometres every day. And so GPS clocks are deliberately tuned to account for their own orbital speed.
greene.26: Connects These very satellites are the circular orbits you met in topic 2: the orbital radius fixes the speed v, the speed v fixes γ, and so the relativistic correction traces straight back to the same orbital mechanics that lifted the satellite into the sky in the first place.
greene.27: Now we arrive at the most elegant result of the whole journey, and it concerns energy. Picture yourself standing beside an object that is utterly at rest. You might suppose it holds nothing in reserve. Yet here is the discovery: its very mass is itself a vast store of energy, the rest energy:
greene.28: Now set that object in motion. Its total energy is no longer the rest energy alone; it is the rest energy together with the energy of motion, the two added in sum:
greene.29: Step carefully through the low-speed limit, and you find that (γ − 1)mc² quietly settles into the familiar ½mv² — which is precisely why the classical formula serves us so faithfully at everyday speeds. But push toward c and watch the two expressions part company. Picture an electron streaking along at 0.99c (γ = 7.09). The classical recipe offers ½mv² = ½ × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × (2.97 × 10⁸)² = 4.0 × 10⁻¹⁴ J, while the true relativistic energy is Ek = (7.09 − 1) × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 9.00 × 10¹⁶ = 5.0 × 10⁻¹³ J — more than twelve times larger. And as v → c, γ → ∞: the energy you would need to supply climbs without any ceiling at all. The lesson follows inescapably — no massive object can ever reach the speed of light.
greene.30: Lift your gaze to the Sun, pouring out 3.8 × 10²⁶ W from the nuclear fusion churning in its core. There, hydrogen nuclei fuse into helium, and here is the subtle point: the products carry slightly less mass than the reactants went in with. That radiated energy is nothing other than mass transformed — and we can ask, second by second, exactly how much mass the Sun spends in this way.
greene.31: Begin with what each second delivers: the Sun radiates E = 3.8 × 10²⁶ J. Now turn E0 = mc² around to ask for the mass, and it rearranges into m = E0/c².
greene.32: Now put the numbers in their places: m = 3.8 × 10²⁶ ÷ (3.00 × 10⁸)² = 3.8 × 10²⁶ ÷ 9.00 × 10¹⁶
greene.33: And the result emerges: m = 4.2 × 10⁹ kg every single second — picture some four million tonnes of mass streaming away as sunlight with each tick of the clock. Yet take the long view, and over the whole of its lifetime the Sun spends well under 0.1% of itself this way.
greene.34: Carry the idea to its absolute extreme and you reach positron–electron annihilation, where the conversion of mass into energy is total. The positron is the electron's antiparticle, carrying exactly the same mass. Now let the two meet, drifting together at negligible speed: both vanish, and the whole of their rest energy departs as two gamma-ray photons — 2mec² = 2 × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 9.00 × 10¹⁶ = 1.64 × 10⁻¹³ J in all, which is 8.2 × 10⁻¹⁴ J ≈ 0.51 MeV carried by each photon. Notice that they fly off in opposite directions, for the momentum they began with was very nearly zero and the books must balance. It is precisely these back-to-back pairs that PET medical scanners listen for as they hunt down tumours.
greene.35: Particle accelerators run this same exchange in reverse: in collisions at large γ, kinetic energy is woven into the rest energy of fresh matter, conjuring particles heavier than the ones that collided to make them. These are nuclear transformations, and in both directions they answer to one and the same ledger, E0 = mc².
greene.36: Trap Hold fast to the distinction: γmc² is the total energy, not the kinetic energy. The kinetic energy is only the portion riding above the rest energy: Ek = (γ − 1)mc². And do not lean on the classical ½mv² anywhere near c — at 0.99c it falls short of the true kinetic energy by a factor of about twelve.
greene.37: Key idea Mass and energy are two faces of one thing, freely interchanged at the fixed rate E0 = mc². The Sun turns mass into radiation, annihilation turns mass wholly into pairs of photons, and accelerators turn kinetic energy into the mass of new particles.
greene.38: Connects Recall the radius formula r = mv/qB that served the synchrotron in topic 5; it came quietly bound to the condition v << c. The electrons of the Australian Synchrotron, by deliberate design, refuse that condition — their energy runs to thousands of times their rest energy — so the machine's magnetic fields must be engineered for the relativistic energies of this very page, not for the classical formula.

hawking.0: Approach the speed of light, and the classical rules for time, length and energy break down. They give the wrong answers. This topic sets out Einstein’s two postulates, measures their consequences through the Lorentz factor, and arrives at a single profound conclusion: mass and energy are the same thing. E0 = mc².
hawking.1: Classical mechanics makes four assumptions. Time passes at the same rate for everyone. Lengths are the same for everyone. Velocities simply add. And the kinetic energy ½mv² can grow without limit. At everyday speeds these assumptions work. Approach c = 3.00 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹, and all four fail. An accelerator can pour energy into an electron forever. The electron never reaches c.
hawking.2: Light travels at c, as topic 6 established. But classical physics could not say relative to what. Classical velocity addition demands that an observer moving toward a source measure something greater than c. So nineteenth-century physics invented a medium. The ether: a substance through which light moves at c. Earth’s orbit through this ether should produce an “ether wind”, and the measured speed of light should depend on direction.
hawking.3: In 1887 Michelson and Morley split a beam of light along two perpendicular arms, then recombined it into an interference pattern. An ether wind would make light along the wind take a different time from light across it. Rotate the apparatus, and the fringes should shift by a predicted, measurable amount. They shifted by nothing. At every orientation, every hour, every season. This is the null result.
hawking.4: The measured speed of light is the same in all directions, whatever Earth is doing. There is no ether. There never was. The experiment supports the central claim of special relativity, that c is the same for all observers. Einstein adopted it in 1905 as a postulate.
hawking.5: Key idea Michelson and Morley predicted that an ether wind would shift their fringes when the interferometer turned. They saw no shift under any conditions. The null result supports the postulate that the speed of light is the same for all observers, regardless of how the source or the observer moves.
hawking.6: A frame of reference is the set of coordinates from which we measure positions, times and motions. An inertial frame of reference does not accelerate. It moves at constant velocity. Constant speed is not enough. A car rounding a bend at steady speed changes direction, so its frame is non-inertial. Special relativity applies to inertial frames, and to nothing else.
hawking.7: The theory rests on two postulates. They are short. The consequences are not.
hawking.8: Postulate 1. The laws of physics are the same in all inertial frames of reference. No experiment performed within an inertial frame can reveal its absolute motion. There is no such thing as absolute rest.
hawking.9: Postulate 2. The speed of light in a vacuum, c, has the same value for all observers in inertial frames. It does not matter how the source moves. It does not matter how the observer moves. The answer is always c.
hawking.10: The second postulate is the break with classical physics. Classically, if a train moving at 0.50c turns on its headlights, a trackside observer should measure the light at 1.50c. The postulate says otherwise. The driver and the trackside observer each measure exactly c. Two observers in relative motion can agree on the speed of the same light beam only if they disagree about distances and time intervals. So absolute time and absolute length must go. This is not science fiction. It is measured fact.
hawking.11: Trap “Inertial” means constant velocity, not constant speed. An electron circulating in the Australian Synchrotron storage ring moves at constant speed, yet it accelerates continuously toward the centre. Its frame is non-inertial. The standard time-dilation and length-contraction formulas apply to inertial frames alone.
hawking.12: An event is something that happens at a single point in space at a single moment in time. The birth of a muon. One tick of a clock. Nothing more. Observers moving relative to one another will not agree on the time between two events, nor on the length of an object. They are not mistaken. Each disagreement is settled by one privileged measurement: the proper quantity.
hawking.13: Proper time t0 is the time between two events measured in the frame where both happen at the same point in space. A traveller’s heart beats twice, both beats at the traveller’s chest. So the traveller measures proper time. Everyone else measures more. Time is dilated. Proper length L0 is the length of an object measured in the frame where it is at rest. Move relative to it and it shrinks. The length is contracted, but only along the direction of motion.
hawking.14: Every relativity problem begins with two questions, asked before a single formula is written. In which frame do the two events happen at the same point in space? That observer measures t0. In which frame is the object, or the distance crossed, at rest? That observer measures L0. How much time stretches and how much length shrinks is fixed by one number, the Lorentz factor:
hawking.15: For any object with mass, v < c. So γ is never below 1. At rest, γ = 1. At 0.10c it is still only 1.005, which is why classical mechanics survives in everyday life. At 0.50c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.25) = 1.15. At 0.90c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.81) = 2.29. At 0.99c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.9801) = 7.09. As v → c the denominator falls toward zero and γ climbs without bound, as shown in Figure 11.1.
hawking.16: Trap Both observers are right, each in their own frame. A contracted length and a dilated time are real measurements, not tricks of the mind. Proper time does not belong to the stationary observer by default, and proper time and proper length usually belong to different observers in the same problem. A muon is born and dies at the muon, so t0 is measured in the muon’s frame. The atmosphere it falls through is at rest with Earth, so L0 is measured in the Earth frame. Locate the events and the object first. Only then assign γ.
hawking.17: Key idea Identify the proper quantity before any formula. t0 belongs to the frame where both events happen at the same point in space. L0 belongs to the frame where the object is at rest. Then t = γt0 stretches time and L = L0/γ shrinks length, by the same factor γ ≥ 1.
hawking.18: Three real systems put these predictions to the test. Each yields to the same two questions about proper quantities.
hawking.19: Cosmic rays strike the upper atmosphere. They create muons some 5.0 km above a detector at sea level. The muons fall at 0.995c. At rest, a muon lives on average 2.2 μs. Then it is gone. We model each one as a straight line at constant speed, ending in decay.
hawking.20: Classically, the muon covers 0.995 × 3.00 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹ × 2.2 × 10⁻⁶ s = 6.6 × 10² m in a lifetime. That is about an eighth of the way. Almost none should survive the trip. Yet the detectors at sea level count them in abundance. The muons arrive. At 0.995c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.995²) = 10.0, and each frame explains the same fact in its own language.
hawking.21: In the Earth frame: the muon is born and dies at the same point, which moves relative to Earth. So the 2.2 μs lifetime is the proper time. Dilated, it becomes t = γt0 = 10.0 × 2.2 μs = 22 μs. In 22 μs the muon travels 2.985 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹ × 2.2 × 10⁻⁵ s = 6.6 km. That is more than the 5.0 km of atmosphere. The muon arrives.
hawking.22: In the muon’s frame: the muon is at rest. Its 2.2 μs lifetime is the proper time, and the atmosphere rushes upward past it at 0.995c. The atmosphere is at rest relative to Earth, so 5.0 km is a proper length in the Earth frame. In the muon frame it shrinks to L = L0/γ = 5.0 × 10³ ÷ 10.0 = 5.0 × 10² m, crossed in 1.7 μs, less than a single lifetime. The muon arrives.
hawking.23: The two frames disagree about times and distances. They agree on what can be seen: the muon arrives. One frame’s dilated time is the other frame’s contracted length, as shown in Figure 11.2.
hawking.24: A linear accelerator 3.2 km long sits at rest in the laboratory, so the laboratory measures its proper length. Electrons racing along it at 0.998c (γ = 15.8) see the beamline contracted to L = 3.2 × 10³ ÷ 15.8 = 2.0 × 10² m. The accelerating structures must be built for particles that experience this shorter length. The engineering bends to the physics, not the other way around.
hawking.25: A GPS receiver fixes its position from the travel times of signals from satellites. So the satellite clocks must match ground clocks to extraordinary precision. Each satellite orbits at about 3.9 × 10³ m s⁻¹, giving γ = 1 + 8.5 × 10⁻¹¹. A tiny number. Yet over one day (86 400 s) the orbiting clock, which keeps proper time aboard the satellite, falls behind the ground by about 7.3 μs. In 7.3 μs light travels 2.2 km. Left uncorrected, the system would wander by kilometres every day. So the clocks are corrected for their speed. This is not science fiction. It is engineering.
hawking.26: Connects These satellites are the circular orbits of topic 2. The radius fixes the speed v, and v fixes γ. The relativistic correction is set by the very same orbital mechanics that placed the satellite in the sky.
hawking.27: Relativity ends with energy. A stationary object is not empty. Its mass is energy, locked away and waiting. We call this the rest energy:
hawking.28: Set the object moving, and its total energy is the rest energy plus the kinetic energy:
hawking.29: At low speeds (γ − 1)mc² matches ½mv². This is why the old formula works in everyday life. Near c the two part ways. Take an electron at 0.99c (γ = 7.09). The classical formula gives ½mv² = ½ × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × (2.97 × 10⁸)² = 4.0 × 10⁻¹⁴ J. The true value is Ek = (7.09 − 1) × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 9.00 × 10¹⁶ = 5.0 × 10⁻¹³ J, more than twelve times larger. As v → c, γ → ∞. The energy needed grows without limit. So no object with mass will ever reach the speed of light. Nothing can.
hawking.30: The Sun pours out 3.8 × 10²⁶ W from fusion in its core. Hydrogen nuclei fuse into helium, and the products weigh slightly less than the reactants. The missing mass becomes light. Each second, this much mass disappears.
hawking.31: Formula: each second the Sun radiates E = 3.8 × 10²⁶ J. So E0 = mc² rearranges to m = E0/c².
hawking.33: Answer: m = 4.2 × 10⁹ kg each second. That is four million tonnes vanishing into sunlight, every second. Yet over its whole life the Sun spends well under 0.1% of its mass this way.
hawking.34: In positron–electron annihilation the conversion is total. The positron is the electron’s antiparticle, with the same mass. When the two meet at rest, both vanish, and all their rest energy departs as two gamma-ray photons: 2mec² = 2 × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 9.00 × 10¹⁶ = 1.64 × 10⁻¹³ J in total, which is 8.2 × 10⁻¹⁴ J ≈ 0.51 MeV per photon. They fly apart in opposite directions, because the momentum before was nearly zero. PET scanners find tumours by catching these back-to-back pairs. This is not science fiction. It is routine medicine.
hawking.35: Accelerators run it backward. Collisions at large γ turn kinetic energy into the rest mass of new matter, heavier than the particles that collided. Mass into energy, energy into mass. Both are nuclear transformations, both ruled by E0 = mc².
hawking.36: Trap γmc² is the total energy, not the kinetic energy. The kinetic energy is only what lies above the rest energy: Ek = (γ − 1)mc². The classical ½mv² will not do near c. At 0.99c it underestimates the kinetic energy by a factor of about twelve.
hawking.37: Key idea Mass and energy are the same thing, traded at the fixed rate E0 = mc². The Sun turns mass into radiation, annihilation turns mass wholly into photon pairs, and accelerators turn kinetic energy into the mass of new particles.
hawking.38: Connects The radius formula r = mv/qB used for the synchrotron in topic 5 carries the condition v << c. The electrons of the Australian Synchrotron break that condition on purpose, since their energy is thousands of times their rest energy. So the machine’s magnetic fields must be built for the relativistic energies on this page, not the classical formula.

nye.0: Now check this out! When things start zooming along close to the speed of light, the everyday rules you trust for time, length and energy give you the wrong answers — totally wrong! In this topic we’ll lay out Einstein’s two postulates, we’ll pin down their consequences with the Lorentz factor, and we’ll finish with one of the greatest ideas in all of science: mass and energy are the same stuff — E0 = mc²!
nye.1: Here’s what classical mechanics assumes, and it sounds totally reasonable: time ticks at the same rate for everybody, lengths are the same for everybody, velocities just add up, and the kinetic energy ½mv² can climb as high as you like with no ceiling. And at the speeds we deal with every day — walking, driving, even flying — those assumptions match experiments beautifully! But consider the following: as speeds creep up toward c = 3.00 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹, all four of those assumptions break down. Watch — an accelerator can keep pumping energy into an electron forever, and yet that electron’s speed never quite reaches c. Never!
nye.2: We know light travels at c — we nailed that down back in topic 6 — but here’s the puzzle classical physics couldn’t crack: at c relative to what? Classical velocity addition says if you go racing toward a light source, you ought to clock the light coming in faster than c. So nineteenth-century physicists came up with an idea — the ether, a sort of invisible medium filling all of space that light supposedly travels through at c. And get this: as Earth sails along its orbit through that ether, you’d expect an “ether wind” to blow past us, making the measured speed of light come out different depending on which way you point your instrument!
nye.3: So in 1887, Michelson and Morley built a beautiful experiment to catch that wind. Here’s how it works: they took one beam of light, split it down two interferometer arms set at right angles to each other, then recombined the reflections to make an interference pattern. If there were an ether wind, light going with the wind would take a different time than light going across it — so when they spun the whole apparatus around, the fringes should shift by a definite, measurable amount. And what did they see? Nothing! Zero shift — at every orientation, every time of day, every season. That’s the famous null result!
nye.4: Think about what that null result tells us! The measured speed of light is exactly the same in every direction, no matter how Earth is moving — and there’s not a shred of evidence for any ether. It backs up the big idea at the heart of special relativity: c is the same for all observers, period. And that’s the very idea Einstein grabbed hold of in 1905 and made into a postulate!
nye.5: Key idea Michelson and Morley predicted an ether wind would shift the fringes when their interferometer rotated — but they saw no shift under any conditions whatsoever. That null result supports the postulate that the speed of light is the same for all observers, no matter how the source or the observer is moving!
nye.6: Now check this out — a frame of reference is just the set of coordinates you use to measure where things are, when they happen, and how they move. Here’s the key: an inertial frame of reference never accelerates — it cruises along at constant velocity! And watch out, constant speed isn’t enough: a car taking a bend at a steady speed is still changing direction, so its frame is non-inertial. Special relativity? It plays by the rules of inertial frames only!
nye.7: And the whole magnificent theory rests on just two postulates — consider the following!
nye.8: Postulate 1. The laws of physics are exactly the same in every inertial frame of reference! No experiment you run inside an inertial frame can tell you its absolute motion — not one!
nye.9: Postulate 2. Now here’s the wild one: the speed of light in a vacuum, c, comes out to the same value for every single observer in an inertial frame — it doesn’t matter how the light source is moving, and it doesn’t matter how you’re moving!
nye.10: This second postulate is where we break from classical physics — and it’s amazing! Classically, if a train zipping along at 0.50c flips on its headlights, you’d figure someone standing by the track should clock that light at 1.50c, right? Nope! The postulate says the driver AND the trackside observer each measure exactly c! And here’s the mind-bender: two observers moving relative to each other can only agree on the speed of the same light beam if they disagree about distances and time intervals. So absolute time and absolute length? We toss them out!
nye.11: Trap Don’t get fooled — “inertial” means constant velocity, not constant speed! Take an electron zooming around the Australian Synchrotron storage ring at constant speed: it’s accelerating toward the centre the whole way, so its frame is non-inertial. The standard time-dilation and length-contraction formulas only apply to inertial frames!
nye.12: Consider the following! An event is just something that happens at one particular spot in space at one particular moment in time — the birth of a muon, say, or a single tick of a clock. Now check this out: inertial observers zooming past each other will flat-out disagree about the time interval between two events, and they’ll disagree about how long an object is, too! How do we keep score? Each comparison gets anchored by one special, privileged measurement we call the proper quantity.
nye.13: Here’s the first one. Proper time t0 is the time interval between two events measured in the frame of reference where both events happen at the very same point in space. Picture a space traveller: both beats of their heart happen right there at their chest, so the traveller measures proper time — and everybody else? They clock a longer, dilated time! And then there’s proper length L0, the length of an object measured in the frame where that object is sitting still. Anyone moving past it measures a shorter, contracted length — and get this, it’s squished only along the direction of the relative motion!
nye.14: Now here’s the trick — every single relativity problem kicks off with two questions you ask before you touch a formula! First: in which frame do the two events happen at the same point in space? That observer measures t0. Second: in which frame is the object — or the distance being crossed — sitting at rest? That observer measures L0. And just how much stretching and squishing do you get? That’s set by the magnificent Lorentz factor:
nye.15: Here’s the beautiful part: since v < c for anything with mass, γ is never, ever less than 1! At rest, γ = 1, and even cruising at 0.10c it’s still only 1.005 — that’s why good old classical mechanics does the job at everyday speeds. But watch what happens as you crank it up! At 0.50c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.25) = 1.15; at 0.90c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.81) = 2.29; and at 0.99c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.9801) = 7.09! As v → c the denominator dives toward zero and γ rockets up without any limit at all, just like you see in Figure 11.1.
nye.16: Trap Don’t fall for it — both observers’ measurements are absolutely correct in their own frames! A contracted length and a dilated time are real, honest-to-goodness measurements, not optical illusions. And here’s the kicker: proper time does NOT automatically belong to the stationary observer, and proper time and proper length usually belong to two different observers in the same problem! Check this out — a muon’s creation and decay both happen right at the muon, so t0 is measured in the muon’s frame of reference, while the atmosphere it’s falling through sits at rest relative to Earth, so L0 is measured in the Earth frame. Locate the events and the object first — only THEN do you assign γ!
nye.17: Key idea Pin down the proper quantity before you touch a single formula! t0 belongs to the frame where both events happen at the same point in space, and L0 belongs to the frame where the object is at rest. Then — here we go — t = γt0 stretches time out and L = L0/γ shrinks length down, both by that very same factor γ ≥ 1. Isn’t that something?
nye.18: Now check this out — we’ve got three real systems out there in the world that show these predictions actually working! And here’s the great part: we crack every single one of them open using those same two proper-quantity questions from above.
nye.19: Consider the following! Cosmic rays smack into the upper atmosphere and create muons about 5.0 km above a detector sitting down at sea level, and these muons come barrelling straight down at 0.995c. Now, a muon just sitting still lives for an average of 2.2 μs — that’s its lifetime. We’ll picture each one zooming in a straight line at constant speed until, pop, it decays.
nye.20: Here’s what classical physics tells us: in one lifetime the muon travels 0.995 × 3.00 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹ × 2.2 × 10⁻⁶ s = 6.6 × 10² m — that’s only about an eighth of the way down! So almost none of them should ever reach the detector. But check this out — detectors at sea level catch these muons by the bucketload! What gives? Well, at 0.995c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.995²) = 10.0, and each frame has its own beautiful way of explaining the mystery.
nye.21: In the Earth frame: the muon is born and dies right at the muon, and the muon is zipping along relative to Earth — so that 2.2 μs lifetime is the proper time! And here’s the payoff: the stretched-out, dilated lifetime is t = γt0 = 10.0 × 2.2 μs = 22 μs. In those 22 μs the muon covers 2.985 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹ × 2.2 × 10⁻⁵ s = 6.6 km — that’s more than the 5.0 km of atmosphere! The muon makes it!
nye.22: In the muon’s frame: now flip it around — the muon thinks it’s standing perfectly still, its 2.2 μs lifetime is the proper time, and it’s the whole atmosphere that comes rushing up past it at 0.995c! The atmosphere sits at rest relative to Earth, so 5.0 km is a proper length in the Earth frame. But in the muon’s frame? It gets squished down to L = L0/γ = 5.0 × 10³ ÷ 10.0 = 5.0 × 10² m, and the muon crosses that in just 1.7 μs — way less than one lifetime! The muon makes it!
nye.23: Isn’t that something? The two frames argue all day about times and distances, but they shake hands on the one thing you can actually see: the muon arrives! The time dilation in one frame is exactly the length contraction in the other — two sides of the same coin, as shown in Figure 11.2.
nye.24: Now consider a linear accelerator 3.2 km long, bolted down at rest in the laboratory — so the lab measures its proper length. But check this out from the electron’s point of view! Electrons screaming down it at 0.998c (γ = 15.8) see the beamline scrunched down to L = 3.2 × 10³ ÷ 15.8 = 2.0 × 10² m. So the accelerating structures have to be engineered for particles that genuinely experience this shorter length. Science!
nye.25: And here’s one in your pocket! A GPS receiver figures out where you are from how long timing signals take to arrive from satellites — which means those satellite clocks have to match ground clocks to crazy-high precision. Each satellite orbits at about 3.9 × 10³ m s⁻¹, giving γ = 1 + 8.5 × 10⁻¹¹. Tiny, right? But watch what happens over a whole day (86 400 s): the orbiting clock, ticking off proper time up on the satellite, falls behind the ground clocks by about 7.3 μs. And light travels 2.2 km in 7.3 μs! So a system that ignored this would drift by kilometres every single day — that’s why GPS clocks get corrected for their orbital speed. Relativity is keeping you from getting lost!
nye.26: Connects Those satellites we just corrected? They’re the very same circular orbits from topic 2! Here’s how it all links up: the orbital radius fixes the speed v, and v fixes γ — so the relativistic correction is set by the exact same orbital mechanics that parked the satellite up there in the first place. It all connects!
nye.27: Now check this out — the grand finale of relativity is all about energy! Here’s the thing: an object just sitting there, perfectly still, is not energetically empty. Not even close! Its mass is itself a store of energy, and we call it the rest energy:
nye.28: And get this — once the object starts moving, its total energy is simply the rest energy plus the kinetic energy, added right together:
nye.29: At everyday speeds, (γ − 1)mc² matches up with good old ½mv² — that’s exactly why the classical formula works so well when you’re tossing a baseball! But watch what happens when you get close to c: the two expressions split apart dramatically. Consider an electron zooming at 0.99c (γ = 7.09). The classical formula gives ½mv² = ½ × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × (2.97 × 10⁸)² = 4.0 × 10⁻¹⁴ J, but the real deal is Ek = (7.09 − 1) × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 9.00 × 10¹⁶ = 5.0 × 10⁻¹³ J — more than twelve times bigger! And as v → c, γ → ∞: the kinetic energy you’d need shoots up forever. That’s the cosmic speed limit, folks — no massive object can ever reach the speed of light!
nye.30: Consider the following! Our own Sun pumps out 3.8 × 10²⁶ W from nuclear fusion roaring away in its core. Hydrogen nuclei smash together into helium, and here’s the kicker — the helium weighs just a little bit less than the hydrogen that made it! That missing mass becomes the radiated energy, and we can figure out exactly how much mass disappears every single second, like so.
nye.31: Formula: every second the Sun radiates E = 3.8 × 10²⁶ J, so we take E0 = mc² and rearrange it to m = E0/c². Easy!
nye.32: Substitution: now plug in the numbers — m = 3.8 × 10²⁶ ÷ (3.00 × 10⁸)² = 3.8 × 10²⁶ ÷ 9.00 × 10¹⁶
nye.33: Answer: m = 4.2 × 10⁹ kg each second — that’s about four million tonnes of mass turned into sunlight every single second! And here’s what blows my mind: even at that incredible rate, the Sun burns up well under 0.1% of its total mass over its entire lifetime. Talk about a big tank of gas!
nye.34: Now check this out — sometimes mass turns into energy completely, and that happens in positron–electron annihilation. The positron is the antiparticle of the electron, same mass and everything. When these two meet up at a crawl, they wipe each other out entirely, and their whole rest energy comes flying out as two gamma-ray photons: 2mec² = 2 × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 9.00 × 10¹⁶ = 1.64 × 10⁻¹³ J all together, which works out to 8.2 × 10⁻¹⁴ J ≈ 0.51 MeV per photon. And the photons shoot off in opposite directions, because the total momentum before annihilation was just about zero. Here’s the everyday payoff: PET medical scanners hunt down tumours by catching these back-to-back pairs!
nye.35: And consider this — particle accelerators run the whole thing in reverse! Slam particles together at huge γ and the kinetic energy transforms into the rest energy of brand-new matter, building particles heavier than the ones that crashed in the first place. These are nuclear transformations, and they’re all governed by E0 = mc² running both directions. Science!
nye.36: Trap Careful now — γmc² is the total energy, not the kinetic energy! The kinetic energy is just the part sitting above the rest energy: Ek = (γ − 1)mc². And don’t you dare reach for the classical ½mv² near c — it just doesn’t cut it; at 0.99c it lowballs the kinetic energy by a factor of about twelve!
nye.37: Key idea Here’s the big one: mass and energy are interchangeable, swapping back and forth at the fixed rate E0 = mc². The Sun turns mass into radiation, annihilation turns mass completely into photon pairs, and accelerators turn kinetic energy into the mass of brand-new particles. Same rule, every time!
nye.38: Connects Remember the radius formula r = mv/qB we used for the synchrotron in topic 5? It comes with a fine-print condition: v << c. But the electrons of the Australian Synchrotron break that condition on purpose, because their energy is thousands of times their rest energy! So the machine’s magnetic fields have to be engineered for the relativistic energies right here on this page, not the classical formula.

munroe.0: If you go fast enough, the ordinary rules for time, length and energy stop giving the right answers. They don't bend a little; they're just wrong. This topic states Einstein’s two postulates, works out exactly how wrong things get by way of the Lorentz factor, and finishes with the one equation everyone has seen on a T-shirt, the claim that mass is energy: E0 = mc².
munroe.1: Classical mechanics quietly assumes four things: time ticks at the same rate for everyone, a ruler is a ruler for everyone, velocities just add up, and kinetic energy ½mv² can climb as high as you like. At the speeds you encounter on a normal Tuesday, all four are correct to within any precision you can measure. The trouble is that they are not actually correct. As you approach c = 3.00 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹, every one of them breaks. You can keep pouring energy into an electron in an accelerator forever, and it will keep speeding up, and it will never reach c. Not slowly. Never.
munroe.2: Light travels at c, which topic 6 established, but classical physics couldn't answer the obvious follow-up question: c relative to what? Everything else is measured relative to something. If you just add velocities the normal way, then an observer racing toward a light source ought to clock the light at more than c. Nineteenth-century physics had a tidy fix: the ether, a hypothetical medium that fills all of space and carries light at c the way air carries sound. If it exists, then Earth, plowing through it on its orbit, ought to feel an “ether wind”, and the measured speed of light should come out different depending on which way you point your instrument.
munroe.3: In 1887 Michelson and Morley decided to measure the ether wind. They took one beam of light, split it down two perpendicular arms, bounced both halves back, and recombined them into an interference pattern. The logic is airtight: if there's a wind, light going along it takes a different time than light going across it, so spinning the whole apparatus around should slide the interference fringes by a specific, calculated, you-can-write-it-down-in-advance amount. They turned it. They turned it at noon and at midnight, in summer and in winter. The fringes moved by zero. This is the null result, and it is one of the most important times in history that nothing happened.
munroe.4: What the null result tells you is precise and a little unsettling: the measured speed of light is the same in every direction, no matter how Earth is moving, and there is no ether to be found. The thing they built the experiment to detect simply isn't there. What's left is the central claim of special relativity, that c is the same for every observer, which Einstein took on board in 1905 not as a result to be derived but as a starting assumption.
munroe.5: Key idea Michelson and Morley predicted that an ether wind would shift the fringes when they rotated their interferometer. They rotated it under every condition they could arrange and the fringes did not shift, at all. That nothing-happened supports the postulate that the speed of light is the same for every observer, no matter how the source is moving and no matter how the observer is moving.
munroe.6: A frame of reference is just the set of coordinates you use to write down where things are, when they happen and how they move. An inertial frame of reference is one that doesn't accelerate: it moves at constant velocity. Constant speed alone won't do it. A car rounding a bend at a perfectly steady speedometer reading is still changing direction, which is an acceleration, so its frame is non-inertial. Special relativity, to be precise, only talks about inertial frames.
munroe.7: The whole theory sits on exactly two postulates:
munroe.8: Postulate 1. The laws of physics are the same in all inertial frames of reference. Put bluntly: no experiment you run inside an inertial frame can tell you how fast the frame itself is moving. There is no test for it, because there is no answer.
munroe.9: Postulate 2. The speed of light in a vacuum, c, comes out to the same value for every observer in an inertial frame. It does not matter how fast the source is moving, and it does not matter how fast you are moving. Everyone measures the same number.
munroe.10: The second postulate is where things stop being reasonable. Classically, if a train moving at 0.50c switches on its headlights, you'd expect someone standing by the track to clock the light at 1.50c. They don't. The driver measures exactly c, and the trackside observer also measures exactly c. The only way two people in relative motion can both get the same speed for the same beam of light is if they disagree about the distances and the times that went into that speed. So they do. Absolute time and absolute length are the casualties; we give them up.
munroe.11: Trap “Inertial” means constant velocity, not constant speed, and the distinction matters. An electron going around the Australian Synchrotron storage ring at a perfectly constant speed is, the entire time, accelerating toward the centre of the ring. That makes its frame non-inertial. The standard time-dilation and length-contraction formulas are written for inertial frames, so the electron does not get to use them as-is.
munroe.12: An event is something that happens at one specific point in space at one specific moment in time. The creation of a muon is an event. So is one tick of a clock. The unsettling part is this: two inertial observers moving relative to each other will not agree on how much time passed between two events, and they will not agree on how long an object is. They are both measuring carefully, and they both get different answers. To make sense of the disagreement, each comparison gets anchored to one privileged measurement, called the proper quantity.
munroe.13: Proper time t0 is the time interval between two events, measured in the one frame of reference where both events happen at the same place. If a traveller’s heart beats twice, both beats happen at the traveller’s chest, which never moves relative to the traveller. So the traveller measures proper time. Everyone else measures a longer interval, a dilated time. Proper length L0 is the length of an object measured in the frame where the object is sitting still. Anyone moving relative to it measures it as shorter, a contracted length, and it is shortened only along the direction of motion. The object is not squashed sideways. Just along the way it is going.
munroe.14: Before you touch a single formula, you answer two questions. First: in which frame do the two events happen at the same point in space? That observer is measuring t0. Second: in which frame is the object, or the distance being crossed, sitting at rest? That observer is measuring L0. Get those two right and the rest is arithmetic. The size of the stretching and the shrinking is set entirely by one number, the Lorentz factor:
munroe.15: For any object with mass, v < c, so γ is never below 1. At rest, γ = 1. At 0.10c it is 1.005, which is so close to 1 that classical mechanics is, for all practical purposes, correct, which is why nobody noticed any of this for a couple of centuries. At 0.50c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.25) = 1.15. At 0.90c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.81) = 2.29. At 0.99c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.9801) = 7.09. As v → c, the denominator heads for zero and γ grows without any upper limit at all, as shown in Figure 11.1.
munroe.16: Trap Both observers are right. The contracted length is a real measurement and the dilated time is a real measurement; neither is an optical illusion or a trick of perspective. Proper time is not automatically the stationary observer’s, and here is the part people get wrong: proper time and proper length usually belong to two different observers in the same problem. A muon is created and decays, and both of those events happen right at the muon, so t0 is measured in the muon’s frame. Meanwhile the atmosphere it falls through is sitting still relative to Earth, so L0 is measured in the Earth frame. Locate the events, then locate the object. Only after that do you get to write down γ.
munroe.17: Key idea Identify the proper quantity first, before any formula goes on the page. t0 belongs to the frame where both events happen at the same point in space; L0 belongs to the frame where the object is at rest. After that, t = γt0 stretches time and L = L0/γ shrinks length, both by the same factor γ ≥ 1. One number, applied two ways.
munroe.18: Three real systems show these predictions actually happening, out in the world, where you can point a detector at them. Each one is worked out the same way: ask the two proper-quantity questions above, then turn the crank.
munroe.19: Cosmic rays hit the upper atmosphere and make muons about 5.0 km above a detector sitting at sea level. The muons head straight down at 0.995c, which is very nearly the fastest anything is allowed to go. A muon that is just sitting still lasts, on average, 2.2 μs before it falls apart. We will pretend each muon flies in a straight line at constant speed until it decays, which is close enough to true.
munroe.20: Let us be precise about this. In one lifetime the muon classically covers 0.995 × 3.00 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹ × 2.2 × 10⁻⁶ s = 6.6 × 10² m, which is roughly an eighth of the way down. So almost none of them should make it to the detector. They make it to the detector anyway, in large numbers, all day long. At 0.995c the Lorentz factor is γ = 1/√(1 − 0.995²) = 10.0, and each frame explains the same stubborn fact in its own way.
munroe.21: In the Earth frame: the muon is born and the muon dies, and both happen at the muon, which is moving relative to Earth. So the 2.2 μs lifetime is the proper time, and to us on the ground it gets stretched. The dilated lifetime is t = γt0 = 10.0 × 2.2 μs = 22 μs. In 22 μs the muon goes 2.985 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹ × 2.2 × 10⁻⁵ s = 6.6 km. That is more than the 5.0 km of atmosphere it needs to cross. The muon arrives.
munroe.22: In the muon’s frame: the muon considers itself to be sitting perfectly still, its 2.2 μs lifetime is the proper time, and the entire atmosphere comes rushing up past it at 0.995c. The atmosphere is at rest relative to Earth, so its 5.0 km thickness is a proper length in the Earth frame; from the muon’s point of view that distance is squashed to L = L0/γ = 5.0 × 10³ ÷ 10.0 = 5.0 × 10² m. That takes 1.7 μs to cross, which is less than one lifetime. The muon arrives.
munroe.23: The two frames cannot agree on a single time or a single distance, and they do not have to. They agree on the one thing you can actually check: the muon arrives. What one frame calls time dilation, the other frame calls length contraction, and they come out to exactly the same answer, as shown in Figure 11.2.
munroe.24: A linear accelerator is 3.2 km long and bolted to the laboratory floor, so the lab measures its proper length. The electrons flying down it at 0.998c (γ = 15.8) do not see 3.2 km. From their point of view the beamline is contracted to L = 3.2 × 10³ ÷ 15.8 = 2.0 × 10² m. The accelerating structures have to be built to push particles that genuinely experience this shorter length, which is the sort of thing that matters when you are building one.
munroe.25: A GPS receiver figures out where it is by timing signals from satellites, which means the satellite clocks have to agree with the clocks on the ground to an almost unreasonable degree. Each satellite orbits at about 3.9 × 10³ m s⁻¹, which gives γ = 1 + 8.5 × 10⁻¹¹. That is a tiny number. But run it for one day (86 400 s) and the orbiting clock, which is keeping proper time up on the satellite, falls behind the ground clocks by about 7.3 μs. In 7.3 μs light travels 2.2 km, so a GPS system that ignored this would tell you that you are kilometres from where you actually are, and it would be more wrong every day. So the clocks are corrected for their orbital speed before anyone is allowed to use them.
munroe.26: Connects The satellites being corrected here are the same circular orbits from topic 2. The orbital radius pins down the speed v, and v pins down γ, so the relativistic correction is determined entirely by the same orbital mechanics that put the satellite up there in the first place.
munroe.27: Relativity saves its strangest result for the end, and it is about energy. An object just sitting there, not moving, is not energetically boring. Its mass is, by itself, a quantity of stored energy. We call this the rest energy:
munroe.28: Once the object starts moving, you add the energy of motion on top. Its total energy is just the rest energy plus the kinetic energy:
munroe.29: At low speeds (γ − 1)mc² works out to be almost exactly ½mv², which is why nobody noticed anything was wrong for a few centuries. Near c the two answers part ways and stop being friends. Take an electron at 0.99c (γ = 7.09). The classical formula confidently reports ½mv² = ½ × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × (2.97 × 10⁸)² = 4.0 × 10⁻¹⁴ J. The correct value is Ek = (7.09 − 1) × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 9.00 × 10¹⁶ = 5.0 × 10⁻¹³ J, which is more than twelve times bigger. As v → c, γ → ∞, so the kinetic energy you need grows without limit. To reach the speed of light you would need infinite energy, and you do not have that, so no massive object ever gets there.
munroe.30: The Sun puts out 3.8 × 10²⁶ W from nuclear fusion in its core. Hydrogen nuclei fuse into helium, and the helium weighs slightly less than the hydrogen you started with. That missing mass is where the light comes from. We can treat the radiated energy as converted mass and work out exactly how much mass disappears each second, as follows.
munroe.31: Formula: in one second the Sun radiates E = 3.8 × 10²⁶ J, so we take E0 = mc² and solve for the mass: m = E0/c².
munroe.33: Answer: m = 4.2 × 10⁹ kg each second. That is about four million tonnes of the Sun turned into sunlight every second. Let's be precise about this: it sounds alarming, but it adds up to well under 0.1% of the Sun's mass across its entire lifetime. The Sun is large.
munroe.34: If you want to convert mass to energy with no leftovers at all, use positron–electron annihilation. The positron is the electron's antiparticle and has exactly the same mass. When the two meet while barely moving, both vanish entirely and 100% of their rest energy comes out as two gamma-ray photons: 2mec² = 2 × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 9.00 × 10¹⁶ = 1.64 × 10⁻¹³ J in total, which is 8.2 × 10⁻¹⁴ J ≈ 0.51 MeV per photon. The two photons head off in exactly opposite directions, because the total momentum beforehand was approximately zero and momentum has to be conserved whether you like it or not. PET medical scanners find tumours by catching these back-to-back pairs.
munroe.35: Particle accelerators run the whole thing backwards: collisions at large γ turn kinetic energy into the rest energy of brand-new matter, manufacturing particles heavier than the ones that crashed together. These are nuclear transformations, and E0 = mc² works in both directions, with no preference for either.
munroe.36: Trap γmc² is the total energy, not the kinetic energy. The kinetic energy is only the part sitting above the rest energy: Ek = (γ − 1)mc². And the classical ½mv² is not a forgivable approximation near c. At 0.99c it undercounts the kinetic energy by a factor of about twelve, which is not a rounding error.
munroe.37: Key idea Mass and energy are the same thing at a fixed exchange rate, E0 = mc². The Sun spends mass and gets radiation, annihilation spends mass and gets photon pairs, and accelerators spend kinetic energy and get the mass of new particles. Same rate every time.
munroe.38: Connects The radius formula r = mv/qB used for the synchrotron in topic 5 comes with the fine print v << c. The electrons in the Australian Synchrotron break that condition deliberately, since their energy runs thousands of times their rest energy, so the machine's magnetic fields have to be built for the relativistic energies on this page and not the classical formula. The fine print matters.

bronowski.0: There comes a moment, as motion quickens toward the speed of light, when the old and trusted rules for time, for length, for energy simply cease to hold; they give the wrong answer. This topic begins with the two postulates Einstein had the courage to set down, follows their consequences through the Lorentz factor, and arrives at last at one of the great unifications of human thought—that mass and energy are the same thing: E0 = mc².
bronowski.1: Classical mechanics was built upon assumptions so natural that for two centuries no one thought to question them: that time flows at one rate for every observer, that a length is the same for all who measure it, that velocities simply add, and that the kinetic energy ½mv² may climb forever without bound. At the speeds of ordinary life these beliefs match what we find, and so they were never doubted. Yet as motion approaches c = 3.00 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹, all four of them break: an accelerator may pour energy into an electron without end, and still the electron never reaches c.
bronowski.2: Light travels at c, as topic 6 established—but relative to what? Classical physics could not say. Its rule for adding velocities demanded that an observer hurrying toward a source of light should measure something greater than c. To rescue the picture, the nineteenth century imagined the ether, a medium filling all of space through which light moved at c; and since the Earth sweeps through this medium on its orbit, there ought to be an “ether wind”, making the speed of light come out differently according to the direction in which one looked.
bronowski.3: In 1887 Michelson and Morley devised an experiment of beautiful simplicity. They divided a single beam of light along two perpendicular arms, sent each to a mirror, and brought the reflections back together to interfere. If an ether wind existed, light running along the wind would take a different time from light crossing it, and turning the apparatus should shift the fringes by an amount they could calculate and watch for. They turned it; they watched. And at every orientation, every hour of the day, every season of the year, the shift was nothing at all: the null result.
bronowski.4: Here was a fact that would not be argued away. The speed of light is the same in every direction, whatever the Earth’s motion, and there is no trace of an ether to be found. It is the firm ground beneath the central claim of special relativity—that c is one and the same for all observers—the claim Einstein had the boldness to take, in 1905, as a postulate.
bronowski.5: Key idea Michelson and Morley reasoned that an ether wind must shift the fringes as their interferometer turned; they looked, and under no condition did the fringes move; and that silence of the instrument upholds the postulate that the speed of light is the same for every observer, whatever the motion of the source or of the one who watches.
bronowski.6: Before we can speak of what is true, we must ask: true for whom? A frame of reference is the vantage from which a man measures positions, times and motions — the scaffold he builds around himself to make sense of the world. An inertial frame of reference is one that does not accelerate; it drifts on at constant velocity. Constant speed alone will not do: a car taking a bend at unchanging speed is forever changing its direction, and so its vantage is non-inertial. It is to the steady, unhurried frames — and to those alone — that special relativity addresses itself.
bronowski.7: On these two foundations the whole edifice is raised:
bronowski.8: Postulate 1. The laws of physics are the same in all inertial frames of reference. No experiment, however cunning, that a man performs within his own steadily moving frame can tell him how fast that frame is truly travelling through the universe — for there is no such truth to be found.
bronowski.9: Postulate 2. The speed of light in a vacuum, c, has the same value for all observers in inertial frames, regardless of the motion of the light source or of the observer. Light keeps its own counsel, indifferent to who chases it and who flees.
bronowski.10: It is the second postulate that demands real courage, for here Einstein breaks with two centuries of common sense. Classically, if a train running at 0.50c turns on its headlights, a man at the trackside should clock the light at 1.50c; the postulate insists instead that the driver and the trackside observer each measure exactly c, neither more nor less. Two men in relative motion can agree upon the speed of one and the same beam only by surrendering their agreement about distances and about the passing of time. And so absolute time and absolute length, the old certainties, are let go — abandoned, as the ether was abandoned, because the evidence will not have them.
bronowski.11: Trap Beware the easy word. “Inertial” means constant velocity, not constant speed — and the difference is everything. An electron circling the Australian Synchrotron storage ring holds its speed unchanged, yet it is forever falling inward toward the centre, accelerating at every instant, so its frame is non-inertial. The familiar formulas of time dilation and length contraction belong to the inertial frames, and we must not press them where they have no right to go.
bronowski.12: An event is the simplest thing the world can offer us: something that happens at one point in space and one moment in time — the birth of a muon, the single tick of a clock. It is the atom of experience. And here is the discovery that cost us our certainty: inertial observers in relative motion do not agree about the interval of time between two such events, nor about the length of an object. Yet the disagreement is not chaos. Each comparison is anchored to one privileged measurement, the proper quantity, and it is to that anchor that we must learn to return.
bronowski.13: Proper time t0 is the interval between two events as measured in the one frame of reference in which both events happen at the same place. Both beats of a traveller’s heart occur at the traveller’s own chest, so it is the traveller who reads proper time; every other observer reads a longer, a dilated time. In the same spirit, proper length L0 is the length of an object measured in the frame in which the object rests. Those who move relative to it find it shorter, contracted — and contracted only along the line of the motion, never across it.
bronowski.14: So before a single formula is written down, the mind must ask two honest questions, the same two that nature itself answers. In which frame do the two events occur at the same point in space — for that observer holds t0? And in which frame does the object, or the distance to be crossed, lie at rest — for that observer holds L0? How far time is stretched, how far length is squeezed, is then fixed by a single number, the Lorentz factor:
bronowski.15: Because v < c for any object with mass, γ can never fall below 1: it is exactly γ = 1 for a body at rest, and even at 0.10c it has crept only to 1.005 — which is why the old mechanics serves us faithfully at the speeds of ordinary life. At 0.50c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.25) = 1.15; at 0.90c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.81) = 2.29; at 0.99c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.9801) = 7.09. And as v → c the denominator slips toward zero and γ climbs without any bound at all, as Figure 11.1 shows — the speed of light standing before matter like a wall it can approach forever and never pass.
bronowski.16: Trap Both observers are right, each within his own frame; a contracted length and a dilated time are genuine measurements of the world, not tricks of perception. Proper time is not the private property of whoever happens to stand still, and proper time and proper length commonly belong to two different observers in one and the same problem. A muon is born and dies at the muon itself, so t0 is read in the muon’s frame of reference; the atmosphere it falls through rests with the Earth, so L0 is read in the Earth frame. First locate the events and the object — only then has one earned the right to assign γ.
bronowski.17: Key idea The proper quantity is named before any formula is touched: t0 belongs to the frame in which both events fall at the same point in space, and L0 belongs to the frame in which the object lies at rest. After that, t = γt0 stretches time and L = L0/γ shrinks length — both by the one same factor γ ≥ 1.
bronowski.18: It is one thing to write down a prediction; it is another to find the world obeying it. Three real systems carry these consequences out of the equations and into experience, and each one yields to the same two questions about the proper quantities that we have learned to ask.
bronowski.19: Consider the muon, a particle that nature manufactures for us without our asking. Cosmic rays striking the upper atmosphere create muons approximately 5.0 km above a sea-level detector, moving downward at 0.995c. A muon at rest has an average lifetime of 2.2 μs. We treat each muon simply: travelling in a straight line at constant speed until it decays.
bronowski.20: Reckon it by the old arithmetic and the muon is doomed. It travels 0.995 × 3.00 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹ × 2.2 × 10⁻⁶ s = 6.6 × 10² m in one lifetime, approximately an eighth of the distance, so almost none should reach the detector. And yet the detectors at sea level record muons in abundance. The discrepancy is not an error of measurement but a summons to think again. At 0.995c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.995²) = 10.0, and each frame, faithful to its own experience, resolves the puzzle in its own way.
bronowski.21: In the Earth frame: the muon's birth and death both happen at the muon itself, which moves relative to Earth, so the 2.2 μs lifetime is the proper time. Time, stretched, grants the muon a longer span of life: t = γt0 = 10.0 × 2.2 μs = 22 μs. In 22 μs the muon travels 2.985 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹ × 2.2 × 10⁻⁵ s = 6.6 km, which exceeds the 5.0 km of atmosphere. The muon arrives.
bronowski.22: In the muon's frame: here the muon is at rest, its 2.2 μs lifetime is its own proper time, and it is the atmosphere that rushes upward past it at 0.995c. The atmosphere is at rest relative to Earth, so 5.0 km is a proper length in the Earth frame; in the muon's frame that depth is contracted to L = L0/γ = 5.0 × 10³ ÷ 10.0 = 5.0 × 10² m, crossed in 1.7 μs, less than a single lifetime. The muon arrives.
bronowski.23: The two observers will never agree about times and distances, and they need not, for they agree on what alone can be witnessed: the muon arrives. What one frame accomplishes by the stretching of time, the other accomplishes by the shrinking of space, as shown in Figure 11.2.
bronowski.24: Where nature provides the muon, human hands build the accelerator. A linear accelerator 3.2 km long is at rest in the laboratory, so the laboratory measures its proper length. Electrons travelling along it at 0.998c (γ = 15.8) measure the beamline contracted to L = 3.2 × 10³ ÷ 15.8 = 2.0 × 10² m, and the engineers must build the accelerating structures to match the shorter length the particles themselves experience.
bronowski.25: And the same truth steadies the satellites overhead. A GPS receiver fixes its position from the travel times of signals broadcast by satellites, so the satellite clocks must keep faith with the ground clocks to an extraordinary precision. Each satellite orbits at approximately 3.9 × 10³ m s⁻¹, giving γ = 1 + 8.5 × 10⁻¹¹. A minute number, yet over one day (86 400 s) the orbiting clock, which records proper time aboard the satellite, falls behind the ground clocks by approximately 7.3 μs. Light travels 2.2 km in 7.3 μs, so a system left uncorrected would wander by kilometres each day; and so the GPS clocks are corrected for their orbital speed, relativity made into a daily instrument of navigation.
bronowski.26: Connects The very satellites corrected here are the circular orbits of topic 2: the orbital radius fixes the speed v, and v in turn fixes γ, so the relativistic correction is decided by the same orbital mechanics that lifted the satellite into the sky.
bronowski.27: We come now to the last and the boldest result of relativity, which is about energy. Look at an object standing still, and you might call it empty, inert, doing nothing. It is not so. Its very mass is a hidden reservoir of energy, the rest energy that it carries simply by existing:
bronowski.28: Set the object in motion, and we do not throw away what it had at rest. The total energy is now what it always held plus what its motion adds — the rest energy together with the kinetic energy:
bronowski.29: At the slow speeds of our ordinary lives, (γ − 1)mc² matches ½mv² almost perfectly, and that is why the old formula of Newton served us faithfully for two centuries. But press toward c and the two part company. Take an electron at 0.99c (γ = 7.09): the classical formula offers ½mv² = ½ × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × (2.97 × 10⁸)² = 4.0 × 10⁻¹⁴ J, yet the true reckoning gives Ek = (7.09 − 1) × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 9.00 × 10¹⁶ = 5.0 × 10⁻¹³ J, more than twelve times larger. And as v → c, γ → ∞: the energy you must pour in climbs without any limit. There is the deep verdict — no object of mass can ever reach the speed of light.
bronowski.30: Lift your eyes to the Sun. It pours out 3.8 × 10²⁶ W, and the source of that flood is fusion in its core, where hydrogen nuclei are forged into helium. The helium weighs a little less than the hydrogen that made it; that missing fraction is not lost but transformed. We treat the radiated energy as mass made into light, and we can ask plainly how much mass the Sun gives up in a single second.
bronowski.31: Formula: in each second the Sun pours out E = 3.8 × 10²⁶ J, so Einstein's relation E0 = mc² turns about to give us m = E0/c².
bronowski.33: Answer: m = 4.2 × 10⁹ kg in every second — some four million tonnes of matter vanishing into sunlight with each tick of the clock. And yet, vast as that sounds, it spends well under 0.1% of the Sun's mass across the whole span of its life.
bronowski.34: There is a place where the conversion of mass into energy is not partial but complete — in positron–electron annihilation. The positron is the mirror-twin of the electron, its antiparticle, carrying the same mass. Let the two meet, barely moving, and both are wholly undone; the whole of their rest energy departs as two gamma-ray photons: 2mec² = 2 × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 9.00 × 10¹⁶ = 1.64 × 10⁻¹³ J in all, which is 8.2 × 10⁻¹⁴ J ≈ 0.51 MeV in each photon. They fly apart in opposite directions, for the momentum they began with was very nearly nothing. It is this back-to-back signature that PET scanners read, and so human ingenuity turns the annihilation of matter into a way of finding tumours within the living body.
bronowski.35: In the great accelerators we run the same exchange the other way. Collisions at large γ take the energy of motion and forge it into the rest energy of new matter, calling into being particles heavier than the ones that struck together. These are transformations of one nuclear thing into another, and in both directions they answer to the one law, E0 = mc².
bronowski.36: Trap γmc² is the whole of the energy, not the kinetic energy alone. The kinetic energy is only that part which rises above the rest energy: Ek = (γ − 1)mc². Do not lean on the classical ½mv² near c; at 0.99c it falls short of the true kinetic energy by a factor of about twelve.
bronowski.37: Key idea Mass and energy are two faces of one thing, freely exchanged at the fixed rate E0 = mc². The Sun turns mass into radiation, annihilation turns mass wholly into pairs of photons, and the accelerators turn the energy of motion into the very mass of new particles.
bronowski.38: Connects The radius formula r = mv/qB that we used for the synchrotron in topic 5 was won under the quiet condition v << c. The electrons of the Australian Synchrotron break that condition deliberately, for their energy is thousands of times their rest energy; so the magnets of the machine must be built for the relativistic energies of this page, and not for the classical formula of an earlier understanding.

einstein.0: When a thing moves so swiftly that it begins to chase a beam of light, I have found that the old, comfortable rules for time, length and energy quietly give us the wrong answer. Here I shall set down the two simple postulates I trusted, follow them to their consequences through the Lorentz factor, and arrive at the modest little equation I have always loved best, that mass is itself a kind of energy: E0 = mc².
einstein.1: The old mechanics, in which I too was raised, takes it for granted that time flows alike for every observer, that a measuring rod is the same length for all, that velocities simply add as one would add coins, and that the kinetic energy ½mv² may climb forever. At the gentle speeds of trains and horses these beliefs sit happily with experiment. But let me imagine a speed creeping up toward c = 3.00 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹, and all four beliefs betray us: an accelerator may pour energy into an electron without end, and still the electron never quite catches up to c.
einstein.2: Light travels at c, as we saw in topic 6, yet the old physics could never tell me relative to what. If I imagine running toward a lamp, the rule for adding velocities insists I ought to measure something greater than c. The physicists of the nineteenth century answered with the ether, an invisible medium in which light was supposed to swim at c; and since the Earth sails through this ether on its orbit, there ought to be an “ether wind”, so that the measured speed of light would lean one way or another depending on which direction one looked.
einstein.3: In 1887 Michelson and Morley took a single beam of light, sent it down two arms set at right angles, and let the two reflections meet again to weave an interference pattern. If an ether wind were truly blowing, light swimming along the wind would take a little longer than light crossing it, so turning the apparatus ought to make the fringes drift by a definite, measurable amount. Yet the drift was nothing, at every angle, every hour of the day, every season of the year: the null result.
einstein.4: This nothing tells us a great deal. It shows that the speed of light comes out the same in every direction, heedless of how the Earth is rushing along, and it leaves the ether without a shred of evidence to stand on. To me this is the quiet confirmation of the heart of special relativity, that c is one and the same for all observers, which in 1905 I simply took as a postulate and built upon.
einstein.5: Key idea Michelson and Morley foresaw that an ether wind would set the fringes shifting as their interferometer turned; they looked, and saw no shift under any conditions; and this nothing supports the postulate I have always trusted, that the speed of light is the same for all observers, whatever the motion of the source or of the one who watches.
einstein.6: Before we can speak of anything, we must say from where we are looking. A frame of reference is simply this: the set of coordinates from which one measures positions, times and motions. I like to picture an observer with his rulers and his clock. If this observer does not accelerate — if he drifts along at constant velocity — I call his an inertial frame of reference. Constant speed alone will not do, and here one must be careful: imagine a car taking a bend at a steady speed. Its direction is forever changing, so its frame is non-inertial. It is to the quiet, unaccelerated frames that special relativity speaks.
einstein.7: The whole edifice rests on two simple postulates, and I have always preferred to begin from the simplest possible ideas:
einstein.8: Postulate 1. The laws of physics are the same in every inertial frame of reference. I find it helpful to imagine myself sealed in a railway carriage with the blinds drawn: no experiment I perform inside can ever tell me how fast my carriage is truly moving, or whether it moves at all.
einstein.9: Postulate 2. The speed of light in a vacuum, c, has the very same value for every observer in an inertial frame, no matter how the light source moves, and no matter how the observer moves. Nature, it seems, holds this one number sacred.
einstein.10: It is this second postulate that parts company with the old physics, and it cost me much thought. Picture a train rushing along at 0.50c which switches on its headlights. The old reckoning would have a man by the track measure that light at 1.50c; yet the postulate insists that the driver and the trackside man each find exactly c. I puzzled over this for a long time, and the only way I could reconcile it was this: two observers in relative motion can agree upon the speed of one and the same beam only if they quietly disagree about distances and about intervals of time. So absolute time and absolute length, which had seemed so solid, must be let go.
einstein.11: Trap “Inertial” means constant velocity, not constant speed — a distinction worth keeping firmly in mind. Picture an electron circling the storage ring of the Australian Synchrotron at constant speed; all the while it is accelerating inward toward the centre, so its frame is non-inertial. The familiar formulas for time dilation and length contraction belong to the inertial frames alone.
einstein.12: I have always found it helpful to begin with a single happening. An event is simply something that occurs at one point in space at one moment in time — the birth of a muon, say, or a single tick of a clock. Now picture two such events, and two observers gliding past one another. To my own quiet astonishment, they will not agree about the time between the events, nor about the length of an object. Yet there is always one observer for whom the measurement is, so to speak, the natural one, and this privileged value I call the proper quantity.
einstein.13: Proper time t0 is the time between two events as read in the frame where both events happen at the very same place. I like to imagine a traveller’s heart: both beats fall at the traveller’s own chest, so it is the traveller who carries the proper time — while everyone else, watching them rush past, finds a longer, dilated time. In the same gentle spirit, proper length L0 is the length of a thing measured where it sits at rest. Anyone moving past it will see a shorter, contracted length — and only along the direction of the motion, never across it.
