einstein.14: I have learned never to reach for a formula first. Every relativity problem opens with two simple questions: in which frame do the two events occur at the same point in space (there sits t0), and in which frame does the object, or the distance to be crossed, rest quietly (there sits L0)? Once these are settled, the size of the stretching and the shrinking is fixed by what I call the Lorentz factor:
einstein.15: Since nothing with mass can quite reach light, v < c always, and so γ can never dip below 1. At rest γ = 1, and even at 0.10c it is a mere 1.005 — which is why, for the ordinary speeds of daily life, the old mechanics serves us perfectly well. But press on, and the factor begins to climb: at 0.50c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.25) = 1.15; at 0.90c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.81) = 2.29; at 0.99c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.9801) = 7.09. As v → c the denominator slips toward zero and γ runs away without bound, as shown in Figure 11.1.
einstein.16: Trap I must insist: each observer’s measurements are perfectly correct within their own frame. A contracted length and a dilated time are not tricks of the eye but honest measurements. Proper time does not belong to the stationary observer by some default, and in a single problem proper time and proper length usually belong to two different observers. Picture a muon: its creation and its decay both happen at the muon, so t0 lives in the muon’s frame of reference; meanwhile the atmosphere it falls through rests with the Earth, so L0 lives in the Earth frame. First locate the events and the object — only then assign γ.
einstein.17: Key idea I name the proper quantity before I touch any formula: t0 belongs to the frame in which both events fall at the same point in space, and L0 belongs to the frame in which the object rests. Then time is stretched, t = γt0, and length is shrunk, L = L0/γ — both by the very same factor γ ≥ 1. Nature, I have always felt, is wonderfully economical that way.
einstein.18: I have always trusted nature to settle these questions for me, so let me show you three real systems where these predictions step out of the imagination and into the world; each one I examine by asking the same two quiet questions about proper quantities.
einstein.19: Picture cosmic rays striking the upper atmosphere: they create muons roughly 5.0 km above a detector resting at sea level, falling straight down at 0.995c. A muon sitting at rest lives, on average, only 2.2 μs before it decays. I imagine each one travelling in a straight line at constant speed until that short life runs out.
einstein.20: Reasoning in the old classical way, I follow the muon for one lifetime: it travels 0.995 × 3.00 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹ × 2.2 × 10⁻⁶ s = 6.6 × 10² m, only about an eighth of the journey, so almost none ought to survive the fall. And yet the detectors at sea level catch muons in abundance. Nature has clearly outwitted the classical picture. At 0.995c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.995²) = 10.0, and each frame, I find to my quiet delight, tells the same story in its own way.
einstein.21: In the Earth frame: I stand on the ground and watch. The muon's birth and death both happen at the muon itself, which is rushing past me, so its 2.2 μs lifetime is the proper time. To me its clock runs slow, and the stretched lifetime is t = γt0 = 10.0 × 2.2 μs = 22 μs. In those 22 μs the muon covers 2.985 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹ × 2.2 × 10⁻⁵ s = 6.6 km, more than the 5.0 km of atmosphere it must cross. The muon arrives.
einstein.22: In the muon's frame: now let me ride along beside the muon. Here it is at rest, its own 2.2 μs lifetime is the proper time, and it is the atmosphere that comes rushing upward past me at 0.995c. That atmosphere is at rest relative to Earth, so its 5.0 km thickness is a proper length in the Earth frame; from where I sit on the muon, it is squeezed to L = L0/γ = 5.0 × 10³ ÷ 10.0 = 5.0 × 10² m, swept past in only 1.7 μs, less than a single lifetime. The muon arrives.
einstein.23: The two pictures quarrel about times and distances, yet they shake hands on the one thing anyone can see: the muon arrives. What looks like slowed time from one seat is exactly the shortened distance seen from the other, as shown in Figure 11.2. This pleasing agreement is just what I had hoped nature would say.
einstein.24: Imagine a linear accelerator 3.2 km long, lying still in the laboratory, so the laboratory reads off its proper length. But the electrons streaming down it at 0.998c (γ = 15.8) see the beamline shrunk to L = 3.2 × 10³ ÷ 15.8 = 2.0 × 10² m, and the accelerating structures must be built for particles that genuinely live within this shorter length.
einstein.25: Picture a GPS receiver finding where it is from the travel times of timing signals sent down from satellites; for this the clocks aloft must agree with the clocks below to an almost unreasonable precision. Each satellite circles at about 3.9 × 10³ m s⁻¹, which gives γ = 1 + 8.5 × 10⁻¹¹. A tiny thing, yet over one day (86 400 s) the orbiting clock, which keeps proper time aboard its own satellite, falls behind the ground clocks by roughly 7.3 μs. In 7.3 μs light runs 2.2 km, so left uncorrected the system would wander off by kilometres each day; this is why the GPS clocks are corrected for their orbital speed.
einstein.26: Connects The very satellites corrected here are the circular orbits I imagined in topic 2: the orbital radius fixes the speed v, and v in turn fixes γ, so the relativistic correction is governed by the same orbital mechanics that lifted the satellite into the sky in the first place.
einstein.27: The last result of my theory concerns energy, and I confess it is the one that still delights me most. I had always supposed that a body sitting quietly at rest must be empty of energy; but nature taught me otherwise. Its very mass is a hidden store of energy, what I came to call the rest energy:
einstein.28: And once the body is set in motion, I picture its total energy simply as the rest energy it already carried, with the kinetic energy of the motion added on top:
einstein.29: At gentle speeds I find that (γ − 1)mc² agrees nicely with the familiar ½mv², which is why the old formula has served so faithfully in everyday life. But as one approaches c the two part ways. Imagine an electron hurried along to 0.99c (γ = 7.09): the classical formula offers ½mv² = ½ × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × (2.97 × 10⁸)² = 4.0 × 10⁻¹⁴ J, while the true value is Ek = (7.09 − 1) × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 9.00 × 10¹⁶ = 5.0 × 10⁻¹³ J, more than twelve times as much. And as v → c, γ → ∞: the energy one must pour in grows without any limit. So nature politely forbids any massive body from ever quite reaching the speed of light.
einstein.30: I like to think of our own Sun, pouring out 3.8 × 10²⁶ W from the fusion quietly burning in its core. There hydrogen nuclei join into helium, and the products weigh a little less than what went in. That radiated energy I picture simply as mass that has changed its form, and the mass spent each second may be reckoned as follows.
einstein.31: Formula: in each second the Sun sends out E = 3.8 × 10²⁶ J, and so I have only to turn E0 = mc² around to read m = E0/c².
einstein.33: Answer: m = 4.2 × 10⁹ kg in every second, which is to say about four million tonnes of the Sun's own substance carried off as sunlight, each second. And yet, to my quiet satisfaction, this spends well under 0.1% of the Sun's mass across the whole of its long life.
einstein.34: The conversion of mass into energy is complete, holding nothing back, in positron–electron annihilation. The positron is the electron's antiparticle and carries the very same mass. When the two come together almost at rest, both vanish, and their whole rest energy departs as two gamma-ray photons: 2mec² = 2 × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 9.00 × 10¹⁶ = 1.64 × 10⁻¹³ J altogether, which is 8.2 × 10⁻¹⁴ J ≈ 0.51 MeV for each photon. I picture them flying off in opposite directions, as they must, since the momentum before the meeting was very nearly nothing. The PET scanners of the physicians find tumours by catching these back-to-back pairs.
einstein.35: The great accelerators run the same exchange backwards: in collisions at large γ the kinetic energy is poured into the rest energy of fresh matter, and particles heavier than the ones that struck together come into being. These are transformations of the nucleus, and in both directions they are governed by the same simple E0 = mc².
einstein.36: Trap Be careful: γmc² is the total energy, and not the kinetic energy. The kinetic energy is only the part standing above the rest energy: Ek = (γ − 1)mc². And the old ½mv² simply will not do as an approximation near c; at 0.99c it falls short of the true kinetic energy by a factor of about twelve.
einstein.37: Key idea Mass and energy are, in truth, two faces of one thing, exchanged at the fixed rate E0 = mc². The Sun turns mass into radiation, annihilation turns mass wholly into pairs of photons, and the accelerators turn kinetic energy into the mass of new particles.
einstein.38: Connects The radius formula r = mv/qB used for the synchrotron in topic 5 quietly carries the condition v << c. The electrons of the Australian Synchrotron are made, by design, to disobey it, for their energy is thousands of times their rest energy; and so the magnetic fields of the machine must be built for the relativistic energies of this page, and not for the classical formula.

noether.0: Ask, as speeds approach the speed of light, what is preserved and what must yield. The classical accounting of time, length and energy gives the wrong answer, because it holds the wrong things fixed. This topic begins from Einstein’s two postulates, follows their consequences through the invariant they protect by way of the Lorentz factor, and arrives at the most beautiful equivalence of all, mass and energy as one quantity wearing two names: E0 = mc².
noether.1: Classical mechanics quietly assumes a great deal is invariant: that time flows at one rate for every observer, that lengths are the same for all, that velocities add without subtlety, and that the kinetic energy ½mv² may grow without limit. At everyday speeds these assumed invariants survive every experiment. But as speeds approach c = 3.00 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹, all four break, for they were never the quantities Nature truly preserves: an accelerator may pour energy into an electron without end, yet the electron’s speed never reaches c.
noether.2: Light travels at c, as established in topic 6, but classical physics could not say: the same relative to what? If velocities simply add, then an observer moving toward a source ought to measure something greater than c, and the constancy is lost. Nineteenth-century physics sought to anchor it in a medium, the ether, through which light propagates at c; and so Earth’s orbital motion through this medium should betray itself as an “ether wind”, leaving the measured speed of light no longer the same in every direction.
noether.3: In 1887 Michelson and Morley set out to catch that lost symmetry in the act of breaking. They split one light beam along two perpendicular interferometer arms and recombined the reflections into an interference pattern. If an ether wind existed, light along the wind would take a different time from light across it, so rotating the apparatus should shift the fringes by a definite, predictable amount. Yet at every orientation, every time of day, every season, the shift was zero: the null result, the symmetry refusing to break.
noether.4: The null result tells us what is preserved: the measured speed of light is the same in all directions, untouched by Earth’s motion, and the ether leaves no trace at all. This is the very invariance at the heart of special relativity, that c is the same for all observers, which Einstein in 1905 elevated from accident to postulate.
noether.5: Key idea Michelson and Morley predicted that an ether wind would break the symmetry, shifting the fringes as their interferometer rotated; they found the symmetry intact, no shift under any conditions. The null result upholds the postulate that the speed of light is the one quantity all observers agree on, preserved regardless of the motion of source or observer.
noether.6: Before we ask what bends, let us fix where we stand. A frame of reference is the set of coordinates from which positions, times and motions are measured. An inertial frame of reference is the privileged kind that does not accelerate; it moves at constant velocity, gliding without disturbance. Constant speed alone will not do, for it is direction too that must be preserved: a car rounding a bend at steady speed changes its heading, so its frame is non-inertial. Special relativity speaks only of these unaccelerated frames, where the architecture stays unbroken.
noether.7: The whole structure rests on two postulates, two statements about what stays the same:
noether.8: Postulate 1. The laws of physics are the same in all inertial frames of reference. This is the deepest invariance of all: the laws are preserved from frame to frame, and so no experiment performed within an inertial frame can reveal the frame's absolute motion. There is no privileged rest; only the laws are absolute.
noether.9: Postulate 2. Now name the quantity that every observer must agree upon. The speed of light in a vacuum, c, has the same value for all observers in inertial frames, regardless of the motion of the light source or of the observer. This is the one invariant that all frames hold in common.
noether.10: The second postulate is where classical physics gives way. Classically we simply add velocities: if a train moving at 0.50c turns on its headlights, a trackside observer expects to measure the light at 1.50c. But the postulate refuses this; it insists that the driver and the trackside observer each measure exactly c. Ask now what must yield so that this agreement can hold. Two observers in relative motion can agree on the speed of the same light beam only if they disagree about distances and time intervals. To preserve c, absolute time and absolute length must be surrendered. They are not invariant; c is. That is the trade the theory makes.
noether.11: Trap “Inertial” means constant velocity, not constant speed; it is the whole velocity vector, direction included, that must be preserved. An electron circulating in the Australian Synchrotron storage ring at constant speed is forever accelerating toward the centre, its direction never held fixed, so its frame is non-inertial. The standard time-dilation and length-contraction formulas apply only to inertial frames.
noether.12: An event is something that happens at a particular point in space at a particular time, such as the creation of a muon or one tick of a clock. Here is the beautiful tension: inertial observers in relative motion disagree about the time interval between two events and about the length of an object. Ask not what they share but what each holds privately. Each comparison is anchored by one favoured measurement, the proper quantity, against which every other observer’s reading is reckoned.
noether.13: Proper time t0 is the time interval between two events measured in the frame of reference in which both events occur at the same point in space. Both beats of a traveller’s heart occur at the traveller’s chest, so the traveller alone measures proper time; every other observer measures a longer, dilated time. The pattern repeats for distance. Proper length L0 is the length of an object measured in the frame of reference in which the object is at rest. Observers moving relative to the object measure a shorter, contracted length, contracted only along the direction of relative motion, the direction in which the symmetry is broken.
noether.14: Every relativity problem begins by asking where the privileged quantities live, before any formula is applied: in which frame do the two events occur at the same point in space (that observer measures t0), and in which frame is the object, or the distance being traversed, at rest (that observer measures L0)? Once the proper quantities are located, the amount of dilation and contraction is fixed by a single structural number, the Lorentz factor:
noether.15: Since v < c for any massive object, γ is never less than 1: γ = 1 at rest, and at 0.10c it is still only 1.005, so at everyday speeds the structure is almost unchanged and classical mechanics is sufficient. At 0.50c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.25) = 1.15; at 0.90c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.81) = 2.29; at 0.99c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.9801) = 7.09. As v → c the denominator approaches zero and γ increases without bound, as shown in Figure 11.1 — the price the universe pays to keep c invariant.
noether.16: Trap Both observers’ measurements are correct in their own frames; a contracted length and a dilated time are real measurements, not illusions, and the disagreement is exactly what preserves the one quantity all observers share. Proper time does not belong to the stationary observer by default, and proper time and proper length usually belong to different observers in the same problem: a muon’s creation and decay both occur at the muon, so t0 is measured in the muon’s frame of reference, while the atmosphere through which it falls is at rest relative to Earth, so L0 is measured in the Earth frame. Locate where each invariant anchor lives first; only then is γ assigned.
noether.17: Key idea Ask first what is preserved: the proper quantity is identified before any formula is applied. t0 belongs to the frame in which both events occur at the same point in space, and L0 belongs to the frame in which the object is at rest. Then the same structural factor acts on both — t = γt0 stretches time and L = L0/γ shrinks length, by the same γ ≥ 1, so that every observer agrees on c.
noether.18: Three real systems let us watch these predictions at work; in each, the same two questions guide us — where do both events meet at one point in space, and what stands at rest — for those are the questions that locate the proper quantities everything else is built upon.
noether.19: Cosmic rays striking the upper atmosphere create muons approximately 5.0 km above a sea-level detector, moving downward at 0.995c. A muon at rest carries an average lifetime of 2.2 μs — its own invariant clock. Each muon is modelled as travelling in a straight line at constant speed until it decays.
noether.20: Reason classically and the muon travels 0.995 × 3.00 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹ × 2.2 × 10⁻⁶ s = 6.6 × 10² m in one lifetime, barely an eighth of the journey, so almost none should survive the fall. Yet detectors at sea level record muons in abundance. The reconciliation lies in what each frame must preserve: at 0.995c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.995²) = 10.0, and each observer keeps the agreed arrival in its own way.
noether.21: In the Earth frame: the muon’s creation and decay both happen at the muon, which moves relative to Earth, so the 2.2 μs lifetime is the proper time — the invariant the muon carries with it. Earth, watching that clock fly past, must see it stretched: t = γt0 = 10.0 × 2.2 μs = 22 μs. In 22 μs the muon travels 2.985 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹ × 2.2 × 10⁻⁵ s = 6.6 km, which exceeds the 5.0 km of atmosphere. The muon arrives.
noether.22: In the muon’s frame: the muon rests, its 2.2 μs lifetime is again the proper time it preserves, and now the atmosphere streams upward past it at 0.995c. The atmosphere is at rest relative to Earth, so 5.0 km is the proper length there; seen from the muon, that same span is contracted to L = L0/γ = 5.0 × 10³ ÷ 10.0 = 5.0 × 10² m, crossed in 1.7 μs — less than one lifetime. The muon arrives.
noether.23: The two frames disagree about times and disagree about distances, yet they conspire to preserve the one thing that cannot vary: the muon arrives. What one frame calls time dilation, the other calls length contraction — two faces of a single invariance, as shown in Figure 11.2.
noether.24: A linear accelerator 3.2 km long rests in the laboratory, so the laboratory holds its proper length. The electrons sweeping along it at 0.998c (γ = 15.8) find the beamline contracted to L = 3.2 × 10³ ÷ 15.8 = 2.0 × 10² m, and the accelerating structures must be engineered to serve particles for whom that shorter length is the real one.
noether.25: A GPS receiver fixes its position from the travel times of timing signals broadcast by satellites, so satellite clocks and ground clocks must agree to extraordinary precision — and that agreement is exactly what relativity threatens. Each satellite orbits at approximately 3.9 × 10³ m s⁻¹, giving γ = 1 + 8.5 × 10⁻¹¹. A tiny departure from unity, yet over one day (86 400 s) the orbiting clock, which keeps proper time aboard the satellite, falls behind ground clocks by approximately 7.3 μs. Light travels 2.2 km in 7.3 μs, so an uncorrected system would drift by kilometres each day; the clocks are corrected for their orbital speed so that the synchronisation we depend on is preserved.
noether.26: Connects The satellites corrected here are the same circular orbits of topic 2: the orbital radius fixes the speed v, and v fixes γ, so the relativistic correction is set by the very orbital mechanics that placed the satellite in orbit.
noether.27: The last gift of relativity is about energy, and it follows from asking what stays the same when nothing moves. A stationary object is not energetically empty: held in its mass, unchanged whether anyone watches or not, is a store of energy we call the rest energy:
noether.28: Set the object in motion and the total energy is what is now conserved across the whole — the rest energy preserved at the core, with the kinetic energy added on top:
noether.29: At low speeds (γ − 1)mc² agrees with ½mv², and this agreement is why the classical formula keeps faith with experiment at everyday speeds. Near c the two partings of the same quantity diverge. For an electron at 0.99c (γ = 7.09), the classical formula gives ½mv² = ½ × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × (2.97 × 10⁸)² = 4.0 × 10⁻¹⁴ J, while Ek = (7.09 − 1) × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 9.00 × 10¹⁶ = 5.0 × 10⁻¹³ J, more than twelve times larger. As v → c, γ → ∞: the energy demanded grows without bound, and so c remains the one speed no massive object can reach — the boundary that every frame agrees upon.
noether.30: The Sun radiates 3.8 × 10²⁶ W from nuclear fusion in its core. Hydrogen nuclei fuse into helium, and here is the lovely bookkeeping: the products carry slightly less mass than the reactants, yet nothing is truly lost. What disappears as mass reappears as radiated energy, the total preserved. The mass converted each second is calculated as follows.
noether.31: Formula: each second the Sun radiates E = 3.8 × 10²⁶ J, so the conserved equivalence E0 = mc² rearranges to m = E0/c².
noether.33: Answer: m = 4.2 × 10⁹ kg each second — about four million tonnes of mass passing into sunlight every second, the ledger always balancing. And yet this consumes well under 0.1% of the Sun’s mass over its lifetime.
noether.34: The conversion runs to completion in positron–electron annihilation. The positron is the antiparticle of the electron and shares its mass exactly. When the two meet at negligible speed, both vanish and the whole of their rest energy emerges as two gamma-ray photons — nothing destroyed, only transformed: 2mec² = 2 × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 9.00 × 10¹⁶ = 1.64 × 10⁻¹³ J in total, which is 8.2 × 10⁻¹⁴ J ≈ 0.51 MeV per photon. The photons fly in opposite directions precisely because the total momentum before annihilation was approximately zero, and that quantity, like the energy, is preserved through the change. PET medical scanners locate tumours by detecting these back-to-back pairs.
noether.35: Particle accelerators read the same conservation law from right to left: collisions at large γ pour kinetic energy into the rest energy of new matter, creating particles more massive than the ones that collided. These are nuclear transformations, and what is invariant through every one of them is E0 = mc², honoured equally in both directions.
noether.36: Trap γmc² is the total energy, the whole that is conserved, not the kinetic energy alone. The kinetic energy is only the part standing above the rest energy: Ek = (γ − 1)mc². The classical ½mv² is not an acceptable approximation near c; at 0.99c it understates the kinetic energy by a factor of about twelve.
noether.37: Key idea Mass and energy are two faces of one conserved quantity, exchanged at the fixed rate E0 = mc². The Sun converts mass to radiation, annihilation converts mass entirely to photon pairs, and accelerators convert kinetic energy into the mass of new particles — in every case the total preserved while the form is free to change.
noether.38: Connects The radius formula r = mv/qB used for the synchrotron in topic 5 carries the condition v << c. The electrons of the Australian Synchrotron do not satisfy this condition by design, since their energy is thousands of times their rest energy, so the magnetic fields of the machine must be engineered for the relativistic energies on this page rather than the classical formula.

mack.0: Push something fast enough — really fast, a respectable fraction of the speed of light — and the comfortable old rules for time, length and energy quietly start handing you wrong answers. This topic lays out Einstein’s two postulates, pins down exactly how much they bend reality through the Lorentz factor, and lands on the punchline that should not be true and yet is: mass and energy are the same currency, E0 = mc².
mack.1: Classical mechanics makes four cosy assumptions: that time ticks at the same rate for everyone, that a metre is a metre for all observers, that velocities simply add up the way you’d expect, and that kinetic energy ½mv² can climb as high as you like. At the speeds of everyday life, all four behave impeccably — which is exactly why nobody noticed they were lying. As you approach c = 3.00 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹, every one of them breaks: you can pour energy into an electron in an accelerator forever, and its speed will creep toward c and never, ever arrive.
mack.2: Light travels at c, as topic 6 established — but here’s the awkward question classical physics couldn’t answer: c relative to what? Plain velocity addition insists that if you sprint toward a light source, you ought to clock the light coming in faster than c. So nineteenth-century physics invented a stage for light to perform on: the ether, a hypothetical medium carrying light along at c. And if the ether were real, Earth’s motion around its orbit should whip up an “ether wind”, and the measured speed of light would have to depend on which way you looked.
mack.3: In 1887 Michelson and Morley built the experiment to catch that wind in the act. They split a single light beam down two perpendicular interferometer arms, bounced the halves back, and recombined them into an interference pattern. If an ether wind existed, light running along the wind would take a different time from light cutting across it, so rotating the whole contraption should slide the fringes by a crisp, predictable, measurable amount. Every orientation, every hour of the day, every season of the year: the fringes did not budge. The famous null result.
mack.4: Nothing moved — and that nothing is the whole point. The measured speed of light is the same in every direction, no matter how Earth is hurtling through space, and the ether left no fingerprint anywhere. It is precisely the evidence special relativity needs: that c is the same for every observer, the bold idea Einstein simply took as a starting assumption in 1905.
mack.5: Key idea Michelson and Morley bet that an ether wind would shove their interference fringes sideways as the interferometer turned; the fringes refused to move, under any conditions they could throw at it. That stubborn null result backs the postulate that the speed of light is the same for all observers, indifferent to the motion of the source or the observer.
mack.6: First, the bookkeeping. A frame of reference is just the set of coordinates you use to pin down where and when things happen. An inertial frame of reference is one that doesn’t accelerate; it cruises along at constant velocity. And here’s the catch that trips people up: constant speed isn’t enough. A car taking a bend at a steady speedometer reading is still swinging its direction around, so its frame is non-inertial. Special relativity only plays nicely with inertial frames.
mack.7: The whole edifice balances on two postulates:
mack.8: Postulate 1. The laws of physics are the same in every inertial frame of reference. No experiment you run inside an inertial frame can tell you how fast that frame is “really” moving, because there is no such thing as really moving.
mack.9: Postulate 2. The speed of light in a vacuum, c, comes out the same for every observer in an inertial frame, no matter how the light source is moving and no matter how the observer is moving.
mack.10: That second one is the bomb under classical physics. Classically, if a train doing 0.50c flicks on its headlights, you’d expect someone at the trackside to clock the light at 1.50c — just add the speeds, obviously. The postulate flatly refuses: the driver and the trackside observer each measure exactly c. The only way two observers in relative motion can agree on the speed of one and the same beam is if they quietly disagree about distances and time intervals. So that’s the price: absolute time and absolute length get thrown overboard.
mack.11: Trap “Inertial” means constant velocity, not constant speed — and yes, the difference will bite you. An electron whipping around the Australian Synchrotron storage ring at constant speed is accelerating the entire time, forever falling toward the centre, so its frame is non-inertial. The standard time-dilation and length-contraction formulas only apply to inertial frames.
mack.12: An event is just something that happens at one point in space at one moment in time — a muon springing into existence, a clock ticking once. Sounds harmless. But here’s the unsettling part: inertial observers in relative motion will flatly disagree about how much time passed between two events, and about how long an object is. Everyone gets a different answer, and everyone is right. What keeps the whole thing from descending into chaos is one privileged measurement per comparison, the proper quantity.
mack.13: Proper time t0 is the time interval between two events measured in the frame of reference where both events happen at the same point in space. Both beats of a traveller’s heart land at the traveller’s chest, so the traveller is the one who measures proper time; everybody else clocks a longer, dilated time. Proper length L0 is the length of an object measured in the frame where the object is sitting still. Anyone moving past it measures a shorter, contracted length — and here’s the catch worth remembering, the squashing happens only along the direction of relative motion.
mack.14: Every relativity problem starts with the same two questions, asked before you so much as glance at a formula: in which frame do the two events happen at the same point in space (that observer is holding t0), and in which frame is the object, or the distance being crossed, at rest (that observer is holding L0)? Once you’ve pinned those down, the size of the stretching and squashing is dialled in by the Lorentz factor:
mack.15: Because v < c for anything with mass, γ is never allowed to dip below 1: it sits at γ = 1 at rest, and even at 0.10c it has crept up to a mere 1.005 — which is exactly why classical mechanics gets away with it at everyday speeds. At 0.50c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.25) = 1.15; at 0.90c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.81) = 2.29; at 0.99c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.9801) = 7.09. Push v → c and the denominator slides toward zero, sending γ climbing without any ceiling at all, as shown in Figure 11.1.
mack.16: Trap Both observers’ measurements are genuinely correct in their own frames — a contracted length and a dilated time are real readings, not optical tricks the universe is playing on you. Proper time does not automatically belong to whoever is standing still, and the kicker is that proper time and proper length usually belong to two different observers in the same problem: a muon’s creation and decay both happen right at the muon, so t0 lives in the muon’s frame of reference, while the atmosphere it’s plunging through is at rest relative to Earth, so L0 lives in the Earth frame. Locate the events and the object first; only then do you get to hand out γ.
mack.17: Key idea Identify the proper quantity before any formula gets touched: t0 belongs to the frame in which both events happen at the same point in space, and L0 belongs to the frame in which the object is at rest. After that the rest is bookkeeping — t = γt0 stretches time and L = L0/γ shrinks length, both by the very same factor γ ≥ 1.
mack.18: Here is where the abstract machinery earns its keep. Three real systems put these predictions to work, and each one yields to exactly the same two proper-quantity questions we just sharpened above.
mack.19: Start with muons. Cosmic rays slam into the upper atmosphere and manufacture them roughly 5.0 km above a detector sitting at sea level, all of them hurtling downward at 0.995c. Sit a muon still and it survives, on average, a mere 2.2 μs. We model each one as flying in a straight line at constant speed right up until it decays.
mack.20: Run the classical numbers and the muon is doomed: 0.995 × 3.00 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹ × 2.2 × 10⁻⁶ s = 6.6 × 10² m in a lifetime, about an eighth of the way down, so essentially none of them ought to survive the trip. And yet detectors at sea level are practically drowning in muons. The thing that should not work, does. At 0.995c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.995²) = 10.0, and each frame talks itself into the same answer by a completely different route.
mack.21: In the Earth frame: the muon is born and dies at the muon, which is tearing along relative to us, so that 2.2 μs lifetime is the proper time. Time dilation stretches it: t = γt0 = 10.0 × 2.2 μs = 22 μs. In 22 μs the muon covers 2.985 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹ × 2.2 × 10⁻⁵ s = 6.6 km, comfortably more than the 5.0 km of air in its way. The muon arrives.
mack.22: In the muon’s frame: now the muon is the one sitting still, its 2.2 μs lifetime is the proper time, and it is the atmosphere that comes rushing up to meet it at 0.995c. That atmosphere is at rest relative to Earth, so 5.0 km is a proper length in the Earth frame; from the muon’s point of view it shrinks to L = L0/γ = 5.0 × 10³ ÷ 10.0 = 5.0 × 10² m, a journey it polishes off in 1.7 μs, well inside one lifetime. The muon arrives.
mack.23: The two frames squabble endlessly about times and distances, yet they shake hands on the one thing anyone can actually check: the muon arrives. One frame’s stretched time is the other frame’s squashed length, two descriptions of the same stubborn fact, as shown in Figure 11.2.
mack.24: Now flip it around. A linear accelerator 3.2 km long sits at rest in the laboratory, so the lab gets to call that its proper length. But ride along with an electron streaking down it at 0.998c (γ = 15.8) and the whole beamline telescopes down to L = 3.2 × 10³ ÷ 15.8 = 2.0 × 10² m. The engineers have no choice but to build the accelerating structures for particles that genuinely experience this shorter machine.
mack.25: And it is in your pocket. A GPS receiver works out where it is from the travel times of timing signals beamed down by satellites, which means the satellite clocks have to keep faith with ground clocks to absurd precision. Each satellite orbits at roughly 3.9 × 10³ m s⁻¹, giving γ = 1 + 8.5 × 10⁻¹¹. Vanishingly small, sure, but let it run for a day (86 400 s) and the orbiting clock, ticking off proper time up there, slips behind the ground by about 7.3 μs. Light covers 2.2 km in 7.3 μs, so left uncorrected the system would wander off by kilometres a day. The only reason your phone knows which street you are on is that the GPS clocks are corrected for their orbital speed.
mack.26: Connects The satellites doing the correcting here are exactly the circular orbits of topic 2: the orbital radius pins down the speed v, and v pins down γ, so the relativistic fudge is dictated by the very same orbital mechanics that flung the satellite up there in the first place.
mack.27: Relativity saves its best trick for the energy ledger. Sit a stationary object on the bench and you might assume it is doing nothing, energetically speaking. Not so: its mass is a quietly stocked reservoir of energy all by itself, the rest energy:
mack.28: Set the object moving and the books simply add up: its total energy is the rest energy plus the kinetic energy it has earned by getting going:
mack.29: At a leisurely crawl (γ − 1)mc² politely agrees with ½mv², which is precisely why the classical formula gets away with it at everyday speeds. Push toward c, though, and the two part company spectacularly. Take an electron at 0.99c (γ = 7.09): the classical formula offers ½mv² = ½ × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × (2.97 × 10⁸)² = 4.0 × 10⁻¹⁴ J, while the honest answer is Ek = (7.09 − 1) × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 9.00 × 10¹⁶ = 5.0 × 10⁻¹³ J — more than twelve times larger. As v → c, γ → ∞: the kinetic energy bill climbs without limit, which is the universe's polite way of saying no massive object will ever reach the speed of light.
mack.30: The Sun pours out 3.8 × 10²⁶ W from fusion in its core, and here is the lovely sleight of hand: hydrogen nuclei fuse into helium, and the helium weighs slightly less than the ingredients it came from. That missing mass does not vanish — it leaves as light. Treat the radiated energy as converted mass and you can weigh the loss each second as follows.
mack.31: Formula: each second the Sun radiates E = 3.8 × 10²⁶ J, so E0 = mc² rearranges to m = E0/c².
mack.33: Answer: m = 4.2 × 10⁹ kg each second — roughly four million tonnes of the Sun turned into sunshine every single second. Sounds ruinous, yet it barely registers: well under 0.1% of the Sun’s mass over its entire lifetime. The Sun can afford the habit.
mack.34: For the truly total conversion, you want positron–electron annihilation. The positron is the electron’s antimatter twin, identical mass and all. Bring the two together at a crawl and they don’t bounce, don’t scatter — they cease, every scrap of their rest energy walking away as two gamma-ray photons: 2mec² = 2 × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 9.00 × 10¹⁶ = 1.64 × 10⁻¹³ J all up, which is 8.2 × 10⁻¹⁴ J ≈ 0.51 MeV apiece. The photons fly off in exactly opposite directions because the momentum they started with was essentially zero, and the books must balance. PET scanners make a diagnostic living out of catching these back-to-back pairs and tracing them to a tumour.
mack.35: Particle accelerators run the whole thing backwards, and it works beautifully: smash particles together at enormous γ and the kinetic energy congeals into the rest energy of brand-new matter, conjuring particles heftier than the ones that collided. Mass into energy, energy into mass — these are nuclear transformations, with E0 = mc² keeping score in both directions.
mack.36: Trap γmc² is the total energy, not the kinetic energy — a tempting but costly mix-up. The kinetic part is only what sits above the rest energy: Ek = (γ − 1)mc². And do not lean on the classical ½mv² anywhere near c; at 0.99c it lowballs the kinetic energy by a factor of about twelve.
mack.37: Key idea Mass and energy are two currencies with a fixed exchange rate, E0 = mc². The Sun cashes mass in for radiation, annihilation spends mass entirely on photon pairs, and accelerators run the trade the other way, buying the mass of new particles with kinetic energy.
mack.38: Connects The radius formula r = mv/qB you met for the synchrotron in topic 5 comes with fine print: v << c. The electrons of the Australian Synchrotron flout that condition on purpose — their energy runs thousands of times their rest energy — so the machine’s magnetic fields have to be engineered for the relativistic energies on this page, not the tidy classical formula.

levin.0: Near the speed of light, the old rules break. Time, length, energy — the classical answers come back wrong. Here Einstein speaks his two postulates, we count what they cost through the Lorentz factor, and we arrive, at last, at the strange tenderness of a single line: mass is energy, energy is mass. E0 = mc².
levin.1: Classical mechanics believes in a quiet world. Time falls at one rate for everyone. A length is a length, the same in every hand. Velocities simply add. And the kinetic energy ½mv² may climb forever, unbounded. At the speeds of ordinary life these beliefs hold; experiment never catches them out. But as a thing nears c = 3.00 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹, all four quietly fail. Pour energy into an electron without end, and still its speed never touches c. It approaches, and approaches, and never arrives.
levin.2: Light moves at c — topic 6 settled that — but classical physics could never say: relative to what? Add velocities the old way, and an observer rushing toward a lamp ought to clock the light faster than c. So the nineteenth century invented a sea to swim in. The ether: an unseen medium carrying light at c. And if Earth sweeps through that sea on its orbit, there should be a wind — an “ether wind” — and the speed of light should lean with the direction you look.
levin.3: In 1887 Michelson and Morley took one beam of light and split it down two perpendicular arms, then let the reflections meet again and interfere. If a wind were blowing, light running with it and light crossing it would not return together, and turning the apparatus would slide the fringes by a known, measurable amount. They turned it. Nothing moved. Every orientation, every hour of the day, every season — zero. The null result.
levin.4: Nothing moved, and that nothing said everything. The speed of light is the same in every direction, indifferent to Earth’s motion. There is no ether to find. What remains is the quiet, radical heart of special relativity — that c is the same for every observer — which Einstein, in 1905, simply chose to believe, and built a world upon.
levin.5: Key idea Michelson and Morley expected an ether wind to slide their fringes as the interferometer turned. They saw no shift, under any condition. That silence is the evidence: the speed of light is the same for every observer, untouched by the motion of the source or of the one who measures it.
levin.6: A frame of reference is the vantage you measure from: the coordinates that fix where things are, when they happen, how they move. An inertial frame of reference never accelerates; it drifts at constant velocity, and nothing inside it gives a jolt. Constant speed alone is not enough. A car holds its speed through a bend and still swings its direction, so its frame is non-inertial. Special relativity speaks only to frames that do not turn, only to frames in steady flight.
levin.7: Everything rests on two postulates. Just two.
levin.8: Postulate 1. The laws of physics are the same in every inertial frame. There is no experiment, none, that you can run inside your frame to learn how fast you are truly moving. Absolute motion leaves no trace.
levin.9: Postulate 2. The speed of light in a vacuum, c, is one value for every observer in every inertial frame, no matter how the source moves, no matter how you move. Chase it or flee it; it answers the same.
levin.10: Here is where the old world breaks. Classically, if a train running at 0.50c switches on its headlights, someone by the tracks ought to clock the beam at 1.50c. The postulate refuses: the driver and the watcher by the rails each find exactly c. Two observers sliding past each other can only agree on the speed of a single beam of light if they quietly disagree about the distances between things and the intervals between moments. So absolute time is surrendered. So is absolute length. They were never ours to keep.
levin.11: Trap “Inertial” means constant velocity, never merely constant speed. An electron circling the Australian Synchrotron storage ring holds its speed and yet falls forever inward, accelerating toward the centre with every instant, so its frame is non-inertial. The familiar formulas for time dilation and length contraction belong to inertial frames alone.
levin.12: An event is the smallest thing that can happen: a single point in space, a single instant in time. The birth of a muon. One tick of a clock. Set two inertial observers moving past each other and they will not agree on how much time separated two events, nor on how long an object is. But each disagreement has an anchor — one privileged measurement, the proper quantity, against which every other reading is read.
levin.13: Proper time t0 is the interval between two events as measured in the one frame where both events happen at the same point in space. A traveller’s two heartbeats both land at the traveller’s chest, so the traveller alone holds the proper time; everyone else watches a longer, dilated time unspool. Proper length L0 is the length of an object measured in the frame where the object lies still. Move relative to it and it shortens — a contracted length, and only along the line of the motion, never across it.
levin.14: Every relativity problem opens with two quiet questions, asked before a single formula is written down: in which frame do the two events fall at the same point in space (that observer holds t0), and in which frame does the object, or the distance to be crossed, lie at rest (that observer holds L0)? How much time stretches and how much length shrinks is set by a single number, the Lorentz factor:
levin.15: Because v < c for anything with mass, γ can never fall below 1. At rest, γ = 1. At 0.10c it is still barely 1.005, which is why classical mechanics carries us through ordinary life untroubled. At 0.50c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.25) = 1.15; at 0.90c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.81) = 2.29; at 0.99c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.9801) = 7.09. Push v → c and the denominator slides toward zero, and γ climbs without ceiling, as shown in Figure 11.1.
levin.16: Trap Both observers are right, each in their own frame; a contracted length and a dilated time are real measurements, not tricks of the eye. Proper time does not default to whoever stands still, and proper time and proper length usually belong to different observers in the same problem. A muon is born and dies both at the muon, so t0 lives in the muon’s frame; the atmosphere it falls through rests with the Earth, so L0 lives in the Earth frame. Find the events and find the object first. Only then hand out γ.
levin.17: Key idea Name the proper quantity before any formula is reached for: t0 belongs to the frame where both events fall at the same point in space, and L0 belongs to the frame where the object lies at rest. Then t = γt0 stretches time and L = L0/γ shrinks length, both by the same factor γ ≥ 1.
levin.18: Three real systems carry these predictions out of the equations and into the world; each one yields to the same two proper-quantity questions asked above.
levin.19: High up, where cosmic rays strike the thin top of the atmosphere, muons are born approximately 5.0 km above a sea-level detector, falling downward at 0.995c. At rest, a muon lives on average 2.2 μs and no longer. We take each one to travel in a straight line at constant speed until it dies.
levin.20: Reckon it classically and the muon is doomed before it arrives: 0.995 × 3.00 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹ × 2.2 × 10⁻⁶ s = 6.6 × 10² m in one lifetime, about an eighth of the way down, so almost none should ever reach us. And yet the detectors at sea level catch them in abundance. At 0.995c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.995²) = 10.0, and each frame tells the rescue in its own tongue.
levin.21: In the Earth frame: the muon is born and the muon dies, both at the muon, which is rushing past Earth, so the 2.2 μs lifetime is the proper time. Stretched, it becomes t = γt0 = 10.0 × 2.2 μs = 22 μs. In those 22 μs the muon covers 2.985 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹ × 2.2 × 10⁻⁵ s = 6.6 km, more than the 5.0 km of air above us. The muon arrives.
levin.22: In the muon’s frame: the muon holds still, its 2.2 μs lifetime is the proper time, and the whole atmosphere rushes up to meet it at 0.995c. That atmosphere is at rest relative to Earth, so 5.0 km is a proper length in the Earth frame; in the muon’s frame it shrinks to L = L0/γ = 5.0 × 10³ ÷ 10.0 = 5.0 × 10² m, crossed in 1.7 μs, less than a single lifetime. The muon arrives.
levin.23: The frames quarrel over times and over distances, yet they bow to the one observable fact: the muon arrives. What is time stretched in one frame is length shrunk in the other, two faces of the same mercy, as shown in Figure 11.2.
levin.24: A linear accelerator 3.2 km long lies at rest in the laboratory, so the laboratory reads its proper length. But the electrons streaming down it at 0.998c (γ = 15.8) find the beamline foreshortened to L = 3.2 × 10³ ÷ 15.8 = 2.0 × 10² m, and the accelerating structures must be built for particles that live inside this shorter world.
levin.25: A GPS receiver reads its place on Earth from the travel times of signals falling from satellites, so the satellite clocks must keep faith with ground clocks to a punishing precision. Each satellite orbits at approximately 3.9 × 10³ m s⁻¹, which gives γ = 1 + 8.5 × 10⁻¹¹. A small thing, almost nothing, but over one day (86 400 s) the orbiting clock, telling proper time aboard the satellite, slips behind the ground by approximately 7.3 μs. Light runs 2.2 km in 7.3 μs, so a system left uncorrected would wander by kilometres each day; the GPS clocks are corrected for their speed, and so the world stays found.
levin.26: Connects The satellites corrected here are the very circular orbits of topic 2: the orbital radius fixes the speed v, and v fixes γ, so the relativistic correction is laid down by the same orbital mechanics that lifted the satellite into the sky in the first place.
levin.27: Relativity ends where it began, in stillness. An object at rest is not empty. Its mass is already a quiet hoard of energy, the rest energy it carries simply by existing:
levin.28: Set it moving and the energy it holds is the sum of two things, the stillness it never loses and the motion it has gained:
levin.29: At slow speeds (γ − 1)mc² folds back into ½mv², and this is the small mercy that lets the classical formula keep working in the world we walk through. But press close to c and the two part ways. Take an electron at 0.99c (γ = 7.09): the old formula promises ½mv² = ½ × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × (2.97 × 10⁸)² = 4.0 × 10⁻¹⁴ J, while the true cost is Ek = (7.09 − 1) × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 9.00 × 10¹⁶ = 5.0 × 10⁻¹³ J, more than twelve times as much. As v → c, γ → ∞: the energy you must pour in grows without end, and so the speed of light stays forever shut to anything with mass.
levin.30: The Sun pours 3.8 × 10²⁶ W out of its burning core. Hydrogen fuses into helium, and what comes out weighs a little less than what went in. That missing mass is the light. We treat the radiance as mass made over into energy, and we can weigh, second by second, what the Sun spends of itself.
levin.31: Each second the Sun lets go of E = 3.8 × 10²⁶ J, and E0 = mc² turns around to ask for the mass: m = E0/c².
levin.33: And the answer: m = 4.2 × 10⁹ kg gone every second, four million tonnes of the Sun lifted into sunlight with each tick of the clock. And still, across the whole long life of the star, this spends well under 0.1% of its mass. The Sun barely notices.
levin.34: Sometimes the trade is complete, nothing held back. In positron–electron annihilation the conversion is total. The positron is the electron’s mirror twin, its antiparticle, equal in mass. When the two drift together and touch at rest, both vanish, and every last scrap of their rest energy flies out as two gamma-ray photons: 2mec² = 2 × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 9.00 × 10¹⁶ = 1.64 × 10⁻¹³ J between them, which is 8.2 × 10⁻¹⁴ J ≈ 0.51 MeV in each. They leave in opposite directions, because there was almost no momentum to begin with. PET scanners listen for exactly these back-to-back pairs, and find the tumour by the light of matter undone.
levin.35: Accelerators run the same exchange backwards: a collision at large γ takes raw kinetic energy and casts it into the rest mass of new matter, particles heavier than the ones that struck. Matter into energy, energy into matter, both directions kept by the same law, E0 = mc².
levin.36: Trap γmc² is the whole of the energy, not the kinetic part. The kinetic energy is only what rises above the rest energy: Ek = (γ − 1)mc². Near c the old ½mv² will not stand in for it; at 0.99c it falls short of the kinetic energy by a factor of about twelve.
levin.37: Key idea Mass and energy are one thing wearing two faces, traded at the fixed rate E0 = mc². The Sun spends mass as light, annihilation spends mass wholly into photon pairs, and accelerators spend kinetic energy to build the mass of new particles.
levin.38: Connects The radius formula r = mv/qB that steered the synchrotron in topic 5 carries a quiet condition, v << c. The electrons of the Australian Synchrotron break it on purpose, their energy thousands of times their rest energy, so the magnets that bend them must be built for the relativistic energies of this page, not the classical promise of that one.

czerski.0: Push something fast enough — faster than anything you’ll ever throw across a kitchen — and the comfortable rules for time, length and energy that work so well in everyday life quietly stop giving the right answers. This topic sets out Einstein’s two postulates, pins down their consequences with the Lorentz factor, and finishes with one of the loveliest ideas in all of physics: that mass and energy are the same thing wearing different clothes, E0 = mc².
czerski.1: Classical mechanics quietly assumes a few things that feel obviously true: that a second ticks past at the same rate for everyone, that a metre stick is a metre for all of us, that velocities simply add up the way you’d expect when you walk forward on a moving train, and that the kinetic energy ½mv² can keep climbing for ever. At the speeds of cars and cricket balls, every one of these holds up beautifully against experiment. But as speeds climb toward c = 3.00 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹, all four quietly break down: you can pour energy into an electron in an accelerator all day long, and yet its speed creeps closer and closer to c without ever quite getting there.
czerski.2: Light travels at c, as we saw back in topic 6 — but here’s the awkward question classical physics couldn’t answer: at c relative to what? Ordinary velocity addition says that if you run toward a light source, you ought to clock the light coming in faster than c, just as walking toward an oncoming wave makes it reach you sooner. Nineteenth-century physicists reached for a sensible-sounding answer: the ether, an invisible medium filling all of space, the stuff light waves wobble through at c. And if it were real, the Earth ploughing through it on its orbit ought to stir up an “ether wind”, so that the speed of light you measured would depend on which way you looked.
czerski.3: In 1887 Michelson and Morley set out to feel that wind. They took a single beam of light, split it down two arms of an interferometer set at right angles, bounced the halves back and let them recombine into an interference pattern of bright and dark fringes. If there really were an ether wind, light running with it would take a slightly different time from light cutting across it, so simply rotating the whole apparatus should slide those fringes sideways by an amount they could calculate in advance. They looked, and they kept looking — every orientation, every hour of the day, every season of the year. The fringes never budged. This is the famous null result.
czerski.4: That stubborn nothing turns out to be enormously informative. It tells us the measured speed of light is the same in every direction, no matter how the Earth is moving, and it leaves the ether with no evidence to stand on. What it does support is the bold heart of special relativity — that c is the same for everyone, whoever you are and however you’re moving — which Einstein simply took as a starting assumption, a postulate, in 1905.
czerski.5: Key idea Michelson and Morley expected an ether wind to nudge their interference fringes as the interferometer turned; instead the fringes sat perfectly still under every condition they could devise. That null result backs the postulate that the speed of light is the same for all observers, quite independent of how the source or the observer happens to be moving.
czerski.6: Before we go any further, we need to agree on where we’re standing. A frame of reference is simply the set of coordinates you measure positions, times and motions from — your own little patch of the universe with a ruler and a clock. An inertial frame of reference is one that doesn’t accelerate; it sails along at constant velocity. And here’s the catch: constant speed isn’t enough. Picture yourself in a car taking a bend at a steady 60 — the speedometer doesn’t budge, but you’re flung against the door, because you’re changing direction. That makes the car’s frame non-inertial. Special relativity only works in the smooth, unaccelerated ones.
czerski.7: The whole astonishing structure balances on just two ideas:
czerski.8: Postulate 1. The laws of physics are the same in every inertial frame. Do whatever experiment you like inside your frame — spill your tea, swing a pendulum, fire a laser — and nothing will ever tell you how fast you’re really moving. There is no test you can run to pin down your absolute motion.
czerski.9: Postulate 2. The speed of light in a vacuum, c, comes out exactly the same for everyone in an inertial frame — it doesn’t matter a jot how the source is moving, and it doesn’t matter how you are moving either. Light simply refuses to play favourites.
czerski.10: That second one is where everything you thought you knew quietly falls apart. Think of a train rushing along at 0.50c with its headlights on. Common sense — and classical physics — insists that someone standing by the track should clock the light at 1.50c, the train’s speed added on top. But the postulate is stubborn: the driver and the person by the track each measure exactly c, not a whisker more. The only way two people moving relative to each other can possibly agree on the speed of the very same beam is if they disagree about how far it travelled and how long it took. And so absolute time and absolute length, the bedrock of everyday life, simply have to go.
czerski.11: Trap “Inertial” means constant velocity, not constant speed — and the difference matters enormously. An electron whizzing round the storage ring of the Australian Synchrotron holds a perfectly steady speed, yet it’s forever being yanked toward the centre, accelerating the whole way round. So its frame is non-inertial. The familiar time-dilation and length-contraction formulas only apply to inertial frames.
czerski.12: Let's pin down what we mean by an event. It's simply something that happens at one particular spot in space at one particular moment in time — the birth of a muon, say, or a single tick of a clock. Here is the surprising bit: two inertial observers in relative motion will flatly disagree about how much time passed between two events, and about how long an object is. To compare their accounts, each measurement is pinned to one privileged, gold-standard reading called the proper quantity.
czerski.13: Proper time t0 is the time between two events as measured in the one frame of reference where both events happen at the very same point in space. Picture a traveller's heart: both beats land at the traveller's own chest, so it is the traveller who reads off the proper time — while everyone else clocks a longer, dilated time. Proper length L0 is the length of an object measured in the frame where the object is sitting still. Anyone moving past it measures a shorter, contracted length — and here's the lovely detail: it shrinks only along the direction of the motion, not sideways.
czerski.14: So every relativity problem opens with the same two questions, and you ask them before you touch a single formula: in which frame do the two events happen at the same point in space (that's the observer who reads t0), and in which frame is the object — or the distance being crossed — sitting at rest (that's the observer who reads L0)? Once you know that, the size of the stretching and squashing is dialled in by one number, the Lorentz factor:
czerski.15: Because v < c for anything with mass, γ can never dip below 1. At rest γ = 1, and even at a brisk 0.10c it has crept up to just 1.005 — which is exactly why classical mechanics gets away with it at the speeds of everyday life. Push harder and it starts to bite: at 0.50c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.25) = 1.15; at 0.90c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.81) = 2.29; at 0.99c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.9801) = 7.09. As v → c the denominator slides toward zero and γ shoots up without limit, as shown in Figure 11.1.
czerski.16: Trap Don't be tempted to call one of these readings the “wrong” one. Both observers are right in their own frame; a contracted length and a dilated time are genuine measurements, not tricks of perception. Proper time does not automatically belong to whoever is standing still, and the catch is that proper time and proper length usually belong to different observers in the same problem. Take a muon: its creation and its decay both happen right at the muon, so t0 lives in the muon's frame of reference — while the atmosphere it falls through is at rest with respect to Earth, so L0 lives in the Earth frame. Find the events and find the object first; only then do you hand out γ.
czerski.17: Key idea Work out the proper quantity before you reach for any formula: t0 belongs to the frame where both events happen at the same point in space, and L0 belongs to the frame where the object is at rest. After that the rest follows neatly — t = γt0 stretches the time out and L = L0/γ squeezes the length down, both by the very same factor γ ≥ 1.
czerski.18: Here is where the theory stops being a thought experiment and starts showing up in things we can actually measure. Three real systems put these predictions to work, and each one yields to exactly the same two proper-quantity questions we have just been asking.
czerski.19: Start with muons, which are raining down on your head right now. Cosmic rays slam into the upper atmosphere and create them roughly 5.0 km above a sea-level detector, racing downward at 0.995c. Sit a muon still and it lives, on average, just 2.2 μs before it falls apart. We treat each one as if it simply flies in a straight line at constant speed until it decays.
czerski.20: Do the classical sum and it looks hopeless. In one lifetime the muon covers 0.995 × 3.00 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹ × 2.2 × 10⁻⁶ s = 6.6 × 10² m, only about an eighth of the way down, so by rights almost none of them should ever reach the ground. And yet detectors at sea level catch them in their thousands. The resolution is that at 0.995c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.995²) = 10.0, and each frame tells the story in its own way.
czerski.21: In the Earth frame: the muon is born and dies right where the muon is, and the muon is the thing rushing past us, so that 2.2 μs lifetime is the proper time. Stretched out, the lifetime we measure becomes t = γt0 = 10.0 × 2.2 μs = 22 μs. In 22 μs the muon covers 2.985 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹ × 2.2 × 10⁻⁵ s = 6.6 km, comfortably more than the 5.0 km of air between it and us. So it makes it down.
czerski.22: In the muon’s frame: now ride along with the muon. It sits perfectly still, its 2.2 μs lifetime is the proper time, and it is the whole atmosphere that comes hurtling upward past it at 0.995c. That atmosphere is at rest relative to Earth, so the 5.0 km is a proper length in the Earth frame; from the muon’s point of view it is squashed to L = L0/γ = 5.0 × 10³ ÷ 10.0 = 5.0 × 10² m, which sweeps past in 1.7 μs, less than a single lifetime. So it makes it down.
czerski.23: The two frames bicker about times and distances, but they shake hands on the one thing you can actually see: the muon arrives. What one frame calls a stretched clock, the other calls a shrunken ruler, as shown in Figure 11.2.
czerski.24: The same trick runs the other way in a particle accelerator. A linear accelerator 3.2 km long sits bolted to the laboratory floor, so the lab measures its proper length. But ride an electron flying down it at 0.998c (γ = 15.8) and the beamline ahead of you is squeezed to L = 3.2 × 10³ ÷ 15.8 = 2.0 × 10² m. The engineers have to build the accelerating structures with that in mind, because the particles genuinely experience the shorter machine.
czerski.25: And it is happening in the phone in your pocket. A GPS receiver works out where you are by timing signals broadcast from satellites, which only works if those satellite clocks keep pace with ground clocks to an almost absurd precision. Each satellite is sailing round at roughly 3.9 × 10³ m s⁻¹, giving γ = 1 + 8.5 × 10⁻¹¹. A tiny number, but let it run across a single day (86 400 s) and the orbiting clock, which keeps proper time up there, slips behind the ground clocks by about 7.3 μs. Light covers 2.2 km in 7.3 μs, so left uncorrected the whole system would wander off by kilometres a day; that is why GPS clocks are deliberately tweaked for their orbital speed.
czerski.26: Connects The satellites we are correcting here are exactly the circular orbits of topic 2: the orbital radius pins down the speed v, and v pins down γ, so this relativistic nudge is set by the very same orbital mechanics that parked the satellite up there in the first place.
czerski.27: And now the part of relativity that I think is the most wonderful of all, because it’s about energy. Sit something perfectly still on a table and you might assume it’s doing nothing, energetically speaking. But it isn’t empty at all. Its mass is itself a hidden store of energy, the rest energy:
czerski.28: Set the object moving and you simply add to that store. Its total energy is the rest energy plus the kinetic energy of motion:
czerski.29: Dawdle along at everyday speeds and (γ − 1)mc² matches the familiar ½mv² beautifully — which is exactly why the classical formula has served us so well for so long. Push close to c, though, and the two part company dramatically. Take an electron at 0.99c (γ = 7.09): the old formula hands you ½mv² = ½ × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × (2.97 × 10⁸)² = 4.0 × 10⁻¹⁴ J, but the real value is Ek = (7.09 − 1) × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 9.00 × 10¹⁶ = 5.0 × 10⁻¹³ J — more than twelve times bigger. And as v → c, γ → ∞: the energy you’d have to pour in climbs without any limit at all. That’s the universe’s way of saying no massive thing ever gets to ride at the speed of light.
czerski.30: Look up on a sunny day and you’re feeling the output of this idea directly. The Sun pours out 3.8 × 10²⁶ W, all generated by nuclear fusion deep in its core. Hydrogen nuclei merge into helium, and here’s the lovely bit: the helium that comes out weighs slightly less than the hydrogen that went in. That missing mass is the sunlight. We can treat the radiated energy as converted mass and work out how much vanishes each second.
czerski.31: Formula: every second the Sun radiates E = 3.8 × 10²⁶ J, so we just rearrange E0 = mc² to give us m = E0/c².
czerski.33: Answer: m = 4.2 × 10⁹ kg each second — that’s roughly four million tonnes of the Sun turned into light, every single second. It sounds ruinous, but the Sun is so vast that even at this rate it gets through well under 0.1% of its mass across its entire lifetime.
czerski.34: Sometimes the conversion goes the whole way, every last scrap of mass becoming energy, and that happens in positron–electron annihilation. A positron is the electron’s antiparticle, identical in mass. Bring the two together gently and they don’t just collide — they vanish completely, their entire rest energy flying off as two gamma-ray photons: 2mec² = 2 × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 9.00 × 10¹⁶ = 1.64 × 10⁻¹³ J all told, which works out as 8.2 × 10⁻¹⁴ J ≈ 0.51 MeV per photon. They shoot off in exactly opposite directions because there was almost no momentum to begin with. This is the trick behind PET scanners in hospitals, which pinpoint tumours by catching those back-to-back pairs.
czerski.35: Particle accelerators play the whole thing backwards: smash particles together at enormous γ and the kinetic energy gets poured into the rest energy of brand-new matter, conjuring particles heavier than the ones that crashed in the first place. Mass into energy, energy into mass — these are nuclear transformations, and E0 = mc² runs the books in both directions.
czerski.36: Trap Watch this one carefully: γmc² is the total energy, not the kinetic energy. The kinetic part is only what sits above the rest energy: Ek = (γ − 1)mc². And don’t be tempted to fall back on ½mv² anywhere near c — at 0.99c it undercounts the kinetic energy by a factor of about twelve.
czerski.37: Key idea Mass and energy are two faces of the same thing, swapped at the fixed exchange rate E0 = mc². The Sun trades mass for radiation, annihilation cashes mass in entirely for photon pairs, and accelerators spend kinetic energy to mint the mass of new particles.
czerski.38: Connects Remember the radius formula r = mv/qB we used for the synchrotron in topic 5? It comes with the quiet condition v << c. The electrons of the Australian Synchrotron deliberately break that rule — their energy is thousands of times their rest energy — so the machine’s magnetic fields have to be built for the relativistic energies on this page, not the tidy classical formula.

veritasium.0: You probably think time ticks the same for everyone, that a metre is a metre no matter who's holding the ruler, and that if you just keep pushing something it'll go faster and faster without limit. Get close enough to the speed of light and every one of those instincts turns out to be wrong. This topic starts with the two postulates Einstein dared to take seriously, follows their consequences through the Lorentz factor, and ends with the most famous equation in physics, the one that says mass is energy: E0 = mc².
veritasium.1: Here's the model you carry around without realising it: time passes at the same rate for everyone, lengths are the same for everyone, velocities just add up the way you'd expect, and kinetic energy ½mv² can climb forever. At the speeds you live at, that model works perfectly and nothing ever contradicts it. But watch what happens as you approach c = 3.00 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹: all four of those comfortable assumptions break at once. Pour energy into an electron in an accelerator and you can keep doing it forever, yet the electron's speed never quite reaches c. So where is all that energy going?
veritasium.2: Light travels at c, as you saw in topic 6. But here's the question nobody could answer: relative to what? Your intuition says it's obvious, just run toward the source and you should clock the light coming at you faster than c, the same way headlights look faster when you drive toward them. Classical velocity addition demands exactly that. So nineteenth-century physicists invented something to make it work: the ether, an invisible medium filling all of space, the thing light supposedly waves through at c. And if the ether is real, then Earth ploughing through it on its orbit should create an “ether wind”, and the speed of light you measure should depend on which way you point your instrument.
veritasium.3: In 1887 Michelson and Morley set out to catch that wind. They split a single light beam down two perpendicular arms of an interferometer, bounced the halves back, and recombined them into an interference pattern. The logic is airtight: if there's an ether wind, light running with the wind takes a different time than light running across it, so spinning the apparatus around should make the fringes shift by a precise, predictable, measurable amount. They had the sensitivity to see it. They rotated it at every orientation, every time of day, every season. And the shift they measured was zero. Every single time. The null result.
veritasium.4: Sit with what that zero means. The speed of light comes out identical in every direction, no matter how Earth is moving, which is precisely what you would not expect if you were barrelling through an ether. There simply was no ether to find. What looked like a failed experiment was actually pointing at something far stranger and far more important: that c is the same for every observer, full stop. Einstein took that exact idea and, in 1905, promoted it from awkward fact to founding postulate.
veritasium.5: Key idea Michelson and Morley predicted that an ether wind would shift their interference fringes as the interferometer rotated. They looked, and under every condition there was no shift at all. That null result is the evidence behind the postulate you might otherwise refuse to believe: the speed of light is the same for all observers, no matter how the source moves and no matter how you move.
veritasium.6: Here's something you probably believe without ever questioning it: that as long as you're moving at a steady speed, your point of view is just as good as anyone standing still. So let me get precise about when that's actually true. A frame of reference is the set of coordinates from which you measure positions, times and motions. An inertial frame of reference is one that does not accelerate; it moves at constant velocity. And watch the trap here, because steady speed is not enough: a car rounding a bend at a constant speed is changing direction, which means it's accelerating, which means its frame is non-inertial. Special relativity only applies to inertial frames.
veritasium.7: And the whole theory you're about to have your intuition dismantled by rests on just two postulates:
veritasium.8: Postulate 1. The laws of physics are the same in all inertial frames of reference. This one feels harmless, but think about what it forbids: no experiment you perform inside an inertial frame can ever tell you the frame's absolute motion. There is no test for “truly at rest.”
veritasium.9: Postulate 2. The speed of light in a vacuum, c, has the same value for all observers in inertial frames, regardless of the motion of the light source or of the observer.
veritasium.10: Now stop and feel how wrong that second postulate sounds, because that's the whole point. Your intuition insists speeds add up. So if a train moving at 0.50c turns on its headlights, you'd swear a trackside observer measures the light at 1.50c — the train's speed plus the light's speed. Commit to that answer. Now here's the postulate dismantling it: the driver and the trackside observer each measure exactly c. The same beam. And there's only one way out: two observers in relative motion can agree on the speed of that beam only if they disagree about distances and time intervals. So the thing that has to break is the thing you never doubted — absolute time and absolute length are abandoned.
veritasium.11: Trap You almost certainly read “inertial” as “not speeding up or slowing down” — constant speed. But it means constant velocity. An electron circulating in the Australian Synchrotron storage ring holds a perfectly constant speed, yet it accelerates continuously toward the centre, so its frame is non-inertial. The standard time-dilation and length-contraction formulas apply to inertial frames.
veritasium.12: An event is something that happens at a particular point in space at a particular time, such as the creation of a muon or one tick of a clock. Now, you probably think that if two of these events happen, there is one true time interval between them and one true length of the object involved, and that any disagreement between observers just means somebody measured wrong. That is exactly the intuition relativity destroys. Inertial observers in relative motion genuinely disagree about the time interval between two events and about the length of an object, and they are both right. Each comparison is anchored by a privileged measurement called the proper quantity.
veritasium.13: Proper time t0 is the time interval between two events measured in the frame of reference in which both events occur at the same point in space. Here is where your intuition trips you up: you almost certainly assume the person standing still measures the “real” time. Watch what actually happens. Both beats of a traveller’s heart occur at the traveller’s chest, so the traveller measures proper time; every other observer measures a longer, dilated time. The same trap waits for length. Proper length L0 is the length of an object measured in the frame of reference in which the object is at rest. Observers moving relative to the object measure a shorter, contracted length, contracted only along the direction of relative motion.
veritasium.14: Most people reach for the formula first and ask which observer is “moving” second, and that is exactly how you get the wrong answer. Every relativity problem actually begins with two questions, asked before any formula is applied: in which frame do the two events occur at the same point in space (that observer measures t0), and in which frame is the object, or the distance being traversed, at rest (that observer measures L0)? Get those two right and the physics follows. The amount of dilation and contraction is set by the Lorentz factor:
veritasium.15: You might expect this factor to matter the moment anything starts moving, but look at the numbers. Since v < c for any massive object, γ is never less than 1: γ = 1 at rest, and at 0.10c it is still only 1.005, so classical mechanics is sufficient at everyday speeds — that is why you never noticed any of this. The effect hides until you push toward c. At 0.50c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.25) = 1.15; at 0.90c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.81) = 2.29; at 0.99c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.9801) = 7.09. As v → c the denominator approaches zero and γ increases without bound, as shown in Figure 11.1.
veritasium.16: Trap Here is the mistake almost everyone makes: they assume one observer sees the truth and the other is fooled by an illusion. Both observers’ measurements are correct in their own frames; a contracted length and a dilated time are real measurements, not illusions. And do not assume proper time belongs to the stationary observer by default — it does not. Proper time and proper length usually belong to different observers in the same problem: a muon’s creation and decay both occur at the muon, so t0 is measured in the muon’s frame of reference, while the atmosphere through which it falls is at rest relative to Earth, so L0 is measured in the Earth frame. The events and the object must be located first; only then is γ assigned.
veritasium.17: Key idea Resist the urge to plug into a formula before you know who measures what — that is the misstep. The proper quantity is identified before any formula is applied: t0 belongs to the frame in which both events occur at the same point in space, and L0 belongs to the frame in which the object is at rest. Then t = γt0 stretches time and L = L0/γ shrinks length, by the same factor γ ≥ 1.
veritasium.18: You might think these effects are just mathematical curiosities, equations that work on paper but never touch the real world. They’re not. Three real systems put these predictions into operation, and each one is cracked open with the same two proper-quantity questions you just learned.
veritasium.19: Cosmic rays striking the upper atmosphere create muons approximately 5.0 km above a sea-level detector, moving downward at 0.995c. A muon at rest has an average lifetime of 2.2 μs.
