Picture one of these muons, born high in the sky, racing toward the ground, modelled as travelling in a straight line at constant speed until it decays. Here’s the question: does it make it?
veritasium.20: Your gut says no. Run the classical numbers and your gut looks right: the muon travels 0.995 × 3.00 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹ × 2.2 × 10⁻⁶ s = 6.6 × 10² m in one lifetime, roughly an eighth of the way down, so almost none should survive to the detector. And yet detectors at sea level record muons in abundance. The classical prediction is dead wrong. At 0.995c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.995²) = 10.0, and watch how each frame resolves the paradox in its own way.
veritasium.21: In the Earth frame: the muon’s creation and decay both happen at the muon, which moves relative to Earth, so the 2.2 μs lifetime is the proper time. The lifetime you measure from the ground is dilated: t = γt0 = 10.0 × 2.2 μs = 22 μs. In those 22 μs the muon travels 2.985 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹ × 2.2 × 10⁻⁵ s = 6.6 km, which overshoots the 5.0 km of atmosphere. The muon arrives. Its clock, from where you stand, is running slow.
veritasium.22: In the muon’s frame: now flip your viewpoint. The muon is at rest, its 2.2 μs lifetime is the proper time, and it’s the atmosphere that comes screaming upward past it at 0.995c. The atmosphere is at rest relative to Earth, so 5.0 km is a proper length in the Earth frame; in the muon frame that distance is contracted to L = L0/γ = 5.0 × 10³ ÷ 10.0 = 5.0 × 10² m, crossed in 1.7 μs, less than a single lifetime. The muon arrives. No time dilation needed here, just a shorter trip.
veritasium.23: So who’s right? Both are. The frames flatly disagree about times and distances, yet they agree on the one thing you can actually check: the muon arrives. What one frame calls time dilation, the other calls length contraction, two descriptions of the same reality, as shown in Figure 11.2.
veritasium.24: A linear accelerator 3.2 km long sits at rest in the laboratory, so the laboratory measures its proper length. You’d assume the electrons fired down it see the same 3.2 km you do. They don’t. Travelling at 0.998c (γ = 15.8), each electron measures the beamline contracted to L = 3.2 × 10³ ÷ 15.8 = 2.0 × 10² m, and the accelerating structures have to be designed for particles that genuinely experience this shorter length.
veritasium.25: You probably assume the GPS in your phone is just clever geometry, and relativity is irrelevant to it. In fact it would fail without it. A GPS receiver fixes its position from the travel times of timing signals broadcast by satellites, so satellite clocks must agree with ground clocks to staggering precision. Each satellite orbits at approximately 3.9 × 10³ m s⁻¹, giving γ = 1 + 8.5 × 10⁻¹¹. That number looks impossibly tiny, but over one day (86 400 s) the orbiting clock, which records proper time aboard the satellite, falls behind ground clocks by approximately 7.3 μs. Light travels 2.2 km in 7.3 μs, so an uncorrected system would wander off by kilometres every single day; GPS clocks are corrected for their orbital speed.
veritasium.26: Connects Don’t treat these satellites as a separate topic; they are the very same circular orbits from topic 2. The orbital radius fixes the speed v, and v fixes γ, so the relativistic correction is dictated by the same orbital mechanics that put the satellite up there in the first place.
veritasium.27: You probably think a brick sitting on a table has zero energy. It’s not moving, nothing’s happening, so the energy is zero, right? Wrong. The mass itself is a reservoir of energy, locked away just by virtue of existing. This is the rest energy:
veritasium.28: Now set that object moving, and here’s where intuition really fails you. You might assume the energy of motion just gets added on top in the ordinary way. It does get added, but the total energy is the rest energy plus the kinetic energy:
veritasium.29: Here’s the part that catches everyone out. You learned ½mv² in school and you trust it completely. And at low speeds you’re fine: (γ − 1)mc² agrees with ½mv², which is exactly why nobody noticed anything was wrong for centuries. But push toward c and the two answers split apart dramatically. Take an electron at 0.99c (γ = 7.09). Your trusted formula says ½mv² = ½ × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × (2.97 × 10⁸)² = 4.0 × 10⁻¹⁴ J. The real answer is Ek = (7.09 − 1) × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 9.00 × 10¹⁶ = 5.0 × 10⁻¹³ J — more than twelve times bigger. And it only gets worse: as v → c, γ → ∞, so the energy you’d need blows up to infinity. That’s the real reason nothing with mass can ever reach the speed of light.
veritasium.30: You probably picture the Sun as just burning, like a giant fire. It isn’t. The Sun radiates 3.8 × 10²⁶ W, and that power doesn’t come from burning anything — it comes from fusing hydrogen nuclei into helium, where the helium ends up with slightly less mass than the hydrogen you started with. That missing mass is what you see as sunlight. So how much mass actually vanishes every second? Let’s calculate it.
veritasium.31: Formula: each second the Sun radiates E = 3.8 × 10²⁶ J, so E0 = mc² rearranges to m = E0/c².
veritasium.33: Answer: m = 4.2 × 10⁹ kg each second. Stop and feel how big that is — about four million tonnes of the Sun’s own substance disappearing into light, every single second. You’d expect that to drain the Sun fast. It doesn’t: over its entire lifetime this consumes well under 0.1% of the Sun’s mass.
veritasium.34: If you think the Sun is impressive, it’s actually being stingy — only a tiny fraction of the mass turns to energy. For total conversion you need positron–electron annihilation. The positron is the antiparticle of the electron, identical mass. Bring the two together at negligible speed and they don’t just collide — they vanish completely, their entire rest energy reappearing as two gamma-ray photons: 2mec² = 2 × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 9.00 × 10¹⁶ = 1.64 × 10⁻¹³ J in total, which is 8.2 × 10⁻¹⁴ J ≈ 0.51 MeV per photon. And here’s the elegant part: the photons fly off in exactly opposite directions, because the total momentum beforehand was approximately zero. That back-to-back signature isn’t just a curiosity — it’s how PET medical scanners pinpoint tumours inside your body.
veritasium.35: And if you assumed mass-to-energy only runs one way, watch particle accelerators run it backwards. Collisions at large γ take raw kinetic energy and freeze it into the rest energy of brand-new matter — particles heavier than anything that went into the collision. Same equation, E0 = mc², working in both directions: mass into energy, and energy straight back into mass.
veritasium.36: Trap Here’s where people slip up: they see γmc² and call it the kinetic energy. It isn’t — that’s the total energy. The kinetic energy is only the part sitting above the rest energy: Ek = (γ − 1)mc². And don’t reach for the comfortable ½mv² near c, because it lies to you — at 0.99c it understates the kinetic energy by a factor of about twelve.
veritasium.37: Key idea The big revelation is that mass and energy were never two separate things — they’re the same currency, swapped at the fixed exchange rate E0 = mc². The Sun converts mass into radiation, annihilation converts mass entirely into photon pairs, and accelerators run it in reverse, converting kinetic energy into the mass of new particles.
veritasium.38: Connects You might trust the radius formula r = mv/qB from the synchrotron in topic 5 — but read the fine print: it only holds for v << c. The electrons of the Australian Synchrotron deliberately break that condition, since their energy is thousands of times their rest energy. So the machine’s magnetic fields can’t be designed with the classical formula at all; they have to be engineered for the relativistic energies on this page.

attenborough.0: As a traveller approaches the speed of light, the old, familiar rules — the rules of time, of length, of energy — quietly cease to hold. They give the wrong answer. Here we shall watch Einstein’s two postulates take shape, measure their consequences through the Lorentz factor, and arrive, at last, at one of the most remarkable truths in all of nature: that mass and energy are one and the same thing, E0 = mc².
attenborough.1: For centuries we believed the world was simple. Time, we thought, passed at the same steady rate for every observer; lengths were the same for all who measured them; velocities added together, plainly; and the kinetic energy ½mv² could climb forever, without limit. And at the gentle speeds of everyday life, this belief serves us perfectly. But venture closer to c = 3.00 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹, and all four of these certainties fall away. An accelerator may pour energy into a single electron without end — and yet that electron, however hard it is pushed, never quite reaches c.
attenborough.2: Light travels at c, as we saw in topic 6 — but relative to what? That was the question the old physics could not answer. By the classical law of adding velocities, an observer racing toward a source of light ought to measure it arriving faster than c. And so, in the nineteenth century, physicists imagined the ether: an invisible medium filling all of space, through which light glides at c. If it were real, then the Earth, sweeping along its orbit, should sail through this sea and stir an “ether wind” — and the speed of light, measured in different directions, should betray it.
attenborough.3: In 1887, Michelson and Morley set out to catch that wind. They took a single beam of light, split it down two perpendicular arms of an interferometer, and let the reflections fall back together into a delicate pattern of interference. If an ether wind truly blew, the beam travelling with it should take a different time from the beam crossing it — and turning the instrument should make the fringes drift by a precise, predictable amount. They watched. They turned it through every angle, at every hour, in every season. And the fringes did not move. Nothing. The famous null result.
attenborough.4: That silence speaks volumes. It tells us the speed of light is the same in every direction, heedless of the Earth’s ceaseless motion, and it offers not a trace of evidence for the ether. It points, instead, toward the heart of special relativity: that c is the same for every observer, everywhere — the very truth that Einstein, in 1905, would take up as a postulate.
attenborough.5: Key idea Michelson and Morley predicted that an ether wind would shift the fringes as their interferometer turned. They saw no shift, under any conditions whatsoever. That null result upholds the postulate that the speed of light is the same for all observers, independent of the motion of the source or of the observer.
attenborough.6: To follow our traveller, we must first learn the rules of its world. A frame of reference is simply a vantage point — the set of coordinates from which positions, times and motions are measured. Some of these vantage points are special. An inertial frame of reference does not accelerate; it drifts on, serenely, at constant velocity. Constant speed alone will not do: a car sweeping round a bend at steady speed is forever changing direction, and so its frame is non-inertial. It is to the calm, unhurried inertial frames — and to them only — that special relativity belongs.
attenborough.7: And the whole of this strange world rests upon just two laws:
attenborough.8: Postulate 1. The laws of physics are the same in all inertial frames of reference. No experiment, however ingenious, performed within such a frame can ever reveal its absolute motion. Our traveller, adrift, has no way of knowing it moves at all.
attenborough.9: Postulate 2. The speed of light in a vacuum, c, has the same value for all observers in inertial frames, regardless of the motion of the light source or of the observer. This single, unbending rule, more than any other, will shape the journey ahead.
attenborough.10: Here is the moment everything changes — the quiet rebellion against the old physics. Picture a train rushing through the night at 0.50c; it switches on its headlights, and an observer at the trackside, by the old reckoning, should see that light streak past at 1.50c. But the second postulate will not allow it. The driver and the watcher by the rails each measure exactly c, no more, no less. Two travellers in relative motion can agree on the speed of a single beam of light only if they surrender something far more precious: their agreement about distance, and about the passing of time. And so, in this world, absolute time and absolute length are quietly abandoned — the very ground beneath the muon's long fall.
attenborough.11: Trap Beware: “inertial” means constant velocity, not constant speed. Consider an electron circling endlessly in the Australian Synchrotron storage ring at constant speed — yet forever falling inward, accelerating toward the centre, never resting. Its frame is non-inertial. The standard time-dilation and length-contraction formulas hold only in the tranquil inertial frames, and nowhere else.
attenborough.12: Here, in the world of the very fast, the smallest happening matters. An event is something that occurs at a single point in space at a single moment in time — the birth of a muon high above us, or one solitary tick of a clock. And here lies the strangeness: two observers, drifting past one another, will not agree on how much time passed between two events, nor on the length of a thing. Each disagreement is settled, in the end, by one privileged measurement — the proper quantity, the truth as seen from the one frame that has the right to claim it.
attenborough.13: Proper time t0 is the time between two events as measured in the one frame where both happen at the same place. Watch a lone traveller: both beats of the heart fall at the traveller’s own chest, never moving, and so the traveller alone measures proper time. To every other watcher in the universe, that same time runs longer — dilated, stretched thin. Proper length L0 is the length of an object as measured in the frame where it lies at rest. To anyone rushing past, that object appears shorter, contracted — squeezed only along the line of the chase, never across it.
attenborough.14: Before a single formula is spoken, two questions must be asked of every journey through this strange world: in which frame do the two events fall at the same place — for that observer holds t0 — and in which frame does the object, or the distance to be crossed, lie at rest — for that observer holds L0? How far time stretches and length shrinks is governed by a single ruling number, the Lorentz factor:
attenborough.15: No thing with mass may ever quite reach c, so v < c always, and γ never falls below 1. At rest, γ = 1. Even at a tenth of light speed, 0.10c, it has crept only to 1.005 — which is why, in the slow ordinary world we know, the classical rules serve us well. But press onward. At 0.50c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.25) = 1.15; at 0.90c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.81) = 2.29; at 0.99c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.9801) = 7.09. And as v → c, the denominator slips toward zero and γ climbs without end, soaring beyond all bound, as shown in Figure 11.1.
attenborough.16: Trap Make no mistake — every observer is right within their own frame. A contracted length, a dilated time: these are real measurements, true sightings, not tricks of the eye. Proper time does not belong by birthright to the one standing still, and in a single problem proper time and proper length will usually belong to different watchers. Follow the muon: its birth and its death both happen at the muon itself, so t0 lives in the muon’s frame of reference, while the atmosphere it plunges through rests against the Earth, so L0 lives in the Earth frame. Find the events and find the object first; only then may γ be given its place.
attenborough.17: Key idea The proper quantity is named before any formula is uttered: t0 belongs to the frame where both events fall at the same point in space, and L0 belongs to the frame where the object lies at rest. Then time is stretched by t = γt0, and length is shrunk by L = L0/γ — each bent by the very same factor, γ ≥ 1.
attenborough.18: Here, in three real corners of nature, these predictions reveal themselves at last. We can watch them unfold, each one understood by asking the same two patient questions about which traveller carries the proper time, and which holds the proper length.
attenborough.19: High above us, where the air thins towards the edge of space, a cosmic ray strikes the upper atmosphere, and from that collision a muon is born — some 5.0 km above a lone detector waiting at sea level. It begins to fall, plunging earthward at 0.995c. At rest, a muon such as this would live, on average, a mere 2.2 μs. We follow it now, this fleeting traveller, as it races in a straight line at unwavering speed towards the only fate it knows: to decay.
attenborough.20: By the old reckoning, the journey should end almost as soon as it begins. In a single lifetime the muon would cross only 0.995 × 3.00 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹ × 2.2 × 10⁻⁶ s = 6.6 × 10² m — barely an eighth of the way down. Scarcely any should survive to reach the ground. And yet, against all expectation, the detectors far below record them in their multitudes, arriving in abundance. The secret lies in the speed itself: at 0.995c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.995²) = 10.0, and each frame tells the tale of survival in its own way.
attenborough.21: In the Earth frame: from where we stand, the muon’s birth and its death both happen upon the muon itself, and the muon is moving past us — so its 2.2 μs lifetime is the proper time. To our eyes, that brief life is stretched, dilated, the traveller granted more time than it knows: t = γt0 = 10.0 × 2.2 μs = 22 μs. In those 22 μs the little wanderer covers 2.985 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹ × 2.2 × 10⁻⁵ s = 6.6 km — further than the 5.0 km of sky between it and the ground. The muon arrives.
attenborough.22: In the muon’s frame: ride with the traveller and the world inverts. Now the muon is still, its 2.2 μs lifetime its own proper time, and it is the whole atmosphere that rushes upward to meet it at 0.995c. That atmosphere is at rest with the Earth, so 5.0 km is a proper length in the Earth frame; but to the muon the journey shrinks before it, contracted to L = L0/γ = 5.0 × 10³ ÷ 10.0 = 5.0 × 10² m, crossed in just 1.7 μs — less than a single lifetime. The muon arrives.
attenborough.23: Two tellings, two travellers, and they will never agree on the times or the distances. Yet on the one thing that matters they are as one: the muon arrives. What is stretched time to the watcher on the ground is shrunken space to the traveller falling through the sky, as shown in Figure 11.2.
attenborough.24: Elsewhere, a different traveller awaits. A linear accelerator, 3.2 km long, lies at rest in the laboratory, so the laboratory measures its proper length. But to the electrons that streak along it at 0.998c (γ = 15.8), the great beamline collapses inward, contracted to L = 3.2 × 10³ ÷ 15.8 = 2.0 × 10² m — and so the accelerating structures must be built for particles that race through a far shorter world than ours.
attenborough.25: And far overhead, a quieter drama plays out in orbit. A GPS receiver finds its place on Earth from the travel times of signals broadcast by satellites, so those distant clocks must keep faith with our ground clocks to an exquisite precision. Each satellite sweeps along at roughly 3.9 × 10³ m s⁻¹, giving γ = 1 + 8.5 × 10⁻¹¹. A tiny thing — yet over the course of a single day (86 400 s) the orbiting clock, ticking out its own proper time aboard the satellite, falls behind ours by some 7.3 μs. In 7.3 μs light travels 2.2 km, so left uncorrected the system would wander by kilometres with each passing day; and so the GPS clocks are set right for the speed of their flight.
attenborough.26: Connects These very satellites are the circling travellers of topic 2: the radius of the orbit decides the speed v, and v decides γ — so this relativistic correction is governed by the same orbital mechanics that lifted the satellite into the sky in the first place.
attenborough.27: And so we come to the final, most profound rule of this world. Even now, as our muon rests, it is not empty. Within its tiny mass lies a hidden store of energy, waiting, silent. We call it the rest energy:
attenborough.28: And the moment it moves, the moment it begins its descent through the sky, its total energy becomes something more: the rest energy it carried at birth, and the energy of its motion, together:
attenborough.29: At the gentle speeds of everyday life, (γ − 1)mc² matches the old, familiar ½mv² almost perfectly, and that is why the classical formula has served so well for so long. But as the traveller approaches c, the two part ways. Consider an electron racing at 0.99c (γ = 7.09): the old formula offers ½mv² = ½ × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × (2.97 × 10⁸)² = 4.0 × 10⁻¹⁴ J, yet the truth is Ek = (7.09 − 1) × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 9.00 × 10¹⁶ = 5.0 × 10⁻¹³ J — more than twelve times greater. And here lies the harshest law of all. As v → c, γ → ∞: the energy demanded climbs without end. No traveller with mass, however small, however determined, can ever reach the speed of light.
attenborough.30: Now lift your gaze from the lone muon to the great furnace that warms its sky. The Sun pours out 3.8 × 10²⁶ W from the fusion burning deep in its heart. There, hydrogen nuclei fuse into helium, and the offspring weigh a fraction less than their parents. That missing mass does not vanish — it becomes light. And we can ask how much mass is surrendered with each passing second.
attenborough.31: Each second, the Sun sends forth E = 3.8 × 10²⁶ J. And so E0 = mc² turns about, and gives us m = E0/c².
attenborough.33: And the answer: m = 4.2 × 10⁹ kg, given up every single second — some four million tonnes of the Sun itself, dissolved into the sunlight that falls upon our muon and upon us all. And yet, across the whole of its long life, this devours well under 0.1% of the Sun’s mass.
attenborough.34: Nowhere is the surrender of mass more complete than in positron–electron annihilation. The positron is the electron’s mirror twin, its antiparticle, carrying the same mass. When the two drift together at the gentlest of speeds, both are utterly undone, and every last trace of their rest energy departs as two gamma-ray photons: 2mec² = 2 × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 9.00 × 10¹⁶ = 1.64 × 10⁻¹³ J in all, which is 8.2 × 10⁻¹⁴ J ≈ 0.51 MeV in each. They flee in opposite directions, for the momentum before the meeting was very nearly nothing. And it is by hunting these back-to-back pairs that PET scanners trace the hidden tumours within us.
attenborough.35: In the great accelerators, this same exchange is run backwards: in collisions of enormous γ, the energy of motion is woven into the rest energy of new matter — particles born heavier than those that struck. These are nuclear transformations, governed in both directions alike by E0 = mc².
attenborough.36: Trap γmc² is the whole of the energy, not the energy of motion alone. The kinetic part is only what rises above the rest energy: Ek = (γ − 1)mc². And the old ½mv² will not do as the traveller nears c; at 0.99c it falls short of the true kinetic energy by a factor of about twelve.
attenborough.37: Key idea Mass and energy are one and the same, traded at a fixed and unchanging rate, E0 = mc². The Sun turns its mass into radiance, annihilation turns mass wholly into pairs of photons, and the accelerators turn the energy of motion into the mass of newborn particles.
attenborough.38: Connects The radius formula r = mv/qB, which served the synchrotron in topic 5, carries within it the quiet condition v << c. The electrons of the Australian Synchrotron defy that condition by design, for their energy is thousands of times their rest energy — and so the magnetic fields of the machine must be shaped for the relativistic energies of this page, not the classical formula.

greekepic.0: Sing, Muse, of the swift things that outrun the imaginings of mortals: for as speeds climb toward the speed of tireless light, the old laws of time, of length, and of energy speak falsely. This song proclaims the two decrees of Einstein, measures their consequences by the Lorentz factor, and ends where it must end — at the deathless equivalence of mass and energy: E0 = mc².
greekepic.1: The classical tale, told by the elders, holds that time flows at one same rate for every watcher under heaven, that lengths stand equal for all who measure, that velocities are joined together as gifts heaped upon gifts, and that the kinetic energy ½mv² may swell without bound, like the wine-dark sea. At the gentle pace of mortal walking these tellings agree with what eyes behold. But as speeds draw near to c = 3.00 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹, all four boasts are broken: an accelerator may pour energy upon an electron without ceasing, yet the electron, like a runner forever short of the finish, never attains the speed of tireless light.
greekepic.2: Light journeys at c, as was decreed in topic 6 — yet the old physics, blind in this, could not declare relative to what. By the classical joining of velocities, a watcher rushing toward the source of light should reckon a value greater than c itself. So the sages of the nineteenth age conjured the ether, that phantom never seen, a medium through whose body light is borne at c; and the Earth, wheeling upon her orbit through this phantom, ought to raise an “ether wind”, so that the measured speed of light should differ as the wind blew, from quarter to quarter of the sky.
greekepic.3: In the year 1887 the heroes Michelson and Morley clove a single beam of light along two crossed arms of their interferometer, and gathered the returning reflections again into a woven pattern of interference. Were the ether wind to blow, light coursing with the wind would spend a time unlike the light that crossed it, and the turning of the engine should drive the fringes aside by an omen foretold and measurable. Yet the shift they beheld was zero — at every turning, at every hour of day, in every season that the year brings round: the null result.
greekepic.4: This null result decrees that the measured speed of light stands the same in all directions, heedless of the Earth's own wandering, and grants no shred of witness to the ether, that phantom never seen. It upholds the great claim at the heart of special relativity — that c is one and the same for every watcher — which Einstein took up in 1905 and raised aloft as a decree of the cosmos.
greekepic.5: Key idea Michelson and Morley foretold that an ether wind would drive the fringes aside as their interferometer turned; yet under no condition of heaven did they behold any shifting; and the null result upholds the decree that the speed of tireless light is one and the same for every watcher, heedless of the motion of the source or of the watcher alike.
greekepic.6: Hear now of the frame of reference, that throne of coordinates from which a watcher measures all positions, all times, all the wanderings of moving things. Loftiest among these is the inertial frame of reference, which never quickens nor slows, but glides forever at constant velocity. Yet constant speed alone wins no such honour: behold the chariot that rounds a bend at steady pace, for it turns its course and so betrays its motion, and its frame is named non-inertial. To the inertial frames, and to these only, does the great theory of relativity extend its decree.
greekepic.7: Upon two postulates, as upon two unshaken pillars, the whole theory stands:
greekepic.8: Postulate 1. The laws of physics are the same in all inertial frames of reference, decreed alike to every wanderer. No experiment, however cunning, wrought within an inertial frame can ever reveal that frame's absolute motion through the void.
greekepic.9: Postulate 2. The speed of tireless light in the vacuum, c, keeps one selfsame value for all observers in inertial frames, heedless of the motion of the source that kindles it or of the eye that beholds it.
greekepic.10: This second decree is the sundering blow that severs the old world from the new. By the ancient reckoning, should a train that races at 0.50c kindle its headlamps, a watcher beside the track should measure that light at 1.50c; yet the cosmos decrees otherwise, that driver and trackside watcher each measure exactly c, no more and no less. Two observers borne apart in relative motion may agree upon the speed of one selfsame beam only if they fall to quarrelling over distances and over the intervals of time. So absolute time and absolute length, those old idols, are cast down and abandoned forever.
greekepic.11: Trap Mark well: “inertial” means constant velocity, never constant speed, and the swift are easily deceived. An electron circling the storage ring of the Australian Synchrotron at constant speed is nonetheless drawn ever inward toward the centre, forever accelerating, so its frame is non-inertial. The honoured formulas of time-dilation and length-contraction keep their faith with the inertial frames alone.
greekepic.12: Sing now of the event, that single happening fixed at one point of space and sealed in one moment of time — the birthing of a swift-perishing muon, or the lone tick of a clock that counts the doom of all things. Know this, O hearer: inertial observers borne apart in relative motion fall to quarreling, each against the other, over the span of time between two such events and over the very length of a thing. Yet every contest is anchored by one hallowed and privileged measure, the proper quantity, the truth each frame holds in its own keeping.
greekepic.13: Proper time t0 is the span of time between two events, measured in that frame of reference wherein both events befall at the selfsame point in space. Both beats of a wayfarer’s heart sound at the wayfarer’s own breast; therefore the wayfarer measures proper time, while every other watcher, looking on from afar, measures a longer span, a dilated time, stretched as a hero’s journey is stretched. And proper length L0 is the length of a thing measured in that frame wherein the thing lies at rest. Observers borne in motion against it measure a shorter span, a contracted length — yet contracted along the line of relative motion alone, and never crosswise.
greekepic.14: Every labour of relativity opens with two questions, asked aloud before any formula is summoned to the field: in which frame do the two events befall at the selfsame point in space (for that observer measures t0), and in which frame does the thing, or the very road to be travelled, lie at rest (for that observer measures L0)? The measure of the stretching and the shrinking is decreed by the Lorentz factor:
greekepic.15: Since v < c for any thing endowed with mass, never may γ sink below 1: γ = 1 for the thing at rest, and at 0.10c it is still but 1.005, so the old craft of classical mechanics suffices for the slow speeds of mortals. At 0.50c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.25) = 1.15; at 0.90c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.81) = 2.29; at 0.99c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.9801) = 7.09. As v → c the denominator dwindles toward nothing and γ climbs without bound, soaring as no mortal thing has soared, as is shown in Figure 11.1.
greekepic.16: Trap Hear and err not: the measurements of both observers stand true, each within its own frame; a contracted length and a dilated time are true measures, no phantoms nor illusions of the eye. Proper time does not belong to the watcher standing still by any birthright, and proper time and proper length most often dwell with different observers in the selfsame riddle. For a muon’s creation and its decay both befall at the muon, so t0 is measured in the muon’s frame of reference; while the air through which it plummets lies at rest against Earth, so L0 is measured in the Earth frame. First the events and the thing must be set in their places; only then is γ assigned its due.
greekepic.17: Key idea The proper quantity is named before any formula is called to the field: t0 belongs to the frame wherein both events befall at the selfsame point in space, and L0 belongs to the frame wherein the thing lies at rest. Then, by decree of the cosmos, t = γt0 stretches time and L = L0/γ shrinks length, both by the one same factor γ ≥ 1.
greekepic.18: Sing now of three true engines of the world, each one a witness that proclaims these decrees aloud; and each shall be questioned, as ever, by the two questions of the proper quantities set down above.
greekepic.19: Behold the cosmic rays, hurled out of the deep, that smite the high roof of the air and there beget the swift-perishing muon, born some 5.0 km above the sea-level detector that waits below, and plunging earthward like a doomed spear at 0.995c. A muon at rest is granted an average lifetime of but 2.2 μs. Picture each one as a runner upon a straight road, holding constant his pace until death takes him.
greekepic.20: By the old reckoning of the ancients, the swift-perishing muon runs 0.995 × 3.00 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹ × 2.2 × 10⁻⁶ s = 6.6 × 10² m within one lifetime, scarcely an eighth of the road, so that almost none, it seems, should ever reach the detector and live to be counted. Yet the detectors at the sea’s level record the muons in their multitudes. For at 0.995c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.995²) = 10.0, and each frame, like a separate bard, unravels the marvel in its own true measure.
greekepic.21: In the Earth frame: the muon’s birth and its dying both befall at the muon itself, that hero borne along against the Earth, so the 2.2 μs lifetime is the proper time. Stretched wide, the lifetime grows to t = γt0 = 10.0 × 2.2 μs = 22 μs. And in those 22 μs the muon journeys 2.985 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹ × 2.2 × 10⁻⁵ s = 6.6 km, surpassing the 5.0 km of the breathing air. The muon arrives.
greekepic.22: In the muon’s frame: the muon stands at rest, lord of his own reckoning, his 2.2 μs lifetime is the proper time, and the atmosphere itself, that great river of air, rushes upward past him at 0.995c. The air lies at rest against the Earth, so its 5.0 km is a proper length in the Earth frame; but in the muon’s frame it is squeezed thin to L = L0/γ = 5.0 × 10³ ÷ 10.0 = 5.0 × 10² m, a span crossed in but 1.7 μs, which is less than one lifetime. The muon arrives.
greekepic.23: The two frames quarrel, as bards will, over times and over distances, yet they sing as one upon the deed that all eyes behold: the muon arrives. The time-stretching of the one frame answers, measure for measure, the length-squeezing of the other, as is shown in Figure 11.2.
greekepic.24: A linear accelerator, 3.2 km in its length, lies at rest within the hall of the laboratory, so the laboratory measures its proper length. But the electrons coursing along it at 0.998c (γ = 15.8) behold the beamline drawn in close to L = 3.2 × 10³ ÷ 15.8 = 2.0 × 10² m, and the accelerating structures must be forged with cunning to serve particles that suffer this shortened road.
greekepic.25: A GPS receiver divines its place upon the Earth from the travel times of timing signals, sent down like heralds from the satellites on high, so the satellite clocks must keep faith with the ground clocks to a precision exceeding fine. Each satellite circles at near 3.9 × 10³ m s⁻¹, whence γ = 1 + 8.5 × 10⁻¹¹. A small thing this; yet across one whole day (86 400 s) the orbiting clock, which keeps proper time aloft aboard the satellite, falls behind the ground clocks by near 7.3 μs. The tireless light travels 2.2 km in 7.3 μs, so a system left uncorrected would wander by whole kilometres with each day that passes; and therefore the GPS clocks are set true against their orbital speed.
greekepic.26: Connects These satellites here set right are the selfsame circular orbits sung of in topic 2: the orbital radius decrees the speed v, and v decrees γ, so that the relativistic correction is ordained by the very orbital mechanics that first set the satellite wheeling in the heavens.
greekepic.27: Sing now, Muse, of the last and grandest gift of relativity, which is energy. For know this decree of the cosmos: no body at rest is a hollow vessel, empty and barren. Its very mass is a hoard of energy, brimming and waiting, the rest energy that sleeps within all things:
greekepic.28: And when at last the body rouses itself and moves abroad, its total energy is the joining of two streams together, the rest energy and the kinetic energy as one:
greekepic.29: At the slow and earthbound speeds of mortals, (γ − 1)mc² walks hand in hand with old ½mv², and thus the ancient formula triumphs in the sluggish daylight world. But draw near to c, that tireless light, and the two paths sunder and flee apart. Behold an electron racing at 0.99c (γ = 7.09): the old reckoning yields ½mv² = ½ × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × (2.97 × 10⁸)² = 4.0 × 10⁻¹⁴ J, yet the true measure stands forth as Ek = (7.09 − 1) × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 9.00 × 10¹⁶ = 5.0 × 10⁻¹³ J, greater by more than twelvefold. And as v → c, so γ → ∞: the kinetic energy demanded swells without end or limit, and therefore no body of mass, however heroic, shall ever lay hold of the speed of light.
greekepic.30: Lo, the Sun, that ever-burning lord of the sky, pours forth 3.8 × 10²⁶ W from the fusion-fire kindled in his deep heart. There hydrogen nuclei are wedded into helium, and the children born of that union bear slightly less mass than their parents bore. This vanished mass is reckoned as transmuted into radiance, and the mass surrendered with each passing second is counted thus.
greekepic.31: Formula: with each second the Sun casts abroad E = 3.8 × 10²⁶ J; and so E0 = mc² is turned about to yield m = E0/c².
greekepic.33: Answer: m = 4.2 × 10⁹ kg with every single second, four million tonnes of being unmade and sent forth as sunlight, breath upon breath. Yet so vast is the great star that this devours well under 0.1% of the Sun’s mass across the long ages of his life.
greekepic.34: But where the conversion is whole and entire, untempered and complete, there reigns positron–electron annihilation. The positron is the shadow-twin of the electron, its antiparticle, equal to it in mass. When these two doomed kin meet at last, drifting near to stillness, both are utterly destroyed, and the whole of their rest energy departs as two gamma-ray photons: 2mec² = 2 × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 9.00 × 10¹⁶ = 1.64 × 10⁻¹³ J in all, which is 8.2 × 10⁻¹⁴ J ≈ 0.51 MeV to each. The photons flee in opposite ways, for the momentum before the meeting was as nothing, approximately zero. By such back-to-back pairs do the PET healers of medicine hunt out the hidden tumour.
greekepic.35: And the great accelerators, those forges of the bold, run this decree backward: in collisions at mighty γ they turn kinetic energy into the rest energy of new-made matter, begetting particles heavier than those that clashed together. These are the transmutations of the nucleus, ruled in both their directions by the sovereign law E0 = mc².
greekepic.36: Trap Heed this warning, lest you stumble: γmc² is the total energy, and not the kinetic energy alone. The kinetic energy is only that portion which rises above the rest energy: Ek = (γ − 1)mc². The old ½mv² is no fit companion near to c, that tireless light; at 0.99c it falls short of the true kinetic energy by some twelvefold.
greekepic.37: Key idea Mass and energy are kindred shapes of one substance, exchanged at the unbending rate E0 = mc². The Sun turns mass to radiance; annihilation turns mass wholly into photon pairs; and the accelerators turn kinetic energy into the very mass of new-born particles.
greekepic.38: Connects The radius formula r = mv/qB, wielded for the synchrotron in topic 5, bears the binding condition v << c. But the electrons of the Australian Synchrotron, by deliberate design, scorn that condition, for their energy is thousands of times their rest energy; and so the magnetic fields of that machine must be forged for the relativistic energies sung upon this page, and not for the classical formula of old.

cnpop.0: 注意看，当速度开始疯狂往光速冲的时候，那套从小学到大的经典规则——时间、长度、能量——全部当场失灵，算出来的结果离谱到亲妈都不认。这一节我们先甩出爱因斯坦的两条公设，再用洛伦兹因子把它们的后果一个个量化出来，最后引爆全场的终极大招：质量和能量根本就是一回事——E0 = mc²。
cnpop.1: 经典力学的世界观属实是很朴素：它默认时间对每个人流逝得一样快，长度对谁量都一个数，速度可以直接加加加，动能 ½mv² 想涨多高涨多高、没有天花板。在咱们日常这种龟速下，这四条假设跟实验对得严丝合缝，岁月静好。可一旦速度逼近 c = 3.00 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹，好家伙——四条全崩：加速器可以往一个电子身上无限灌能量，结果它的速度死活就是摸不到 c。
cnpop.2: 光以 c 飞奔，这在第6节就已经板上钉钉了，可经典物理一直答不上来一个灵魂问题：相对于啥跑这么快？按经典的速度叠加，一个迎着光源冲过去的观察者，量到的光速应该比 c 还大。于是十九世纪的物理学家脑补了一个东西——以太，一种看不见摸不着、专门让光以 c 传播的神秘介质。地球绕太阳公转，等于在以太里逆风狂奔，理论上会刮起一阵“以太风”，把不同方向上量到的光速吹出差异来。
cnpop.3: 1887年，迈克耳孙和莫雷直接开整：把一束光劈成两半，分别送进两条互相垂直的干涉仪臂里，再让反射回来的光合体，打出干涉条纹。逻辑很硬核——要是真有以太风，顺风跑的光和横风跑的光所花时间就不一样，那么把整套装置一转，干涉条纹就该挪动一个能算出来、也能量出来的量。结果呢？不管你转到哪个角度、不管白天黑夜、不管春夏秋冬，条纹纹丝不动——这就是物理史上著名的零结果。
cnpop.4: 这个零结果一锤定音：测出来的光速在所有方向上都一个样，跟地球怎么运动毫无关系，以太？连根毛都没找到。它反手就为狭义相对论的核心主张撑了腰——c 对所有观察者都是同一个值。爱因斯坦在1905年直接把它拿来当公设，开干。
cnpop.5: Key idea 迈克耳孙和莫雷的剧本是：以太风会让旋转的干涉仪出现条纹移动；可他们反复横跳各种条件，啥移动都没观察到。这个零结果稳稳支持了那条公设——光速对所有观察者都一个样，跟光源动不动、观察者动不动，统统没关系。
cnpop.6: 先搞清楚一个词——参考系。说白了，它就是你用来量位置、时间和运动的那套坐标。而惯性参考系，关键在于：它不加速，只匀速直线地走。注意看，光是速率不变还不够：一辆车以恒定速率拐弯，方向变了，它这就成了非惯性系。狭义相对论只在惯性系里玩，别的不认。
cnpop.7: 整套理论，就压在两条公设上：
cnpop.8: 公设一。物理定律在所有惯性参考系里都一模一样。你在任何一个惯性系里做实验，都没法测出这个参考系本身到底在不在动——绝对运动这玩意儿，根本测不出来。
cnpop.9: 公设二。真空中的光速 c，对所有惯性系里的观察者都是同一个值，不管光源在不在动、不管你这个观察者在不在动，它就是不变。
cnpop.10: 第二条，才是跟经典物理彻底决裂的地方。按经典那套想：一列火车以 0.50c 飞驰，突然打开车头大灯，路边站着的人测出来的光速，照理该是 1.50c 吧？好家伙，公设直接说——不，司机和路边的人，各自量出来都恰好是 c。这就离谱了：两个相对运动的人，要想对同一束光的速度达成一致，那他们就必须在距离和时间间隔上吵起来、谈不拢。所以反转来了——绝对时间和绝对长度，到此为止，全得扔。
cnpop.11: Trap 划重点别踩坑：“惯性”指的是速度矢量不变，可不是速率不变。一个电子在澳大利亚同步加速器储存环里以恒定速率绕圈，它可是一直朝圆心方向加速的——所以它的参考系属实是非惯性的。那套标准的钟慢和尺缩公式，只对惯性系成立，记牢了。
cnpop.12: 什么叫一个事件？说白了，就是在某个地点、某个时刻发生的一件事——比如一颗μ子诞生那一瞬间，或者一只钟"嘀嗒"一下。好家伙，关键来了：两个相对运动的惯性参考系里的观察者，会在"这两件事隔了多久"和"这个东西有多长"上吵起来，谁也说服不了谁。怎么办？每一组比较都得先找到一个"基准"，一个被特殊对待的测量值——这就是所谓的固有量。
cnpop.13: 注意看——固有时间 t0 指的是：两件事在同一个地点发生时，所在参考系测到的那段时间间隔。举个例子，一个旅行者的两次心跳都发生在他自己的胸口，所以他测的就是固有时间；而其他所有观察者测到的时间都更长，也就是被"拉长"了——这就是时间膨胀（钟慢）。同理，固有长度 L0 是物体在它自己静止的那个参考系里测到的长度。相对物体运动的人测出来的长度更短，被"压扁"了——也就是长度收缩（尺缩），而且只在相对运动的方向上缩，别的方向不变。
cnpop.14: 每道相对论题目开场都得先灵魂两问，公式一个都还没动之前就要问清楚：在哪个参考系里，这两件事发生在同一个地点（那个观察者测的就是 t0）？又在哪个参考系里，物体——或者要走过的那段距离——是静止的（那个观察者测的就是 L0）？至于到底膨胀多少、收缩多少，全由这个硬核角色——洛伦兹因子说了算：
cnpop.15: 因为任何有质量的物体都满足 v < c，所以 γ 永远不会小于 1：静止时 γ = 1，就算飙到 0.10c，它也才 1.005，离谱地接近 1，所以日常速度下用经典力学完全够用、根本看不出差别。可一旦提速：0.50c 时，γ = 1/√(1 − 0.25) = 1.15；0.90c 时，γ = 1/√(1 − 0.81) = 2.29；到了 0.99c，γ = 1/√(1 − 0.9801) = 7.09，直接起飞。当 v → c，分母无限趋近于零，γ 就一路狂飙、没有上限，如图 11.1 所示。
cnpop.16: Trap 划重点：两个观察者在各自参考系里测出来的结果都是对的——收缩的长度、膨胀的时间都是货真价实的测量值，不是什么错觉、更不是"看花眼"。还有个坑：固有时间默认就属于静止那位？不存在的。而且同一道题里，固有时间和固有长度通常分别属于两个不同的观察者：一颗μ子的诞生和衰变都发生在μ子身上，所以 t0 在μ子参考系里测；而它穿过的大气层相对地球静止，所以 L0 在地球参考系里测。先把事件和物体各自定位清楚，然后才轮到 γ 登场。
cnpop.17: Key idea 套公式之前先认准固有量：t0 属于"两件事发生在同一地点"的那个参考系，L0 属于"物体静止"的那个参考系。认准之后，t = γt0 把时间拉长、L = L0/γ 把长度压短，缩放的就是同一个 γ ≥ 1。一个膨胀、一个收缩，反转就反转在这——同一个因子，两副面孔。
cnpop.18: 光说理论太空泛？来，三个真实存在的系统直接把这些预言摆上台面——每一个，我们都用上面那两个老问题来拆解：固有时间在谁手里，固有长度又在谁手里。
cnpop.19: 注意看：宇宙射线一头撞进高层大气，在离海平面探测器大约 5.0 km 高的地方，造出一堆μ子，以 0.995c 的速度往下俯冲。问题是，静止的μ子平均寿命只有 2.2 μs。我们把每个μ子简化成沿直线匀速飞行，飞到衰变为止。

cnpop.21: 站在地球参考系：μ子的诞生和衰变都发生在μ子身上，而它相对地球在飞，所以那 2.2 μs 寿命就是固有时间。膨胀后的寿命是 t = γt0 = 10.0 × 2.2 μs = 22 μs。这 22 μs 里，μ子能跑 2.985 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹ × 2.2 × 10⁻⁵ s = 6.6 km，直接超过那 5.0 km 厚的大气层。μ子，到了。
cnpop.22: 切到μ子自己的参考系：这回μ子躺平不动，它那 2.2 μs 寿命就是固有时间，反倒是大气层以 0.995c 的速度从下往上呼啸而过。大气层相对地球是静止的，所以 5.0 km 是地球参考系里的固有长度；到了μ子眼里直接尺缩成 L = L0/γ = 5.0 × 10³ ÷ 10.0 = 5.0 × 10² m，1.7 μs 就穿过去了，连一个寿命都没用满。μ子，照样到了。
cnpop.23: 两个参考系在时间和距离上吵得不可开交，但在那个能看见摸得着的事实上完全一致：μ子到了。这边的钟慢，正好对应那边的尺缩——一体两面，如图 11.2 所示。属实是同一件事的两种讲法。
cnpop.24: 再上硬核的：一台 3.2 km 长的直线加速器静静躺在实验室里，所以实验室量到的是它的固有长度。可电子以 0.998c（γ = 15.8）一路狂飙，在它眼里这条束流线收缩成了 L = 3.2 × 10³ ÷ 15.8 = 2.0 × 10² m——所以那些加速结构，必须按照粒子实际体验到的这个更短的长度来设计。
cnpop.25: 再来个天天揣兜里的：GPS接收机靠卫星广播的授时信号的传播时间来算位置，这就要求卫星上的钟和地面的钟精度高到丧心病狂地一致。每颗卫星大约以 3.9 × 10³ m s⁻¹ 绕地球转，对应 γ = 1 + 8.5 × 10⁻¹¹。数字看着小得可怜，可一天下来（86 400 s），记录着卫星上固有时间的那只钟，会比地面钟慢大约 7.3 μs。光在 7.3 μs 里能跑 2.2 km——所以一个不做修正的系统每天会漂出好几公里，直接给你定位定到隔壁城市。GPS的钟，全都为它们的轨道速度做了修正。

cnpop.28: 等它动起来，总能量就更猛了：是静能加上动能，两份打包一起算：

cnpop.31: 公式：太阳每秒辐射 E = 3.8 × 10²⁶ J，把 E0 = mc² 反手一变形，就得到 m = E0/c²。

cnpop.37: Key idea 质量和能量可以互相换，汇率是固定死的 E0 = mc²。太阳把质量换成辐射，湮灭把质量整个换成光子对，加速器把动能换成新粒子的质量——硬核三连。

ai.0: Push something fast enough — really fast, close to the speed of light — and the familiar rules you trust for time, length and energy quietly start handing you wrong answers. In this topic you’ll meet Einstein’s two postulates, see exactly how much they bend reality through the Lorentz factor, and arrive at one of the most famous ideas in all of physics: that mass and energy are two faces of the same thing, E0 = mc².
ai.1: Classical mechanics makes four comfortable assumptions: that time ticks at the same rate for everyone, that a metre is a metre for every observer, that velocities simply add up, and that the kinetic energy ½mv² can climb forever. At the speeds you live at, these hold up beautifully — every experiment agrees. But as things race toward c = 3.00 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹, all four quietly break down. Picture an accelerator pouring energy into an electron without pause: you can keep feeding it forever, yet its speed creeps closer and closer to c without ever quite getting there.
ai.2: Light travels at c, as you saw back in topic 12 — but here’s the puzzle classical physics couldn’t crack: relative to what? Plain old velocity addition says that if you rush toward a light source, you ought to clock the light coming in faster than c. So nineteenth-century physicists invented the ether, an invisible medium filling all of space through which light supposedly glides at c. If it existed, Earth ploughing through it on its orbit should stir up an “ether wind” — and that wind should make light’s measured speed depend on which way you looked.
ai.3: In 1887 Michelson and Morley devised an exquisitely sensitive test. They split a single light beam down two perpendicular arms of an interferometer, bounced the halves back, and let them recombine into an interference pattern. If an ether wind were real, light running with the wind would take a different time than light cutting across it — so spinning the apparatus around should nudge the fringes by a definite, measurable amount. They looked, and looked again: the shift was zero at every orientation, every hour of the day, every season of the year. This is the famous null result.
ai.4: That stubborn nothing is actually a profound something. It tells us the measured speed of light comes out the same in every direction, no matter how Earth is moving — and it offers not a shred of evidence for the ether. Instead it backs the bold central claim of special relativity: that c is the same for every observer. Einstein seized on exactly this in 1905 and built it in as a postulate.
ai.5: Key idea Michelson and Morley reasoned that an ether wind would shift the fringes as their interferometer turned. They saw no shift, under any conditions whatsoever. That null result props up the postulate that the speed of light is the same for every observer — utterly indifferent to how the source or the observer happens to be moving.
ai.6: A frame of reference is simply the set of coordinates you measure positions, times and motions from — your own personal grid for pinning down where and when. An inertial frame of reference is one that doesn’t accelerate; it cruises along at constant velocity. And notice: constant speed alone isn’t enough. A car taking a bend at a steady speedometer reading is still changing direction, so its frame is non-inertial. Special relativity, in this course, plays strictly inside inertial frames.
ai.7: The whole theory balances on just two postulates:
ai.8: Postulate 1. The laws of physics look exactly the same in every inertial frame of reference. No experiment you could ever run inside an inertial frame can reveal that frame’s absolute motion — there’s simply no “truly at rest” to find.
ai.9: Postulate 2. The speed of light in a vacuum, c, comes out the same for every observer in an inertial frame — no matter how the light source is moving, and no matter how the observer is moving.
ai.10: The second postulate is where physics breaks cleanly with the past. Classically, if a train rushing along at 0.50c flicks on its headlights, someone standing by the tracks ought to clock that light at 1.50c. The postulate flatly disagrees: the driver and the trackside observer each measure exactly c. And here’s the catch — two people moving relative to each other can only agree on the speed of the very same light beam if they disagree about distances and time intervals. So absolute time and absolute length have to go.
ai.11: Trap “Inertial” means constant velocity, not constant speed. An electron whipping around the Australian Synchrotron’s storage ring holds a constant speed yet is forever accelerating toward the centre, so its frame is non-inertial. The standard time-dilation and length-contraction formulas only apply in inertial frames — keep that firmly in mind.
ai.12: An event is just something that happens at one point in space at one moment in time — a muon springing into existence, say, or a single tick of a clock. Inertial observers moving relative to one another will flatly disagree about the time between two events and about how long an object is. To make sense of those disagreements, each one is anchored to a special, privileged measurement called the proper quantity.
ai.13: Proper time t0 is the time between two events as measured in the frame where both events happen at the same point in space. Think of a traveller’s heartbeat: both beats happen right there at the traveller’s chest, so the traveller is the one reading proper time — while every other observer clocks a longer, dilated time. Proper length L0 is the length of an object measured in the frame where the object sits at rest. Anyone moving past it measures a shorter, contracted length — and the shrinking happens only along the direction of relative motion.
ai.14: Every relativity problem opens with the same two questions, asked before you reach for a single formula: in which frame do the two events happen at the same point in space (that observer reads t0), and in which frame is the object — or the distance being crossed — at rest (that observer reads L0)? Just how much time stretches and length shrinks is governed by the Lorentz factor:
ai.15: Because v < c for anything with mass, γ never dips below 1: it’s γ = 1 at rest, and even at 0.10c it’s a barely-there 1.005 — which is exactly why classical mechanics serves you so well at everyday speeds. But watch it climb: at 0.50c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.25) = 1.15; at 0.90c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.81) = 2.29; at 0.99c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.9801) = 7.09. As v → c the denominator slides toward zero and γ shoots up without bound, as you can see in Figure 11.1.
ai.16: Trap Both observers are genuinely right within their own frames — a contracted length and a dilated time are real, recorded measurements, not tricks of perception. Proper time doesn’t automatically belong to whoever is “standing still,” and in a single problem proper time and proper length usually belong to different observers: a muon’s birth and death both happen right at the muon, so t0 lives in the muon’s frame of reference, while the atmosphere it falls through sits at rest relative to Earth, so L0 lives in the Earth frame. Locate the events and the object first; only then do you hand out γ.
ai.17: Key idea Pin down the proper quantity before you touch any formula: t0 belongs to the frame where both events happen at the same point in space, and L0 belongs to the frame where the object is at rest. Then t = γt0 stretches time and L = L0/γ shrinks length — both by the very same factor γ ≥ 1.
ai.18: Three real-world systems show these predictions actually playing out, not as thought experiments but as things we measure — and you’ll crack each one open with the same two proper-quantity questions from above.
ai.19: Cosmic rays slamming into the upper atmosphere create muons roughly 5.0 km above a sea-level detector, streaking downward at 0.995c. Sitting still, a muon survives on average just 2.2 μs before it decays. We’ll picture each muon as flying in a straight line at constant speed right up until the moment it decays.
ai.20: Do the classical sum and the muon looks doomed: it covers 0.995 × 3.00 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹ × 2.2 × 10⁻⁶ s = 6.6 × 10² m in one lifetime — only about an eighth of the way down — so almost none should ever reach the detector. And yet detectors at sea level catch muons by the bucketload. What gives? At 0.995c, γ = 1/√(1 − 0.995²) = 10.0, and each frame tells its own story to reach the same happy ending.
ai.21: In the Earth frame: the muon’s birth and death both happen right at the muon, which is racing past Earth, so that 2.2 μs lifetime is the proper time. Stretched out, the lifetime becomes t = γt0 = 10.0 × 2.2 μs = 22 μs. In those 22 μs the muon travels 2.985 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹ × 2.2 × 10⁻⁵ s = 6.6 km — comfortably more than the 5.0 km of atmosphere standing in its way. The muon makes it.
ai.22: In the muon’s frame: now the muon sits still, its 2.2 μs lifetime is the proper time, and it’s the atmosphere that comes rushing upward past it at 0.995c. Since the atmosphere is at rest relative to Earth, that 5.0 km is a proper length in the Earth frame; from the muon’s point of view it’s squeezed down to L = L0/γ = 5.0 × 10³ ÷ 10.0 = 5.0 × 10² m, a gap it clears in just 1.7 μs — less than a single lifetime. The muon makes it.
ai.23: The two frames bicker about times and distances, yet they shake hands on the one thing you can actually check: the muon arrives. What looks like time dilation from one frame shows up as length contraction in the other — two descriptions of a single reality, as shown in Figure 11.2.
ai.24: A linear accelerator 3.2 km long sits at rest in the laboratory, so the lab is the one measuring its proper length. Electrons hurtling down it at 0.998c (γ = 15.8) see the beamline squashed to L = 3.2 × 10³ ÷ 15.8 = 2.0 × 10² m — and the accelerating structures have to be engineered for particles that genuinely experience this shorter length.
ai.25: A GPS receiver works out where you are from how long timing signals take to arrive from orbiting satellites — which means those satellite clocks have to agree with ground clocks to a breathtaking precision. Each satellite circles at roughly 3.9 × 10³ m s⁻¹, giving γ = 1 + 8.5 × 10⁻¹¹. Tiny, sure — but over a single day (86 400 s) the orbiting clock, which keeps proper time up there aboard the satellite, slips behind ground clocks by about 7.3 μs. In 7.3 μs light travels 2.2 km, so an uncorrected system would wander off by kilometres every day. That’s why GPS clocks are deliberately corrected for their orbital speed.
ai.26: Connects The satellites being corrected here are the very same circular orbits from topic 5: the orbital radius locks in the speed v, and v locks in γ — so the relativistic correction is dictated by exactly the same orbital mechanics that lofted the satellite into the sky in the first place.
ai.27: Relativity saves its grand finale for energy. A motionless object is far from energetically empty — its very mass is a hidden vault of energy, its rest energy:
ai.28: Set the object moving, and its total energy is simply the rest energy plus the kinetic energy it has picked up:
ai.29: At gentle speeds (γ − 1)mc² lines up neatly with ½mv² — which is exactly why the old classical formula works so well in everyday life. But push toward c and the two part ways dramatically. Take an electron at 0.99c (γ = 7.09): the classical formula gives ½mv² = ½ × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × (2.97 × 10⁸)² = 4.0 × 10⁻¹⁴ J, while the real value is Ek = (7.09 − 1) × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 9.00 × 10¹⁶ = 5.0 × 10⁻¹³ J — more than twelve times bigger. As v → c, γ → ∞: the kinetic energy you’d need balloons without limit, which is nature’s ironclad way of saying no massive object can ever reach the speed of light.
ai.30: The Sun pours out 3.8 × 10²⁶ W from nuclear fusion deep in its core. Hydrogen nuclei fuse into helium, and the helium that comes out carries a touch less mass than the hydrogen that went in. We treat that radiated energy as that missing mass converted into light — and here’s how to work out exactly how much mass vanishes each second.
ai.31: Formula: every second the Sun radiates E = 3.8 × 10²⁶ J, so E0 = mc² rearranges to m = E0/c².
ai.33: Answer: m = 4.2 × 10⁹ kg every second — that’s about four million tonnes of mass streaming away as sunlight, second after second after second. Astonishingly, even at that rate the Sun burns through well under 0.1% of its mass across its entire lifetime.
ai.34: Mass-to-energy conversion goes all the way — total, complete — in positron–electron annihilation. The positron is the electron’s antiparticle, carrying exactly the same mass. When the two meet at a crawl, both vanish entirely and their whole rest energy bursts out as two gamma-ray photons: 2mec² = 2 × 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 9.00 × 10¹⁶ = 1.64 × 10⁻¹³ J in total, which works out to 8.2 × 10⁻¹⁴ J ≈ 0.51 MeV per photon. The two photons fly off in exactly opposite directions, because the total momentum before annihilation was essentially zero. PET medical scanners hunt down tumours by catching these back-to-back pairs.
ai.35: Particle accelerators run the whole thing backwards: smash particles together at huge γ and their kinetic energy gets forged into the rest energy of brand-new matter — conjuring particles heavier than the ones that collided. These are genuine nuclear transformations, governed by E0 = mc² running in both directions.
ai.36: Trap γmc² is the total energy, not the kinetic energy. The kinetic energy is only the part stacked above the rest energy: Ek = (γ − 1)mc². And don’t reach for the classical ½mv² near c — it just won’t do: at 0.99c it undercounts the kinetic energy by a factor of around twelve.
ai.37: Key idea Mass and energy are interchangeable, traded at the one fixed rate E0 = mc². The Sun turns mass into radiation, annihilation turns mass entirely into photon pairs, and accelerators turn kinetic energy into the mass of new particles — the same currency, flowing every which way.
ai.38: Connects The radius formula r = mv/qB you used for the synchrotron in topic 8 comes with the fine print v << c. The electrons of the Australian Synchrotron break that condition on purpose — their energy runs thousands of times their rest energy — so the machine’s magnetic fields must be engineered for the relativistic energies on this page, not the classical formula.
